question: A 30-year-old female presents with a rash on her cheeks and hands. On exam, there is an erythematous, confluent, macular eruption on the face and papules on the dorsum of the hands. She has a past medical history of multiple first-trimester losses. She is taking NSAIDs for migraines. Which of the following labs will most likely be positive?
explanation:
This patient's presentation is consistent with lupus, a connective tissue disorder. Variants include skin-limited forms of lupus, including discoid lupus, subacute cutaneous lupus erythematosus, and acute cutaneous lupus erythematosus. When lupus involves additional organ systems and meets specific diagnostic criteria, it is termed systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE). This patient has acute lupus erythematosus, given the classic malar rash with sparing of the nasolabial folds. There is no scarring associated with this rash. This subtype of cutaneous lupus is more likely to progress to systemic lupus erythematosus than other types. Patients with discoid lupus have scarred, raised, or atrophic, scaly, red plaques that appear on the face, ears, and upper extremities. The lesions are often hyperpigmented at the periphery and depigmented in the center. Patients in subacute cutaneous lupus erythematosus have a photo-distributed eruption of annular, scaly, pink plaques without scarring. The diagnosis is based on a clinical picture and biopsy. Laboratory testing is usually positive for anti-nuclear antibodies. In SLE, anti-dsDNA and anti-Smith are often positive. Mainstay treatment options include photoprotection to prevent flares of lupus, topical corticosteroids for localized disease, and antimalarials for treatment of more generalized disease or systemic lupus erythematosus. Other systemic options include methotrexate, cyclosporine, oral steroids, and cyclophosphamide. Patients with involvement of other organ systems should also follow up closely with rheumatology

Incorrect Answers:
B. Anti-centromere antibodies are found in limited scleroderma
C. Anti-topoisomerase antibodies are found in systemic sclerosis.
D. Anti-histone antibodies are found in drug-induced lupus.

question: An elderly man presents with tense bullae on his lower extremities and trunk that are extremely pruritic. He denies exposure to chemicals or plants. He recently began hydrochlorothiazide for hypertension. Which of the following is true of this condition?

choices.A: This patient will likely develop significant oral involvement in the next few months.
choices.B: This condition is more common in middle-aged individuals rather than in elderly individuals.

explanation:
Patients develop pruritic, urticarial-like plaques or tense bullae on their trunk and extremities, which resolve without scarring. Bullae can be localized or generalized. Mucous membranes are not typically involved, unlike in pemphigus vulgaris, which presents with flaccid bullae and frequent mucosal involvement. This condition is typically self-limited. Bullous pemphigoid results from autoantibodies binding to the antigens that make up the hemidesmosome complex in the basement membrane. It is not clear exactly what causes this disorder, but medications like hydrochlorothiazide, furosemide, penicillamine, captopril, and antibiotics (among others) have been known to trigger it. The diagnosis is based on clinical findings, biopsy, and direct immunofluorescence. Additionally, blood tests can help confirm the diagnosis if direct immunofluorescence is falsely negative. Management includes stopping the offending medication if identified and topical steroids for localized disease. For more extensive disease, a course of oral prednisone with a slow taper or doxycycline has been effective. Other therapies that have been tried include azathioprine, cyclophosphamide, and mycophenolate mofetil, although these are not usually first-line medications

Incorrect Answers:
D. Antibodies against desmosome proteins are found in pemphigus vulgaris.

question: A 30-year-old man presents with well-defined pink plaques with silvery scales on his knees and elbows. He has associated pruritus and severe axial joint pain with no improvement with nonsteroidal anti- inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) or methotrexate. When he picks at these lesions, it causes pinpoint bleeding. What is the mechanism of action of the most appropriate next step in therapy?
explanation:
Psoriasis is a chronic inflammatory skin condition that causes well-circumscribed, pink plaques with silvery scales that often appear on the extensor surfaces, umbilicus, and scalp. The nails can also be involved. Psoriatic arthritis is an inflammatory condition that affects 10-30% of patients and typically develops after the cutaneous eruption. Psoriatic arthritis can cause severe pain, functional impairment, and joint destruction. It commonly leads to asymmetric oligoarthritis, although variations in presentation are possible. Providers should screen patients with psoriasis for joint symptoms such as pain, morning stiffness for over 1 hour, and tenderness at sites of tendon insertions into bone, which may indicate psoriatic arthritis. Physical exam findings may show tender, painful, edematous joints, dactylitis, and enthesitis. Since psoriatic arthritis can progress, it is important to refer patients with findings suggestive of psoriatic arthritis to a rheumatologist as soon as possible for treatment. Lab testing (including HLA-B27, rheumatoid factor, and anti-CCP) can help differentiate psoriatic arthritis from other inflammatory conditions. X-rays of the hands, feet, and sacroiliac joints are often obtained to aid diagnosis. Treatment options include NSAIDs and nonbiologic and biologic disease-modifying antirheumatic drugs (DMARDs). Non- biologic DMARDs are not efficacious in patients with axial disease. Instead, TNF and IL-17 inhibitors are reasonable options. Patients with psoriasis and psoriatic arthritis should follow up closely with dermatology and rheumatology

Incorrect Answers:
B. Cyclooxygenase (COX) inhibitors are in the NSAID family. They are used in mild disease. This
C. Calcineurin inhibitors affect an enzyme that plays a significant role in cell-mediated immunity.
D. Dihydrofolate reductase inhibitors (DRIs) are nonbiologic DMARDs that inhibit folate production.

question: An elderly male presents with flat-topped, purple, polygonal, pruritic papules on his anterior wrists. He denies taking any medications. When he scratches unaffected skin, he develops similar-appearing lesions at the site he scratched. Which of the following are the names of this phenomenon and the condition described?
explanation:
This patient’s presentation suggests lichen planus, an inflammatory condition of the skin and mucous membranes. Patients often develop purplish flat-topped papules on the wrists, ankles, and forearms, but any site may be involved. When mucous membranes are affected, patients often present with reticular white plaques called Wickham striae. They may also have erosions and ulcerations, and the patient may complain of pruritus or burning of the lesions. Other sites of involvement include the nails, which can present with fissuring, longitudinal ridging, and dorsal pterygium due to scarring of the proximal nail fold with the nail matrix. The cause of lichen planus is not known, but it has been associated with infections such as hepatitis C, dental materials in oral lichen planus, and physical and emotional stress. Certain medications including beta-blockers, ACE inhibitors, calcium channel blockers, NSAIDs, and diuretics can cause a lichen planus-like eruption that is more photodistributed than in classic lesions. In response to trauma, lesions of lichen planus can develop on previously unaffected skin; this is known as the Koebner phenomenon or koebnerization. This phenomenon can also appear in conditions such as psoriasis or vasculitis. Lichen planus lesions on the skin often clear spontaneously. Treatment for pruritus includes topical steroids and antihistamines. For more severe cases of lichen planus, therapies such as acitretin and narrowband UVB light therapy have been used

C. Nikolsky sign in Stevens-Johnson syndrome occurs when horizontal pressure to the skin leads
D. Auspitz sign in psoriasis refers to pinpoint bleeding from the removal of scales on psoriatic

question: A 30-year-old man presents to his primary care physician with persistent lower extremity ulcer and hematochezia. Examination reveals an ulcer with exudate and dusky-red irregular borders. ESR is notably elevated. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
This patient’s presentation suggests pyoderma gangrenosum, a noninfectious, ulcerative condition that is often associated with inflammatory bowel disease. Lesions typically occur on the lower extremities with a painful nodule or pustule that later becomes an ulcer with a violaceous hue and undermined border. Other variants include a pustular subtype that causes pustules with surrounding redness, a vesiculobullous subtype that causes vesicles and bullous lesions on an erythematous base that may have central necrosis, and a vegetative subtype with superficial ulcerations. Lesions of pyoderma gangrenosum may heal with cribriform, hyperpigmented, or hypopigmented scars. Pyoderma gangrenosum has been associated with various conditions, including inflammatory bowel disease, lymphoproliferative conditions, and connective tissue disorders. Other more common causes of ulceration, such as venous stasis ulcers, ecthyma, squamous cell carcinoma, and others, should be ruled out before making a diagnosis of pyoderma gangrenosum. A biopsy should be done for hematoxylin and eosin staining, as well as for culture, to rule out infectious causes. Treatment includes wound care, steroids, cyclosporine, and topical tacrolimus. Underlying conditions that could precipitate pyoderma gangrenosum, such as inflammatory bowel disease, should be treated in conjunction with the skin lesions. As lesions of pyoderma gangrenosum show pathergy (the development of lesions at sites of trauma to the skin), it is recommended to avoid surgical therapy if possible

B. Behcet’s disease is a vasculitis affecting many sites throughout the body. Skin can be affected
D. Lipodermatosclerosis is an inflammation of subcutaneous fat in the lower extremities. This can

question: A 30-year-old man presents with frontotemporal hair loss and thinning in the vertex scalp. His father has similar issues. There are no skin lesions present, and no pruritus. What is the best treatment for this patient's condition?
explanation:
This patient's presentation is characteristic of male pattern (androgenic) alopecia. Male patients often report recession of the anterior hairline along their temples and thinning of hair on the vertex scalp. They do not typically report pain or pruritus. In female-pattern hair loss, patients often do not notice recession of their anterior hairline but have hair thinning on the vertex scalp. Androgenic alopecia involves dihydrotestosterone promoting the "miniaturization" of androgen-sensitive hair follicles. It is classified as non-cicatricial or non-scarring hair loss as opposed to the hair loss in central centrifugal alopecia, discoid lupus, or frontal fibrosing alopecia, which are scarring processes. For men, androgenic alopecia can be classified based on the Norwood- Hamilton system. Approved treatment options include topical minoxidil, a vasodilator that stimulates hair growth, and oral finasteride, a 5-alpha reductase inhibitor. Patients should be counseled that discontinuation of these medications will lead to hair loss again

B. Tinea capitis is a fungal scalp infection, most commonly affecting young children. It causes
C. Telogen effluvium is a temporary increased loss of hair following stress. It does not follow the
D. Alopecia areata is an autoimmune disorder causing patchy loss of hair. Treatment may include

question: A 4-month-old male is brought to the pediatrician’s office for a check-up. He has had red papules on the cheeks and forehead, now extending to the trunk and the extremities. There is a family history of asthma and childhood eczema. How does infantile atopic dermatitis differ from childhood atopic dermatitis?

choices.A: Infantile atopic dermatitis typically affects the extensor surfaces.
choices.B: Childhood atopic dermatitis typically affects the face and scalp.
choices.C: Infantile atopic dermatitis is usually not pruritic.
choices.D: There is no difference between the presentation of infantile and childhood atopic dermatitis.

correct_text: Infantile atopic dermatitis typically affects the extensor surfaces
explanation:
Atopic dermatitis is a chronic condition, part of the atopic triad, along with allergic rhinitis and asthma. It is the most common form of eczema, followed by nummular and papular eczema. Individuals with atopic dermatitis can retain less moisture in their skin, leading to xerosis (dry skin) and pruritus. Acute atopic dermatitis causes red plaques, vesicles, and crusted plaques, whereas chronic atopic dermatitis leads to thickened, scaly, and dry plaques. The plaques of atopic dermatitis are classically pruritic. Genetic and environmental factors may predispose individuals to atopic dermatitis, and triggers such as harsh soaps, heat, or dry weather can cause atopic dermatitis flares. Other findings in patients with atopic dermatitis include keratosis pilaris and hyper linear palms. Infants, children, and adults may be affected by this condition, although about 85% of patients present within the first year, and about 95% appear by 5 years of age. Infantile atopic dermatitis classically affects the extensor aspects of the extremities, face, and trunk. Atopic dermatitis in children and adults typically affects the flexural aspects and trunk. First- line treatment for atopic dermatitis includes topical steroids for active plaques, low-potency steroids used for the face and intertriginous areas, and mid- to high-potency topical steroids used for the trunk, hands, and feet. Alternatively, topical nonsteroidal agents can be used when there is a concern for adverse effects from topical steroids. For extensive skin diseases not controlled by topical therapies, agents such as cyclosporine, azathioprine, light therapy, and dupilumab have been used. For pruritus affecting sleep, antihistamines such as hydroxyzine may be used. If atopic dermatitis plaques are secondarily infected, treatment must target the underlying infection (e.g., bacterial infection due to Staphylococcus aureus and viral infection due to herpes simplex virus). Patients with difficult-to-control atopic dermatitis should be followed regularly by dermatology

Incorrect Answers:
B. Childhood atopic dermatitis typically affects the flexural surfaces and trunk. The face and scalp
C. Plaques of infantile atopic dermatitis (and other forms of atopic dermatitis) are quite pruritic.

question: A 25-year-old landscaper presents with a pruritic eruption on his upper (Figure 1) and lower extremities after cutting the grass and some small bushes yesterday. The eruption consists of multiple tense vesicles in a linear distribution. He denies difficulty breathing, tongue swelling, or a similar rash elsewhere. Which type of reaction is this patient most likely experiencing?
explanation:
This patient's presentation is most consistent with allergic contact dermatitis (ACD) to poison ivy, which is a type IV hypersensitivity reaction. Patients with allergic-contact dermatitis to poison ivy typically present with well-circumscribed erythematous, edematous papules or plaques that can progress to a linear pattern of vesiculation at the site of contact to the allergen (in this case, urushiol) with associated pruritus. These skin changes usually occur 24-72 hours after antigen exposure in patients who have been previously exposed to poison ivy. The diagnosis is typically made by history and physical examination. Management includes immediately washing the sites that came into contact with poison ivy with soap and water to prevent the spread of urushiol to other parts of the body. For patients with limited skin involvement, topical steroids may be appropriate. For more severe cases and widespread body involvement, oral steroids may be indicated with a 2-3 week taper to prevent a rebound flare. Antihistamines are not effective because type IV hypersensitivity reactions are mediated by T cells. If it is left untreated, the rash usually lasts 3-4 weeks. If a patient is re- exposed to poison ivy, they will develop the rash of allergic-contact dermatitis again

Incorrect Answers:
A. Type I hypersensitivity reactions include urticaria and anaphylaxis
B. Type II hypersensitivity reactions include hemolytic anemia
C. Type III hypersensitivity reactions include serum sickness and vasculitis
E. Allergic-contact dermatitis caused by poison ivy is not an infectious process

question: A patient presents with a 3-day history of intensely pruritic, raised, erythematous wheals that appear suddenly and resolve within 24 hours without leaving residual skin changes. The lesions migrate to different areas of the trunk and extremities. She denies fever or systemic symptoms, and vital signs are normal. She is diagnosed with acute urticaria and started on first-line therapy. What is the mechanism of action of the most appropriate treatment for this condition?
explanation:
This patient's presentation is consistent with urticaria, more commonly known as hives. Urticarial lesions present as pink, edematous, well-circumscribed papules or pruritic plaques. Lesions can affect any body area but most commonly affect the trunk. Each lesion of urticaria lasts under 24 hours. This short time frame can help distinguish urticaria from other conditions with similarappearing skin lesions. Urticaria is separated into acute and chronic forms. Acute urticaria refers to new-onset or recurring episodes of urticarial lesions less than six weeks in duration. Chronic urticaria refers to recurring bouts of urticaria for over six weeks. There are many causes of urticaria, including medications, infections, pregnancy, food, autoimmune conditions, and ultraviolet radiation. In about half of the cases of acute urticaria, the cause is unknown, which is idiopathic urticaria. Diagnosis is typically clinical, and a biopsy is unnecessary without unusual findings (e.g., if the lesions last over 24 hours) suggesting a different diagnosis. Usually, no routine laboratory testing is needed for acute urticaria or mild antihistamine- responsive urticaria. Recalcitrant urticaria should prompt a further workup with a complete blood count and thyroid function testing, for example. First-line treatment for urticaria includes non-sedating antihistamines such as cetirizine or loratadine daily. A short-acting antihistamine such as hydroxyzine can be used as needed along with the daily antihistamine. A short course of oral steroids can be beneficial for severe cases of urticaria. Acute urticaria resolves within six weeks, and patients with chronic urticaria typically are clear of lesions within six months, although some cases can last for years. If patients with urticaria present with anaphylaxis, immediate medical attention is necessary, including intramuscular or subcutaneous epinephrine and stabilization in a hospital setting. The airway should be secured and vital signs monitored, as airway obstruction or shock can result from anaphylaxis

B. Patients with viral exanthem or other viral rashes frequently have systemic symptoms of
D. Bacterial skin infections typically produce lesions that are uniformly erythematous, nonmigratory, firm, warm, and tender to touch. Systemic signs, such as fever, may be present. The

question: A middle-aged woman presents for evaluation of persistent redness on her cheeks with prominent telangiectasias (Figure 1). She notes that the redness flares with exercise, spicy foods, and wine. Which of the following questions should be posed?
explanation:
This patient’s presentation is most consistent with rosacea, affecting the skin and the eyes. Rosacea is a common inflammatory condition with four main subtypes -erythematotelangiectatic, papulopustular, phymatous, and ocular rosacea. Erythematotelangiectatic rosacea presents with erythema and telangiectasias of the cheek, with facial flushing in response to triggers such as stress, heat, ultraviolet radiation, and spicy foods. Treatment includes avoiding triggers for flares and good sun protection. Topical vasoconstricting medications such as brimonidine or oxymetazoline and laser therapy can be tried for generalized erythema of the cheeks and telangiectasias. Papulopustular rosacea causes pink papules and pustules on the cheeks without comedones. In addition to avoiding triggers, treatment includes topical metronidazole cream or oral doxycycline therapy. Phymatous rosacea shows irregular, non-tender skin thickening, which commonly affects the nose and possibly the chin and forehead. Oral isotretinoin and ablative laser therapy for reshaping and recontouring the hypertrophied skin have been used, although treatment for this form of rosacea is often challenging. Ocular rosacea may cause a gritty sensation in the eyes, blepharitis, and conjunctivitis. Patients who report visual symptoms should be evaluated by ophthalmology for evidence of ocular rosacea. Oral antibiotics such as doxycycline are often used to help treat ocular rosacea. Untreated, ocular rosacea may lead to keratitis and, in severe cases, blindness

Incorrect Answers:
A. Scleroderma, dermatomyositis, and other autoimmune conditions can cause skin changes and
B. Hypothyroidism, irritable bowel syndrome, and Celiac disease are conditions where a rash is
D. Dermatomyositis, lupus and rheumatoid arthritis are associated with rash and musculoskeletal

question: A 34-year-old patient develops a rash on her back after a long day at the beach. The lesions are pink, with raised annular margins. Most are 0.5 – 2 cm in diameter, and some are confluent. She also has similar scaly pink plaques on other sun-exposed sites. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
This patient’s presentation is consistent with subacute cutaneous lupus, a connective tissue disorder. There are skin-limited forms of lupus, including discoid lupus, subacute cutaneous lupus erythematosus, and acute cutaneous lupus erythematosus, as well as a systemic form of lupus, which involves additional organ systems. In subacute cutaneous lupus erythematosus, patients have an eruption of annular, scaly, pink plaques on sun-exposed sites, including the upper back, chest, and forearms. Lesions heal without scarring in contrast to lesions of discoid lupus. Sunlight can cause flares of lesions of subacute cutaneous lupus as well as the other forms of lupus. In acute lupus erythematosus, patients often present with a malar rash of the cheeks and the bridge of the nose with classic sparing of the nasolabial folds. The rash can be pruritic or painful. This subtype of cutaneous lupus may progress to systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE). In discoid lupus, patients present with scarred, raised, or atrophic, scaly, red plaques, often on the face, ears, and upper extremities. The lesions are often hyperpigmented at the periphery and depigmented in the center. The diagnosis of lupus is based on the clinical picture and biopsy. Laboratory testing is usually positive for anti-nuclear antibodies. In SLE, anti-dsDNA and anti-Smith are often positive as well. The mainstay treatment options include photoprotection to prevent flares of lupus, topical corticosteroids for localized disease, and antimalarials for the treatment of more generalized disease or systemic lupus erythematosus. Other systemic options include methotrexate, cyclosporine, oral steroids, and cyclophosphamide. Patients with involvement of other organ systems should be followed closely by rheumatology

Incorrect Answers:
A. Acute cutaneous lupus classically leads to a malar rash on the face.
C. Discoid lupus classically causes scarred, scaly plaques that may be hyperpigmented at the
D. Psoriasis classically causes pink, well-defined plaques with silvery scale.

question: An elderly man develops a well-circumscribed brown macule with moth-eaten borders. This lesion is symmetric and has uniform pigmentation. The patient has no other symptoms. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
This patient's presentation suggests a solar lentigo, which is a benign brown macule that develops in response to ultraviolet radiation. These lesions are well-circumscribed, uniform in color, and frequently have a "moth-eaten" or scalloped border. Solar lentigos are commonly found on the face and dorsal hands but can appear on any sun-exposed site. They commonly appear in Fitzpatrick skin types I-III as well as individuals with a long history of sun exposure. It is important to distinguish this benign entity from a lentigo maligna, which is a type of melanoma that also appears on sun- exposed sites. Lentigo maligna lesions tend to be more asymmetric, have variegated pigmentation, and may be noted to be changing by the patient or provider. The diagnosis of solar lentigos is made clinically. However, if there is any concern that the lesion could be a lentigo maligna, then a biopsy is strongly recommended. No treatment is needed for a benign solar lentigo, although sun protection is recommended, as solar lentigos are markers of sun damage. Laser therapy and topical lightening creams can be used to reduce the appearance of these lesions if desired

Incorrect Answers:
A. This patient’s presentation is more consistent with a solar lentigo.
C. Actinic keratosis causes scaly pink papules on sun-exposed sites.
D. Dermal nevi cause elevated pigmented papules.

question: A 26-year-old man develops a verrucous papule with thrombosed vessels on his plantar foot (Figure 1). He notes that this developed soon after going to the gym, where he does not wear shoes while showering. Which of the following human papillomavirus (HPV) genotypes is most associated with this presentation?
explanation:
This patient's presentation is most consistent with a plantar wart caused by HPV genotypes 1, 2, and 4. Warts are a common infection of the skin due to HPV. They typically lead to verrucous, hyperkeratotic papules with thrombosed vessels. Warts may merge to form a giant wart called a mosaic wart, which frequently occurs with plantar warts. They may be asymptomatic or painful. Various subtypes (including flat warts, genital warts, and plantar warts) are caused by different strains of HPV. Common warts typically appear on the fingers and back of the hands and are caused by HPV genotypes 2 and 4. Flat warts look more flat-topped and smoother than most other warts and are classically caused by genotypes 3 and 10. Anogenital warts, also known as condyloma acuminata, are often caused by genotypes 6 and 11. Treatment is not necessary for warts as they may clear on their own. Patients may want treatment for symptomatic disease or cosmetic reasons. Patients can use over-the-counter 40% salicylic acid, followed by paring down the wart. Liquid nitrogen therapy, intralesional bleomycin, intralesional candida antigen, or electrodesiccation and curettage can be performed in the office. Liquid nitrogen therapy is the firstline treatment. Patients may require multiple cycles of each of these treatments before the wart is cleared

B. HPV 3 and 10 are responsible for flat warts. Flat warts look more flat-topped and smoother than
C. HPV 5 and 8 are responsible for epidermodysplasia verruciformis (i.e., "tree man syndrome"),
D. HPV 16, 18, 31, and 33 are responsible for squamous cell carcinomas of the upper

question: A 20-year-old man presents to his primary care provider with hypopigmented patches on his left shoulder and chest. The lesions appeared after spending a day at the beach, and they are neither painful nor pruritic. A potassium hydroxide preparation reveals both hyphae and yeast cells. Which of the following describes the mechanism of hypopigmentation in this condition?

choices.C: Loss-of-function tyrosinase mutation in affected cells

explanation:
Pityriasis versicolor (tinea versicolor) is a superficial fungal infection characterized by hypopigmented macules, most commonly on the upper extremities and trunk. Cases are mediated by Malassezia species, which primarily affects young adults and is associated with humid conditions and excessive perspiration. Lesions are typically macules that may coalesce into confluent thin plaques. These are most commonly hypopigmented but may be hyperpigmented to erythematous or scaly. Hypopigmentation may occur through the fungal production of azelaic acid, leading to the suppression of melanocyte activity. Lesions are often asymptomatic and may not be noticed by patients with lighter skin tones until after developing a suntan. While other tinea disease states such as tinea pedis are caused by dermatophytes, this is not the case with tinea versicolor. After treatment with potassium hydroxide, microscopic evaluation of skin scrapings will show both hyphae and yeast cells (“spaghetti and meatballs” appearance). The treatment of choice is selenium sulfide lotion or shampoo. A wide range of other topical antifungals can also be used. A short course of an oral antifungal medication (like fluconazole or itraconazole) is also effective, but the risk of adverse effects is higher

Incorrect Answers:
A. The autoimmune destruction of melanocytes describes vitiligo.
C. A loss-of-function tyrosinase mutation causes hereditary albinism.
D. Post-inflammatory inhibition of melanogenesis may occur in various dermatologic conditions,

question: A 30-year-old female with HIV visits her primary care physician for several skin lesions below her left eyelid along with associated follicular conjunctivitis. She has no other issues to report. Physical examination is otherwise unremarkable, and there are no other lesions on her body. No recent changes to her antiretroviral medications have been made. A CD4 count is 70 cells/mm3. A pathology specimen of the skin lesions was obtained (see below). The rash associated with the following histopathology specimen can best be described as:

choices.A: multiple vesicles and pustules with an overlying honey-colored crust.
choices.B: multiple waxy dome shaped papules with central umbilication.
choices.C: grouped vesicles on an erythematous base.
choices.E: 556 pruritic erythematous maculopapular rash with scale. F. skin-colored or erythematous papule or plaque.

correct_text: multiple waxy dome shaped papules with central umbilication
explanation:
Molluscum contagiosum is caused by a DNA poxvirus. It usually occurs in children and immunosuppressed individuals, and appears as 1-5 mm waxy, dome-shaped nodules with central umbilications (dimples). If near the eyelid margin, it can cause follicular conjunctivitis if not treated. Histologically, the lesions appear lobulated and cup-shaped. At the center of each lobule are eosinophilic-to-basophilic viral inclusions within keratinocytes. These abnormal cells are termed molluscum bodies or Henderson-Patterson bodies (arrow)

question: A 30-year-old male presents to his primary care physician with a 3-week history of painful ulcerations on his hard palate and gingiva. He describes that the pain was first noticeable when chewing food, but ulcerating blisters developed in his mouth. He takes no medications. Family and social history are unremarkable. On physical examination, several hemorrhagic erosions are noted on the hard palate. His gingivae appear diffusely erythematous and show a positive Nikolsky sign. He has no additional skin lesions. The most common form of this blistering disease involves autoantibodies directed against which of the following?
explanation:
In pemphigus vulgaris, a blistering autoimmune disease, IgG autoantibodies bind to a protein known as desmoglein 3. This protein is found in desmosomes, which are present in keratinocytes at the epidermal base. Desmosomes are arranged on the lateral aspect of cell membranes and function in cell-to-cell adhesion. Desmosomes connect cells strongly. They are found in tissues subject to high-stress levels (e.g., cardiac, gastrointestinal, epithelia, bladder). Pemphigus vulgaris is the most common form of pemphigus in the oral cavity

E. Auto-antibodies to the nicotinic acetylcholine receptor are found in myasthenia gravis, an

question: Which HPV subtypes are associated with verrucous carcinoma?
explanation:
Verrucous carcinomas is a rare clinical subtype of cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma (cSCC.) It is characterized by well-defined, exophytic, cauliflower-like growths that resemble large warts. The lesions are subclassified according to site. Oral florid papillomatosis refers to verrucous carcinoma of the oral mucosa. Anogenital lesions are also known as giant condyloma acuminatum of Buschke-Lowenstein. Epithelioma cuniculatum refers to verrucous carcinoma on the plantar surface of the foot. These tumors are associated with HPV 6,11, 16, and 18

Incorrect Answers:
B. HPV 32 is seen in Heck’s Disease (Focal epithelial hyperplasia in the oral cavity).
C. HPV 1,2, and 4 are associated with the common plantar warts.
D. HPV 3,10 are commonly seen in flat warts.

question: Which is not a risk factor for the lesion pictured below?
explanation: Smoking, smokeless tobacco, alcohol, betel nut chewing, weakened immune system, and personal or family history of cancer have all been linked to oral leukoplakia, a precancerous lesion located in the oral mucosa.

question: A patient with a history of uterine leiomyomas presents with new onset cutaneous lesions and notes a history of a parent with similar lesions. Which of the following should the practitioner consider checking if the biopsy comes back consistent with his presumed diagnosis?
explanation:
It is due to a mutation in fumarate hydratase, which encodes an enzyme in the Krebs cycle that converts fumarate to malate. Patients with this condition have cutaneous as well as uterine leiomyomas. In addition, they are at a higher risk of RCC, and thus should undergo renal screening regularly and receive genetic counseling

Incorrect Answers:
A. Reed syndrome does not affect the pulmonary system
B. Reed syndrome does not affect the neural system
C. Reed syndrome does not affect the neural system
E. Reed syndrome does not affect the liver.

question: Which of the following is the greatest risk factor for SCC?
explanation:
A study by Jensen et. al showed that patients with heart transplants are 2.8x more likely than liver transplant patients to have SCCs

Incorrect Answers:
B. Not as high as heart transplant patients.
C. Patients with lupus at increased risk but not as high as heart transplant patients.
D. DM is not associated with an increased risk of SCC.

question: Which is the most common tumor to arise from the lesion below?
explanation: NS usually presents as a solitary lesion on the scalp early in childhood. It appears as a yellowish plaque with surrounding alopecia. Previously it was believed that the most common associated malignant neoplasm was BCC and benign neoplasm was syringocystadenoma papilliferum. More recently, studies have shown trichoblastoma to be more commonly associated with a nevus sebaceus than syringocystadenoma papilliferum.

question: Microcystic adnexal carcinoma is most likely to occur on the:
explanation: It is most commonly found on the skin of the upper lip. It has also been described on the chin, nasolabial fold, and cheek.

question: Which of the following neoplasms is most closely associated with necrolytic migratory erythema?
explanation: NME, also known as glucagonoma syndrome, is most commonly seen in patients with an alpha-2 glucagon secreting islet-cell tumor of the pancreas. These tumors tend to be malignant and metastatic. Lesions present as flaccid vesicles, pustules, bullae, erosions, and erythema over the lower extremity, central face, and perineum. Patients also have elevated serum glucagon, abnormal glucose tolerance, weight loss, +/- glossitis, stomatitis, dystrophic nails, alopecia, and anemia. Lesions tend to disappear 48-72 hours after resection of tumor.

question: Which of the following is most often associated with paraneoplastic pemphigus?
explanation: PNP presents as severe intractable oral ulcerations/erosions that appear EM- like. Skin lesions can be lichenoid, targetoid, as well as PV- like flaccid bullae. Patients can die from respiratory failure. Symptoms are the result of antibodies to plectin, desmoplakin I and II, DSG 1 and 3, envoplakin, periplakin, and BPAg1. DIF shows characteristic IgG and C3 intercellular and linear/granular band at DEJ. Most common associations include NHL (42%), CLL (29%), and Castleman disease (6%). Treatment primarily focuses on treating the underlying malignancy.

question: Trousseau syndrome is most often associated with which of the following?
explanation: Thrombophlebitis migrans has long been thought of as a marker of internal malignancy. Associated neoplasms include pancreatic, prostate, lung, liver, and bowel. It is most commonly associated with mucin-producing adenocarcinomas of the pancreas or lung.

question: Which HPV strain is most closely associated with periungual squamous cell carcinomas?
explanation:
HPV 16 is seen commonly in periungual SCCs, anal lesions, oral papillomas, and high-risk genital carcinomas

Incorrect Answers:
A. HPV 6 has been implicated in the etiology of condyloma and verrucous carcinoma.
C. HPV 32 is seen in Heck’s Disease (Focal epithelial hyperplasia in the oral cavity).
D. HPV 1,2, and 4 are associated with the common plantar warts.
E. HPV 3,10 are commonly seen in flat warts.

question: Which histologic subtype of basal cell carcinoma is considered most likely to recur?
explanation: Morpheaform (also known as sclerosing) and basosquamous BCCs have been shown to be the most aggressive and necessitate Mohs surgery to ensure complete excision and minimize chance for recurrence. Morpheaform BCCs can present as indurated white or erythematous scar-like lesions and make up approximately 5% of all BCCs.

question: A patient presents with the lesions below for 6 months. She notes mild pruritus that has not responded to mid potency topical steroids. What is the next step in evaluation?
explanation:
Given lack of improvement with topical treatment, the next step would be to biopsy to rule out Paget’s disease. Paget’s disease of the breast, also known as mammary Paget's disease, is an intra- epidermal adenocarcinoma seen over the nipple or areola. It typically presents as an eczema like plaque on the nipple that then extends to the areola. It does not respond to topical steroids as eczema would

Incorrect Answers:
A. This patient needs a biopsy to rule out Paget’s disease given the lack of improvement with mid
B. Patch testing is used to identify specific allergens in allergic contact dermatitis. A biopsy is
D. CEA is not a marker for breast adenocarcinoma and thus would not be useful to order in this
E. Observation is inappropriate given the lack of improvement with topical treatment.

question: The lesions below are most likely to be associated with which of the following?
explanation: Sweet’s syndrome (acute febrile netrophillic dermatosis) clinically presents as recurrent “juicy” red papules and nodules and can occasionally present as bullous lesions, most often on face, neck, upper extremity. Other symptoms include fever, leukocytosis, arthalgias, as well as +/- renal, neuro, hepatic, pulmonary symptoms. Lab abnormalities include peripheral leukocytosis and neutrophilia. Associations include Yersinia infection, URI, IBD, medications (most commonly GCSF/neupogen and Bactrim), pregnancy, and malignancy (most commonly AML).

question: What is the most commonly occurring associated condition in patients with POEMS syndrome?
explanation: POEMS, also known as Crow-Fukase syndrome is a rare, paraneoplastic syndrome that manifests as polyneuropathy, organomegaly, endocrinopathy, M-protein, and skin changes such as diffuse hyperpigmentation, dependent edema, skin thickening, hyperhidrosis, and hypertrichosis. It can have associated microvenular and glomeruloid hemangiomas and can have associated Castelman’s syndrome in up to 25% of cases.

question: The lesions below have been present for approximately 3 years and did not improve with low dose topical steroids or emollients. The patient also had palpable lymph nodes on exam. In this condition, which of the following markers are lost?
explanation: The lesions above represent Mycosis Fungoides, the most common type of CTCL. Lesions may present as non-descript eczematous, scaly patches, or progress to nodules and larger plaques (tumor stage), as well as erythroderma. A leukemic variant of MF, Sezary Syndrome, is characterized by pruritic erythroderma, lymphadenopathy, and the presence of > 1000 Sezary cells/microliter in the peripheral blood. MF profile- CD3+ CD4+ CD45O+/ CD 7-, CD 26-.

question: A patient with lesions below has lesions on greater than 50% of the body but no lymphadenopathy and no significant blood involvement. What stage is this considered?
explanation: Mycoses fungoides staging is based on TNMB criteria. Standard staging classification system for mycosis fungoides (MF) and Sézary syndrome (SS) utlizes the TNMB system, which is based on evaluation of the skin (T), lymph nodes (N), visceral involvement (M), and blood (B). T1 refers to patches, papules, and plaques that cover < 10%,BSA. T2 refers to patches, papules, and plaques that cover >10%. T3 refers to presence of one or more tumors 1 cm diameter or greater and T4 refers to confluence of erythema that covers 80 percent or more of the BSA. Nodes: none = N0; N1 = clinically abnormal with Dutch grade 1 or NCI LNO-2 ; N2 = clnically abnormal with Dutch grade 2 or NCI LN3 histopathology. N2a is clone negative and N2b is clone positive. N3 = abnormal lymph nodes on exam with Dutch grades 3-4 or NCI LN4 histopathology; clone positive or negative. Nx refers to palpable abnormal nodes without histological confirmation. Viscera: M0 = no visceral organ involvement. M1 = visceral organ involvement with pathological confirmation. Blood: B0 refers to no significant blood involvement; B1 refers to limited tumor burden; and B2 refers to a high blood tumor burden. IA: T1, N0, M0, B0 or B1 IB: T2, N0, M0, B0 or B1 IIA: T1-2, N1 or N2, M0, B0 or B1 IIB: T3, N0 to N2, M0, B0 or B1 IIIA: T4, N0 to N2, M0, B0 IIIB: T4, N0-N2, M0, B1 IVA: T1-4, N0-N2, M0, B2 IVB T1-4, N0-3, M1, B0-2 i.e. stage I is skin only; stage II is either a palpable LN or tumor; stage III is erythroderma +/- palpable LN; stage IV is metastatic to viscera or histologically positive LN. Please note that “B” or blood status is not involved in staging! The Dutch grading system for lymph nodes in MF is as follows: N1 Grade 1: dermatopathic lymphadenopathy (DL) N2 Grade 2: DL; early involvement by MF (presence of cerebriform nuclei> 7.5 micro-m) N3 Grade 3: partial effacement of LN architecture; many atypicalcerebriform mononuclear cells (CMCs) Grade 4: complete effacement

question: In Woringer-Kolopp disease (Pagetoid Reticulosis), which of the following are generally negative?
explanation: Pagetoid reticulosis, also known as Woringer-Kolopp disease, is a rare variant of MF with localized patches or plaques with an intraepidermal proliferation of neoplastic T cells. The infiltrate can be CD4+ or CD8+. Unlike classic MF, CD45 (leukocyte common antigen) tends to be negative. Lesions tend to favor extremities.

question: Which of the following is a marker in B-cell lymphoma?
explanation:
CD20 (on B cells) is one of the best markers for B-cell lymphomas, but up to 15% of cases may be negative. It also occasionally stains T-cell lymphoma. CD20 is targeted by the drug rituximab

Incorrect Answers:
A. CD5 (T cells, some B cells in the mantle zone) is positive in B-cell chronic lymphocytic
C. CD25 (IL-2 receptor alpha chain) is present on activated T cells (including regulatory T cells), B
D. CD30 (activated T and B cells) is positive in large atypical cells of large cell anaplastic

question: Which of the following is the most common location for glomus tumors?
explanation:
Glomus tumors are usually found on the hands and nails. They arise from the neuromyoarterial glomus cells of the nail bed dermis. Clinically, the patient presents with pain that is triggered by cold temperatures. The tumor appears as a red-bluish nail bed macule visible through the nail plate. Treatment is surgical excision

Incorrect Answers:
A. Milia are subepidermal keratin cysts most often located on the face.
B. Ganglion cysts are often found on the wrist or hand.
D. Dermatofibromas (benign fibrous histiocytomas) are frequently found on the lower extremities.
E. Genitals - this is not a site where glomus tumors are typically found subepidermal keratin cysts

question: A patient with lesions below has lesions on 40% of the body and clinically abnormal peripheral lymph nodes, histopathology Dutch grade 2. On the peripheral smear, 7 % of the cells are atypical Sezary cells. No visceral organ involvement is present. What mycosis fungoides TNMB stage would be correct for this patient?
explanation: None of the tumors are noted to be greater than 1-cm in diameter which may bring the lesion up to a T3 lesion. In addition, there is no confluence of lesions seen which encompass 80% or greater skin surface area which would be appropriate for a T4 . There are clinically abnormal lymph nodes present, which we are told are reported as Dutch grade II in pathology- which is consistent with a N2. There are no visceral lesions present at this time (M0). Sezary cells are noted to be 7% of the peripheral smear, consistent with B1. TNMB stages Skin T1 Limited patches,* papules, and/or plaques† covering < 10% of the skin surface. May further stratify into T1a (patch only) vs T1b (plaque ± patch). T2 Patches, papules or plaques covering ≥ 10% of the skin surface. May further stratify into T2a (patch only) vs T2b (plaque ± patch). T3 One or more tumors‡ (≥ 1-cm diameter) T4 Confluence of erythema covering ≥ 80% body surface area Node N0 No clinically abnormal peripheral lymph nodes§; biopsy not required N1 Clinically abnormal peripheral lymph nodes; histopathology Dutch grade 1 or NCI LN0-2 N1a Clone negative# N1b Clone positive# N2 Clinically abnormal peripheral lymph nodes; histopathology Dutch grade 2 or NCI LN3 N2a Clone negative# N2b Clone positive# N3 Clinically abnormal peripheral lymph nodes; histopathology Dutch grades 3-4 or NCI LN4; clone positive or negative Nx Clinically abnormal peripheral lymph nodes; no histologic confirmation Visceral M0 No visceral organ involvement M1 Visceral involvement (must have pathology confirmation¶ and organ involved should be specified) Blood B0 Absence of significant blood involvement: ≤ 5% of peripheral blood lymphocytes are atypical (Sézary) cells‖ B0a Clone negative# B0b Clone positive# B1 Low blood tumor burden: > 5% of peripheral blood lymphocytes are atypical (Sézary) cells but does not meet the criteria of B2 B1a Clone negative# B1b Clone positive# B2 High blood tumor burden: ≥ 1000/µL Sézary cells‖ with positive clone

question: What is the most common location for sebaceous carcinoma?
explanation: Sebaceous carcinoma is an aggressive malignancy thought to be derived from the sebaceous glands of the skin. This rare tumor may occur in sebaceous glands anywhere on the body, but the vast majority (approximately 75%) are seen in the periocular area. Sebaceous carcinoma represents 0.2% to 0.8% of eyelid tumors and 1% to 6.4% of eyelid malignancies. It is fourth in frequency behind basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, and melanoma of the eyelid. The upper eyelid is more commonly involved, presumably because of the increased number of meibomian glands in this location. The treatment of choice is Mohs surgery.

question: The lesion below appeared over the span of 3 weeks. It is symptomatic and often bleeds. The patient denies any past medical history or similar lesions. Which of the following best represents the lesion?
explanation: Keratoacanthomas are thought to be some variant of squamous cell carcinomas and present with a dome-shaped nodular appearance, with the profound cup-like central crater. A key portion of the history is that these dome-shaped lesions may attain quite a large size within a very short duration of time (within weeks). Of note, dermatopathologist think of this entity of a well-differentiated squamous cell carcinoma, mainly because the deep, mitotically active portions of the lesions are very difficult to differentiate from a squamous cell carcinoma. The treatment of this lesion is total excision. It tends to occur on sun- exposed areas in middle aged and elderly individuals. Without treatment, they tend to involute and resolve within 6 months. Risk factors include sun exposure, immunosuppression, as well as some reports of prior trauma to an area. They can be seen in Ferguson-Smith Syndrome (AD disease of multiple self-healing KAs in a localized area), eruptive KAs of Gryzbowski (hundreds of KAs in a generalized distribution), and Muir-Torre syndrome (AD disease that also includes sebaceous carcinoma and internal malignancies).

question: What is the first stage of carcinogenesis?
explanation: Carcinogenesis has four stages: initiation, promotion, progression and malignant conversion. The first stage, initiation, involves a change in the genetic makeup of a cell. This may occur randomly or when a carcinogen interacts with DNA causing damage. This initial damage rarely results in cancer because the cell has in place many mechanisms to repair damaged DNA. However, if repair does not occur and the damage to DNA is in the location of a gene that regulates cell growth and proliferation, DNA repair, or a function of the immune system, then the cell is more prone to becoming cancerous. During promotion, the mutated cell is stimulated to grow and divide faster and becomes a population of cells, which may result in a benign tumor. In human cancers, hormones, cigarette smoke, or bile acids are substances that are involved in promotion. This stage is usually reversible as evidenced by the fact that lung damage can often be reversed after smoking stops. The progression phase is less well understood. During progression, there is further growth and expansion of the tumor cells over normal cells. The genetic material of the tumor is more fragile and prone to additional mutations. These mutations occur in genes that regulate growth and cell function such as oncogenes, tumor suppressor genes, and DNA mismatch- repair genes. These changes contribute to tumor growth until conversion occurs, when the growing tumor becomes malignant and possibly metastatic.

question: What is the lag time between radiation exposure and development of NMSC?
explanation: Ionizing radiation can produce NMSCs as a result of fractionated, small doses of radiation following a lag time of 10-30 years, although most sources quote >20 years.

question: Which of the following has been associated with adult T-cell leukemia/lymphoma?
explanation:
ATLL is endemic in Southwest Japan, Caribbean islands, South America, and parts of Central Africa. Almost all cases have integrated HTLV-1 genes. Skin lesions usually occur because of widely disseminated disease and usually occur after 2 years of viral persistence. Most patients present with acute ATLL: leukemia, lymphadenopathy, organomegaly, and hypercalcemia. Markers- CD3+, CD4+, CD8-, HTLV-1+

Incorrect Answers:
A. Hepatitis B is associated with polyarteritis nodosum. It is not, however, associated with ATLL
B. EBV is associated with different conditions such as mononucleosis and Gianotti Crosti infection.
D. HIV is associated with conditions such as Kaposi sarcoma.

question: Which of the following is associated with extranodal NK/T-cell lymphoma, nasal-Type?
explanation:
Peripheral T-cell lymphomas make up less than 15% of non-Hodgkins lymphomas. Extranodal natural killer T (NK/T) cell lymphoma, nasal type, is most often seen in patients from Asia and Central/South America. There is a strong association with Epstein-Barr virus. The median age at presentation is 52, although rare cases have occurred in childhood. The pathogenesis is related to infection of tumor cells with EBV. Tumors overexpress p53 tumor suppressor protein and 25% have evid3nce of a TP53 mutation. Lesions typically appear as significant nodules/plaques with ulceration over nose/ philtrum, although other extranodal sites may be involved. CD56 is usually expressed. The tumors have a polymorphous lymphoid infiltrate with invasion of vascular walls. This results in fibrinoid necrosis and coagulative necrosis of adjacent tissues. Cell morphology varies. Markers CD2+ CD3+ CD56+ are expressed by the tumors and cytotoxic granule proteins are negative for surface CD3

Incorrect Answers:
A. Hepatitis B can be associated with polyarteritis nodosum. It is not associated with extra NK/T
C. HTLV-1 is associated with adult T cell leukemia. It is however not associated with extra NK/T
D. HIV is associated with Kaposi sarcoma. It is not associated with extra NK/T cell lymphoma

question: Which of the following is associated with the lesions pictured below?
explanation:
The lesions above represent Kaposi’s Sarcoma, a malignant proliferation of vascular endothelial cells. KS is broken into 4 types: 1) Classic type Kaposi’s sarcoma is typically seen in elderly European men on the lower extremities. This form is slow growing 2) African Endemic type is seen more commonly in individuals living in sub-Saharan Africa. It is more likely to occur in patients younger than 40 yrs. old. 3) Patients who have received a transplant and are on immunosuppressive medications are more likely to have infection with HHV8 and Kaposi sarcoma. 4) HIV associated KS is the most common type of Kaposi sarcoma that is diagnosed in the US. Patients with HIV and/or AID have weakened immune system that increases the risk of Kaposi sarcoma

Incorrect Answers:
A. EBV is associated with mononucleosis and Gianotti Crosti infection. It is not associated with
B. HCV has been associated with mixed cryoglobulinemia, PCT, and lichen planus. It is not
C. HBV is associated with polyarteritis nodosum. It is not associated with Kaposi sarcoma

question: A patient has been referred to a surgeon for a Mohs procedure. Unfortunately, the patient and the Mohs surgeon cannot identify the site of the skin cancer. What is the appropriate next step?

choices.A: Call the primary dermatologist to ask for pictures/diagrams
choices.B: Do a frozen section of all suspicious lesions to determine the site
choices.C: Ask a family member to identify
choices.D: Wait to see if recurrence occurs

correct_text: Call the primary dermatologist to ask for pictures/diagrams
explanation: If there is any confusion regarding the site of surgery, the surgeon must contact the referring physician immediately and request more information. Photographs are often very helpful, and should be taken prior to biopsying lesions, especially on the face where the biospy site may be difficult to identify after it heals.

question: An infant with the lesions below is suspected of having Langerhans cell histiocytosis. Which of the following would you use to confirm the diagnosis?
explanation: Clonal LCH cells have a similar though not identical immunophenotype to Langerhans cells. Like Langerhans cells, LCH cells show positive immunostaining for CD1a, CD207, and S100, and do not characteristically express factor XIIIa (a marker for dermal dendrocytes) or classic macrophage/monocyte markers such as CD68, Mac387 or HAM56. LCH cells are also positive for ATPase, peanut lectin and α-D-mannosidase.

question: In suspected carcinoid syndrome, which urine test should be ordered?
explanation: Carcinoid syndrome occurs in approximately 5% of carcinoid tumors and becomes clinically evident when vasoactive substances from the tumors enter the systemic circulation escaping hepatic degradation. Interestingly, if the primary tumor is from the GI tract (hence releasing serotonin into the hepatic portal circulation), carcinoid syndrome generally does not occur until the tumor has metastasised to the liver, because prior to metastasis, the released serotonin would be metabolised in the liver. If the primary is in the lung, no metastasis needs to occur for carcinoid syndrome to become clinically evident, as the secretion occurs directly in the systemic circulation. Common symptoms include flushing, pellagra-like dermatitis, and scleroderma-like skin changes. The best test is urinary 5-HIAA (the degradation product of serotonin) which is 100% sensitive.

question: What is the most common location for a spindle cell lipoma?
explanation: Spindle cell lipomas are slow growing, asymptomatic subcutaneous tumors histologically composed of mature fat cells, a population of spindle cells, and strands of dense collagen. The tumor occurs almost exclusively on the upper back and posterior neck of middle-aged and older men.

question: Which of the following are risk factors for SCC?
explanation: SCC is the 2nd most common skin cancer and makes up approximately 20% of all NMSC. It is more prevalent in men, the elderly, and those closer to the equator. Risk factors include having light skin (types I and II), chronic sun exposure (whereas BCCs tend to be more closely related to intermittent UVR exposure), immunosuppression, chronic ulcers, burn scars, and chemical carcinogens (such as coal or arsenic).

question: A pediatric patient suddenly develops multiple lesions similar to the one pictured below that eventually resolve on their own. This is known as which variant?
explanation: A keratoacanthoma is considered by many as a low-grade SCC. It is characterized by a rapidly growing, well-demarcated nodule with a “central crater". It tends to occur on sun- exposed areas in middle-aged and elderly individuals. Without treatment, they tend to involute and resolve within 6 months. Risk factors include sun exposure, immunosuppression, as well as some reports of prior trauma to an area. They can be seen in Ferguson-Smith Syndrome (AD disease in childhood of multiple self-healing KAs in a localized area), eruptive KAs of Gryzbowski (hundreds of KAs in a generalized distribution), and Muir-Torre syndrome (AD disease that also includes sebaceous carcinoma and internal malignancies).

question: Which of the following is NOT a risk factor for metastasis in a patient with an SCC?
explanation: It is more prevalent in men, the elderly, and those closer to the equator. Risk factors for metastasis include invasion beyond subcutaneous fat; Breslow >2 mm; diameter >20 mm; poor differentiation; location on temple, ear, and lip; perineural involvement; and immunosuppression.

question: A 22-year-old female has likely metastasis to the skin from an unknown primary tumor. Given the patient's age and sex, which of the following is the most likely location of the primary neoplasm?
explanation: Most common neoplasms in male patients >40 years old (in decreasing frequency): prostate,lung,colorectal. Most common neoplasms in male patients <40 years old (in decreasing frequency): testicular, melanoma, Non-Hodgkin lymphoma. Most common neoplasms in female patients >40 years old(in decreasing frequency): breast, colon, lung, ovarian, melanoma. Most common neoplasms in female patients <40 years old (in decreasing frequency): breast, colon, ovarian.

question: The most common location of a Merkel cell carcinoma is:
explanation: Merkel cell tumors are rare malignancies that demonstrate both neuroendocrine and epithelial differentiation. They occur most often on the head and neck and appear as pink to bluish subcutaneous firm nodules.

question: Which of the following is associated with Merkel cell carcinoma?
explanation:
Merkel cell tumors are rare malignancies that demonstrate both neuroendocrine and epithelial differentiation. They occur most often on the head and neck and appear as pink to bluish subcutaneous firm nodules. Studies have shown MCC to be associated with polyomavirus virus

Incorrect Answers:
A. There are several types of herpes viruses. Herpes simplex type one often causes cold sores
B. CMV is also a type of herpes virus (herpes virus 5). It can cause symptomatic infections in
C. Flaviviridae include West Nile virus, dengue fever, and yellow fever
D. This class of virus includes coxsackie virus, a cause of hand foot mouth syndrome

question: What is the most common location of metastases for basal cell carcinoma (BCC)?
explanation:
A BCC usually presents as pink, pearly papule with arborizing telangiectasias on sun- exposed areas. They are the most common skin cancer. Most are only locally destructive, and metastasis occurs rarely

Incorrect Answers:
A. Colorectal, breast, esophageal, stomach, pancreatic, kidney, and lung cancers and melanoma
B. Melanoma is associated with brain metastasis, as are lung, breast, colon, kidney, and thyroid
C. Primary lung cancers, breast cancers, and hematologic malignancies spread to the brain.
E. Metastatic tumors to the kidneys are rare and do not include BCC.

question: Under dermoscopy, a seborrheic keratosis has which of the following features?
explanation: Hairpin vessels and Millia-like cysts. Under dermoscopy, SKs show fissures, ridges, sharp border, millia-like cysts, follicular openings, fat fingers, and hair pin vessels. Glomerular vessels are commonly found in Bowen’s disease.

question: A melanoma is excised from the cheek of a 65-year-old male. Below is a schematic of the histopathology. What is the best treatment for this patient?

choices.A: Wide local excision with 1-2cm margins, observe neck.
choices.B: Wide local excision with 1 cm margin, intraparotid sentinel lymph node biopsy.
choices.C: Wide local excision with 1-2cm margins, parotidectomy, sentinel lymph node biopsy.
choices.D: Wide local excision with 1-2cm margins, parotidectomy, neck dissection.
choices.E: Wide local excision with 1-2cm margins, neck dissection.

correct_text: Wide local excision with 1 cm margin, intraparotid sentinel lymph node
explanation:
biopsy. This is a T1bN0M0 tumor (see revised staging below), so the best treatment of the neck following wide local excision is intraparotid sentinel lymph node biopsy. Observation is for Tis and T1a only. The measurement would be rounded to 1.0 mm. For a more detailed table of the updated staging of head and neck melanoma, click here. T1a is defined as nonulcerated melanomas <0.8 mm in thickness, and T1b is defined as melanomas from 0.8 to 1.0 mm in thickness regardless of ulceration status and ulcerated melanomas less than 0.8 mm in thickness. The eighth edition Melanoma Expert Panel also noted that the subcategorization of T1 melanomas at a 0.8-mm threshold has clinical relevance, particularly for the role of SLN biopsy in patients with T1 melanomas. Overall, SLN metastases are very infrequent (<5%) in melanomas <0.8 mm in thickness but occur in approximately 5% to 12% of patients with primary melanomas from 0.8 to 1.0 mm in thickness, and consensus guidelines have recommended that SLN biopsy be considered in T1b patients. The prognosis and treatment of cutaneous melanoma depend greatly on the thickness of the lesion. Thus, the key to the evaluation of suspected lesions focuses on obtaining a full- thickness biopsy. Excisional biopsy is the best choice for small lesions or for large lesions in cosmetically favorable locations. Excisional biopsy should extend down to the subcutaneous fat, with a small (2-3 mm) peripheral margin. Punch biopsy can be performed for large lesions or for lesions with a low suspicion of melanoma in a cosmetically unfavorable location. The biopsy should be performed at the highest or thickest point of the lesion. Incisional biopsy is not recommended. Likewise, techniques that do not permit a full- thickness sample, such as shave or curette biopsy, are discouraged. Furthermore, pigmented lesions should not be definitively treated with laser therapy, electrocautery, or cryotherapy unless biopsy analysis proves them to be noncancerous. Some controversy exists in a number of areas. In terms of excision of the primary site, thin melanoma margins are generally accepted to be 1 cm. For melanomas greater than 2 mm in thickness, 2 cm margins are the norm. However, Krown and Chapman noted that the ideal margin width for these deeper lesions has not been adequately studied. More information regarding current excision margin recommendations is reviewed on the subject by Lens. In the head and neck regions, adequate margins may not be possible or advisable due to cosmetic or functional concerns. Radiation therapy also has been controversial in the past due to the fact that the radiosensitivity of melanoma was at least considered questionable. This is changing as more recent data becomes available. Adjuvant radiation therapy for neck disease has been shown to be beneficial for patients with aggressive disease. There are also data that support radiation treatment alone for regional disease control in head and neck melanoma, although no direct comparison of radiation versus surgical treatment has been studied In contrast to the widespread acceptance of lymphatic mapping and sentinel lymph node biopsy for melanomas of the trunk or extremities, its role in the management of cutaneous melanomas of the head and neck continues to evolve. These techniques are limited by the complexity of lymphatic drainage patterns and the frequent need to remove sentinel nodes from the parotid gland, which risks damaging the 644 Board Vitals · Pathology facial nerve. A sentinel lymph node can be identified in approximately 95 percent of patients with low false-negative rates when appropriate techniques are used by experienced surgeons. Thus, until recently, a strong argument could be made for the “wait-and-see approach” to the clinically negative nodal basin because of the morbidity of dissection without evidence of a clear survival benefit. But a recent study has shown that high-dose interferon (IFN) alfa-2b can be given as an adjuvant treatment for high-risk melanomas and indicated that elective nodal dissection should not be delayed until the disease is clinically detectable. In this study, relapse-free survival and overall survival rates improved in patients treated with interferon (IFN) alfa-2b versus controls subject. The widespread use of sentinel lymph node (SLN) biopsy in the management of head and neck melanoma has been limited by several concerns. One is that the lymphatic drainage in the head and neck region is complex, with multiple primary channels and the potential for multiple SLN sites. Secondarily excision of these nodes can be technically challenging as small distances between sentinel nodes make detection and isolation difficult. Furthermore, approximately 25–30% of the sentinel nodes are found within the parotid gland, and concern of facial nerve injury has led many surgeons to advocate superficial parotidectomy over SLN biopsy. And lastly, the cooperation of experienced pathologists and nuclear medicine staff is essential to the success of the procedure. SLNs were successfully localized in greater than 90% of cases, with the combined use of blue dye mapping and gamma probe lymphoscintigraphy. 5 studies demonstrated success rates of 95% or better. The rate of tumor-containing SLNs ranged from 11–17%. Additionally, reviews by Schmalbach et al. and Loree et al. describe accurately localizing intraparotid SLNs in at least 93% of cases

Incorrect Answers:
C. A parotidectomy would be indicated in this case if the sentinel node were positive because of
E. A parotidectomy would be indicated in this case if the sentinel node were positive because of

question: A 27-year-old man with a history of epilepsy presents for an annual exam. He has no complaints. Upon examination, he has several lesions on his toenails and fingernails (see image). He denies pain. What is the most likely condition affecting this patient?
explanation:
TS is a multisystem autosomal dominant disorder caused by mutations in hamartin and tuberin. It is characterized primarily by hamartomas in multiple organ systems. Skin findings include hypopigmented ash-leaf macules, Shagreen patch (CT nevus), facial angiofibromas (adenoma sebaceum), periungual fibromas, and fibrous plaque on the face. CNS findings include infantile spasms, seizures, MR, subependymal nodules with or without hydrocephalus, and astrocytomas. Other related symptoms include retinal hamartomas, renal cysts or angiomyolipomas, and cardiac and pharyngeal rhabdomyomas

Incorrect Answers:
A. Lichen planus affecting the nails causes irregular longitudinal grooving and ridging of the nail
B. Onychomycosis is a superficial fungal infection of the nail bed and underside of the nail plate. It
C. Pachyonychia congenita is a rare genetic disorder that can cause thickening of the fingernails
D. Psoriasis can cause nail pitting, nail whitening, bleeding in capillaries in the nails, and a yellowreddish nail discoloration known as salmon spot.

question: Which of the following cutaneous tumors has the highest potential of becoming a Neurofibrosarcoma?
explanation:
Neurofibrosarcomas, or malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumors (MPNST), can transform from plexiform neurofibromas in 3-15% of patients with Neurofibromatosis Type 1. For this reason, plexiform neurofibromas, which are either congenital or appear by the age of 4-5, require constant monitoring. The sudden onset of acute pain, firmness, or neurologic deficit in an associated plexiform neurofibroma may be a sign of malignant degeneration. Plexiform neurofibromas are invasive and difficult to treat because of their widespread nature. They also can lead to soft tissue hypertrophy, skeletal defects, and limb size discrepancy. Their malignant counterpart, neurofibrosarcomas, can be very aggressive and often fatal

Incorrect Answers:
E. These are incorrect answers. Schwannomas, which are tumors that originate from the schwann

question: What percentage of Plexiform Neurofibromas undergo malignant transformation into Neurofibrosarcomas?
explanation:
Several studies following the behavior of Plexiform Neurofibromas have found a rate of malignant degeneration between 3 to 8 and 15%. The sudden onset of acute pain, firmness, or neurologic deficit in an associated plexiform neurofibroma may be a sign of malignant degeneration. For this reason, a patient with a plexiform neurofibroma requires frequent follow-up for the above changes as well as an increase in growth

Incorrect Answers:
E. These are incorrect.

question: A mutation in the gene on chromosome 17 that encodes the protein neurofibromin increases the risk of which malignancy in patients?
explanation: Neurofibromin is the protein mutated in Neurofibromatosis Type 1 and its mechanism as a negative inducer of the Ras protein. The Ras protein is part of the mitogen associated protein kinase (MAPK) pathway that normally promotes cell survival and proliferation. Neurofibromin acts as a tumor suppressor by inhibiting the RAS signal. It is thought that neurofibromin plays a similar role in the expression of myeloid cell, and a mutation leading to a loss of its inhibitory function is one hypothesis explaining the relationship between NF-1 and Juvenile Myelomonocytic Leukemia (JMML). The triple relationship between NF-1, JMML, and Juvenile Xanthogranuloma (JXG) is also well established. Children with NF-1 have a higher risk of developing JMML at baseline (1/2000 – 1/5000) when compared to the general pediatric population. The presence of JXG’s, which in one study were found in 18% of children with a diagnosis of NF-1 by the age of 3, increases the risk for JMML 20- 30 fold. (A) (B) (C) (E) These are not found to be increased in patients diagnosed with Neurofibromatosis Type 1.

question: A young pregnant woman presents herself in your office with a proven pemphigoid gestationis diagnosis, which initial treatment is the most appropriate?

choices.A: High potency topical corticosteroids to minimize systemic exposure

correct_text: High potency topical corticosteroids to minimize systemic exposure
explanation:
High potency topical corticosteroids are appropriate for initial therapy of pemphigoid gestationis

question: For patients with cutaneous T-cell lymphoma (CTCL) what is the minimum body surface area percentage of erythematous skin required to assess a patient as having an erythrodermic (stages III-IVb) in the current NCCN CTCL staging?
explanation: T4 disease is defined as erythroderma with at least 80% of skin surface disease1. The 80% of body surface area (BSA) is required for a patient to be classified as having a cutaneous T-cell lymphoma that qualifies as stages III-IVb – Erythrodermic stage. Note that the definition of erythroderma in general usually refers to a 90% BSA in other conditions such as psoriasis or drug reactions. The term “erythroderma” was introduced in 1868 by Hebra to describe an exfoliative dermatitis involving more than 90% of the skin surface.

question: In cutaneous mastocytosis, many of the symptoms are caused by mast cell mediators. Some of them are preformed and others are newly formed. Out of the listed mediators below, all of the mediators are preformed except one, which one is it (newly formed)?
explanation: All of the listed mediators above are preformed except for leukotriene B41.

question: Vismodegib is a targeted therapy for metastatic basal cell carcinoma. It’s mechanism of action inhibits which molecule below?
explanation: The molecular target of Vismodegib in basal cell carcinoma is Smoothened. It is inhibited by the drug. PTCH is also involved in the Sonic Hedgehog pathway of the basal cell carcinoma but isn’t targeted by vismodegib. CDKN2A, ras and erk are involved in the melanoma pathway.

question: Cutaneous cysts have certain histopathological characteristics that allow their identification. Which cyst listed below corresponds to the following description? - Cyst lined with stratified squamous epithelium. - Cyst wall with abrupt keratinization and absent granular layer. - Cyst contains amorphous, dense and compact homogenized keratin. - Cyst is discrete with swollen and pale-staining cells. - Calcification is common within cyst.
explanation:
A pilar cyst has all the characteristics listed above: - Cyst lined with stratified squamous epithelium. - Cyst wall with abrupt keratinization and absent granular layer. - Cyst contains amorphous, dense and compact homogenized keratin. - Cyst is discrete with swollen and pale-staining cells. Calcification is common within cyst

Incorrect Answers:
C. An epidermoid cyst has a granular layer. It is also the case for a dermoid cyst and a vellus hair
D. A steatocystoma may contain sparse keratin and hair shafts but frequently appears empty

question: A Merkel Cell Carcinoma (MCC) diagnosis can be confirmed with immunostaining in histopathology. It stains as CK20 positive but also has other positive immunostains. Which of the following is usually also positive in MCC?
explanation: NSE often stains positive in MCC. CK7, S-100, TTF-1 and CEA are usually negative in MCC.

question: Which subtype of melanoma is most commonly diagnosed on facial skin of elderly patients (of more than 70 years old)?
explanation: Lentigo maligna melanoma represents a minority of cutaneous melanomas (« around 10% ») and it is diagnosed most frequently during the seventh decade of life. It occurs in chronically sundamaged skin, most frequently on the face.

question: The thickness of a melanoma is of an important prognostic value. In the event that a melanoma is not ulcerated, select the correct boundaries from which this measure is taken in millimiters out of the following choices:

choices.A: From the top of the stratum corneum layer to the deepest point of tumor penetration
choices.B: From the top of the granular cell layer to the deepest point of tumor penetration
choices.C: From the bottom of the granular cell layer to the deepest point of tumor penetration
choices.D: From the bottom of the spinous cell layer to the deepest point of tumor penetration
choices.E: From the top of the papillary dermis to the deepest point of tumor penetration

correct_text: From the top of the granular cell layer to the deepest point of tumor
explanation: penetration. Of the histologic features of a primary cutaneous melanoma, the Breslow depth or tumor thickness (depth of invasion) is the strongest predictor of survival. Melanoma thickness is measured in millimeters from the top of the granular cell layer of the epidermis (or the base of an ulcer) to the deepest point of tumor penetration, using an ocular micrometer.

question: What is the excision margin in centimeters for a primary cutaneous melanoma with a tumor thickness (Breslow) of less than 1.0 millimiter?
explanation: 0 cm. The excision margin in centimeters for a primary cutaneous melanoma with a tumor thickness (Breslow) of less than 1.0 millimeter is of 1.0 centimeter.

question: The diagnosis of a Sezary Syndrome is based on many criterias. One of them includes the assessment of the CD4/CD8 ratio in the T-cell population. That ratio should be of what order to support the diagnosis of a Sezary Syndrome?
explanation: Sezary syndrome is a subtype of cutaneous t cell lymphoma. It is characterized by the triad of erythroderma, circulating atypical T-cells (Sezary cells) and lymphadenopathy. The International Society for Cutaneous Lymphomas has established the following diagnostic criteria: 1) Erythroderma (erythema > 80% body surface area) 2) a clonal t cell receptor rearrangement in the blood 3) absolute Sezary cell count of atleast 1,000 cells/microL OR one of the following two criteria: - increased CD4+ T-cells with a CD4 to CD8 ratio of >10:1 (choice C) - increased CD4+ tcells with an abnormal phenotype (CD4+, CD7- or CD4+, CD26-)

question: A 65 year old male presents with the following skin eruption. Symptoms include occasionally flushing and diarrhea. He has a history of multiple fractures from falls. Rubbing skin lesions result in the development of an erythematous halo and wheal formation within several minutes. Dermal biopsy shows a proliferation of CD117 positive cells in the dermis. Which of the following should be avoided in this patient?
explanation:
This patient has mastocytosis (Darier's sign). Symptoms of flushing, gastrointestinal disease and potential bone involvement are concerning for systemic involvement. Further workup would require the measurement of a serum tryptase and bone biopsy. Biopsy of the skin shows an infiltrate of mast cells which stain positive for Giemsa, toluidine blue, tryptase, or CD117 (c-kit). The patient should be counseled to avoid Ppotential agents that can degranulate mast cells such as alcohol, aspirin, nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs, opiates, polymyxin B and systemic anesthetics should be avoided. Other triggers for mastocytosis include alcohol, spicy foods, heat, cold, temperature change, fatigue, exercise, venom, infection and mechanical irritation
