A. Treatment of mild or moderate otitis externa with an intact tympanic membrane generally
C. Topical aminoglycosides are not generally considered first-line agents (regardless of whether or
D. The duration of treatment is generally one week, with instructions to continue for an additional
E. Topical glucocorticoids (e.g., hydrocortisone, dexamethasone, prednisolone) decrease

question: A 12-year-old girl presents to the pediatric clinic with a recent onset of illness. Her symptoms include a sore throat and a fever. She also has an erythematous fine rough rash, which has worsened and spread over several days. Which of the following is true regarding this condition?

choices.A: The incubation period is usually short, lasting only a few days.
choices.B: Forchheimer spots, seen on the tongue and gums, are signs of this disease.
choices.C: The typical rash spares folds of skin, creating Pastia lines.
choices.D: This illness is mediated by pyogenic exotoxin E.
choices.E: The rash typically appears on the dorsal hands and spreads to other parts of the body.

correct_text: The incubation period is usually short, lasting only a few days
explanation:
The clinical vignette is describing scarlet fever, typified by a fine rough sandpaper-like rash. The incubation period is typically 1 to 4 days

Incorrect Answers:
B. Forchheimer spots are associated with rubella but are also seen in measles and scarlet fever.
C. The typical rash starts around the head and neck and spreads to other parts of the body.

question: A 17-year-old male presents with yellowish crusts on his bilateral axillary hairs. He relates that this symptom started about a month ago, soon after he started frequenting the gym. He has no other symptoms or skin complaints. What is the most likely causative organism?
explanation:
This is a young patient with Trichoymycosis Axillaris. The name of this condition is a misnomer in that the causative agent is a bacterium (Corynebacterium tenuis), not a fungus

Incorrect Answers:
A. Tinea verrucosum is a fungus that causes tinea capitis.
B. Corynebacterium minutissimum typically causes erythrasma.
C. Microsporum gypseum is a ground-dwelling dermatophyte.
E. Tinea schoenleinii is a fungus that causes tinea capitis (favus).

question: A 45-year-old man who lives in rural Pennsylvania visits the clinic for malaise, lethargy, and a very high fever. On physical exam, there is an ulcerative lesion with a raised border on the right dorsal hand. Right axillary lymphadenopathy is noted. A diagnosis of Tularemia is suspected. Which of the following statements is true of tularemia?

choices.A: The treatment of choice is erythromycin.
choices.B: Francisella tularensis, the causative organism, is a gram-negative coccobacillus.
choices.D: When cultured, the organism grows better on blood agar than on chocolate agar
choices.E: A conjugate vaccine is available for use in high-risk groups.

coccobacillus. F. tularensis is a gram-negative coccobacillus

C. F. tularensis is usually harbored by rabbits or rodents.

question: Which of the following is an RNA-based virus?
explanation: The Epstein-Barr virus is a DNA virus. The Togavirus group, of which Rubella virus is a member, includes RNA viruses. The human papillomavirus is a DNA virus and causes viral warts in the skin. The varicella-zoster virus is a DNA virus. The parvovirus group includes DNA viruses. Erythema infectiosum is caused by parvovirus B19.

question: A neonate is born with generalized violaceous purpura. Congenital CMV is suspected. Which of the following is correct concerning congenital CMV?

choices.A: It is the most common infectious cause of blindness in the United States.
choices.B: Microphthalmos is typical in infants with congenital CMV.
choices.C: Cataracts are typical in infants with congenital CMV.
choices.D: It is the most common infectious cause of intellectual disability in the United States.
choices.E: It occurs more often in pregnant women of lower socioeconomic status than in those of higher socioeconomic status.

correct_text: It is the most common infectious cause of intellectual disability in the United
explanation:
States. Congenital CMV is the most common infectious cause of intellectual disability in the United States

Incorrect Answers:
A. Congenital CMV is the most common infectious cause of deafness in the United States.
C. Cataracts and microphthalmos are not typical in infants with congenital CMV and strongly

question: A 36-year-old female presents with a new generalized morbilliform rash. Two weeks prior, she experienced a high fever, malaise, myalgias, and a sore throat and was given antibiotics by her primary care physician. Later, she developed the current morbilliform rash. What infection did she most likely suffer from, and what antibiotic did she receive?
explanation: Infectious mononucleosis can often mimic symptoms of bacterial pharyngitis (e.g. fever, sore throat, and malaise). While a minority of patients may present with a rash during EBV infection, almost all patients with active EBV infection given amoxicillin or ampicillin will develop a generalized pruritic morbilliform eruption.

question: A 1-year-old infant presents to the clinic with a high fever that started 5 days ago. He had a cough, rhinorrhea, and edematous eyelids. Over the last 24 hours, the infant has developed erythematous pink macules and papules with surrounding light rings or halos. The maculopapular rash started on the neck and trunk and spread to the face and extremities. The infant’s mother, who has been taking the infant’s temperature several times a day, states that the fever has improved since the rash appeared. Which of the following statements regarding this infant’s condition is true?

choices.A: The fever occurs gradually while the rash appears suddenly.
choices.B: The rash usually forms first on the limbs and then spreads to the trunk.
choices.C: Reactivation can occur in an adult who was infected as a child.
choices.D: It is most likely caused by HHV- 8.
choices.E: Cidofovir is indicated as the treatment of choice.

correct_text: Reactivation can occur in an adult who was infected as a child
explanation:
The clinical vignette describes an infant with exanthem subitum, also known as roseola infantum or sixth's disease. For infections caused by latent viruses , a person can be infected during childhood, and the virus remains dormant in the body for years. Reactivation can then occur later in adulthood

Incorrect Answers:
A. The fever and rash typically develop suddenly in exanthem subitum.
B. The rash in exanthem subitum typically occurs on the trunk first, then spreads to the limbs.
D. HHV-6 and HHV-7 are the causes of exanthem subitum.
E. Some success has been noted with the use of cidofovir, but it is not a recommended treatment

question: A 4-year-old child presents to the clinic with a recent viral type illness. In addition to his typical viral symptoms, he has erythematous papules and vesicles with a surrounding darker erythematous border on palms, soles, and perioral area. Which of the following is true of this infection?

choices.A: It has an incubation period of 1-2 weeks.
choices.B: The main transmission is by contact with oral and respiratory secretions.
choices.C: The fever and skin rash appear at the same time.
choices.D: Patients can expect to be affected for 3-4 weeks before resolution.
choices.E: It usually occurs during the summer and early autumn.

correct_text: It usually occurs during the summer and early autumn
explanation:
The clinical vignette describes a child with hand-foot-mouth disease (HFMD), most commonly caused by coxsackievirus A16 and enterovirus A71. HFMD usually occurs during the summer and early autumn (similar to other clinical syndromes caused by enteroviruses), but there can be outbreaks and sporadic cases of HFMD during the winter months. In tropical areas, cases of HFMD occur during the rainy season. Temperature and humidity affect the incidence of HFMD, and it has been postulated that the increasing global incidence of HFMD may be related to climate change

Incorrect Answers:
A. The incubation period for HFMD typically is three to five days, but has been reported to be as
B. The viruses that cause HFMD usually are transmitted from person to person by the fecal- oral

question: A 73-year-old presents to the clinic with recent-onset tinnitus and slight difficulty moving the right side of his face. He states that he developed painful “red bumps” around his right ear about three weeks ago. After that, "watery bumps" developed and crusted over. Which part of the nervous system is affected?
explanation:
Varicella-zoster virus (VZV) can result in a primary infection (chicken pox) and reactivation of latent virus (shingles). The former is characterized by vesicular lesions on an erythematous base, mostly on the face and trunk. The latter usually presents as painful, unilateral vesicles involving 1 or 2 contiguous dermatomes. Ramsay Hunt syndrome results from VZV reactivation in the geniculate ganglion. It classically manifests as a triad of ipsilateral facial paralysis, ear pain, and vesicles in the auditory canal or on the auricle. Other findings include altered taste perception, tongue lesions, hearing abnormalities, lacrimation, and vertigo. There is some evidence that combined treatment with valacyclovir (1 g three times per day for 7 to 10 days) and prednisone (1 mg/kg for five days, without a taper, is associated with better outcomes compared to no pharmacologic therapy or use of an antiviral alone. In patients with vertigo, tinnitus, or hearing loss, IV therapy can be initiated, transitioning to an oral antiviral agent when the lesions begin to crust. Herpes zoster can result in the transmission of varicella-zoster virus (VZV) from person to person by direct contact or by aerosolization of the virus from skin lesions to individuals who have not had varicella and have not received the varicella vaccine. If zoster is localized, transmission only occurs during the period the patient has an active rash

Incorrect Answers:
A. VZV infection of the cerebral arteries results in stroke syndromes.
B. VZV reactivation in the ophthalmic division of CN V is associated with herpes zoster
D. VZV infection of the aqueous humor can result in acute retinal necrosis, characterized by acute

question: Which of the following pairs of human herpesvirus (HHV) and related conditions is incorrectly matched?

choices.A: HHV-2 – oral herpes and genital herpes
choices.B: HHV-4 – infectious mononucleosis and Castleman disease

correct_text: HHV-4 – infectious mononucleosis and Castleman disease
explanation:
HHV-4 causes infectious mononucleosis and lymphoma, but HHV-8 causes Castleman disease. HHV-1 HSV-1 (herpes simplex viruses 1) HHV-2 HSV-2 (herpes simplex viruses 2) HHV-3 VZV (varicella zoster virus) HHV-4 EBV (Epstein–Barr virus) HHV-5 HCMV (human cytomegalovirus) HHV-6A and Roseola (exanthem subitum) HHV-6B HHV-7 Generally asymptomatic KSHV (Kaposi's sarcoma-associated HHV-8 herpesvirus), Castleman disease

Incorrect Answers:
A. HHV-2 causes oral and genital herpes (as does HHV-1).
C. HHV-6 causes exanthem subitum.
D. HHV-3 causes chicken pox and shingles.
E. HHV-8 causes Kaposi sarcoma (as well as Castleman disease).

question: Which of the following pairs of human papillomavirus (HPV) type and related condition is correct?
explanation:
Bowen's disease, a form of high-grade intraepithelial neoplasia, has both genital and extragenital forms. It can occur on the fingers, toes, palms, feet, and on the genital mucosa. Multiple HPV types have been isolated from these lesions, including HPV types 16, 18, 31, 32, 34, and others. HPV 16 is also commoly found in squamous intraepithelial lesions and/or carcinoma of the squamous cell oral cavity, vagina, vulva, cervix, anus, or penis

B. HPV 11 causes Bushke-Lowenstein tumor and anogenital lesions, recurrent respiratory
C. HPV 6 is associatyed with genital warts (condyloma acuminatum), and recurrent respiratory
D. HPV 1 causes palmoplantar warts, plantar and common warts.

question: A 58-year-old veterinarian reports to the ED for a single persistent papular lesion on his dorsal index finger. It started as a red bump that recently became whitish and is draining fluid. He has no other systemic symptoms. On further questioning, the veterinarian relates that he treats both household pets and larger farm animals. Which of the following is true concerning this patient’s condition?

choices.A: The causative agent is a poxvirus.
choices.B: The lesion described typically progresses through 3 phases.
choices.C: The virus can live for up to 2 weeks without a natural host.
choices.D: There is a human vaccine against the offending agent.
choices.E: The lesion typically lasts for 2 weeks.

correct_text: The causative agent is a poxvirus
explanation:
The virus that causes orf (contagious pustular dermatitis) is a poxvirus

Incorrect Answers:
B. Orf lesions progress through 6 phases: papular, targetoid, acute, regenerative, papillomatous,
D. There is a vaccine for livestock, but it can cause disease in humans.
E. The orf lesion typically persists for 7-10 weeks.

question: A 6-year-old child had a recent history of low-grade fevers and cold-like symptoms. A few days later, the patient developed an erythematous rash on the cheeks and an erythematous lacy rash on the torso and proximal extremities. Of note, a peripheral blood smear from several years prior showed unusually small erythrocytes lacking normal central pallor. What condition must the practitioner be aware of as a risk in this patient?
explanation: This is a child with parvovirus B19 infection (fifth disease). Parvovirus B19 infection, also known as fifth disease or erythema infectiosum, is a common viral illness that primarily affects children between the ages of 5 and 15. It typically begins with mild, nonspecific symptoms such as lowgrade fever, headache, and malaise, followed by the appearance of a characteristic "slapped cheek" facial rash and a lacy, reticular rash on the trunk and limbs. The illness is usually selflimited in healthy children, but in certain populations, such as individuals with hemolytic anemia, pregnant women, or immunocompromised patients, it can lead to complications like transient aplastic crisis, hydrops fetalis, or prolonged anemia. The virus spreads through respiratory droplets and has an incubation period of 4 to 14 days. Most individuals develop lifelong immunity after infection. The disease is generally not harmful long-term in otherwise healthy patients. However, patients with chronic anemic states such as sickle cell anemia or hereditary spherocytosis (as in this case) are at risk for aplastic crisis.

question: A 6-year-old child who recently immigrated from Eastern Europe presents with a recent mild flulike illness and a rash consisting of pink petechiae and macules. Small erythematous petechiae are noted on the soft palate. Per the child’s mother, the rash first appeared on the face and spread. As it spread to the body, the rash on the face resolved. What type of virus is the causative agent of this condition?
explanation: The clinical vignette describes a child with rubella, which typically begins with mild flu-like symptoms and a pink to an erythematous petechial/macular rash that begins on the face, clearing as it spreads to other parts of the body. The rash resolves after a few days. The illness is caused by the rubella virus. Rubella virus is the only recognized member of the family Matonaviridae, genus Rubivirus. Its genomic structure and replication process are similar to those of the alphaviruses. In contrast to the alphaviruses, however, humans are the only natural host, though experimental rubella virus infection may be induced in several vertebrate animals. Prior to 2018 rubella was considered part of the togavirus family. The virus is spread by the respiratory route. After an incubation period of 1 week, the flu- like symptoms and rash begin. Forchheimer’s sign consists of small red macules and petechiae that appear on the soft palate. These do not occur in all cases of rubella. Incorrect answers: A. Poxviruses are brick or oval-shaped viruses with large double-stranded DNA genomes. Poxviruses exist throughout the world and cause disease in humans and many other types of animals. Poxvirus infections typically result in the formation of lesions, skin nodules, or disseminated rash. Infection in humans usually occurs due to contact with contaminated animals, people, or materials. While some poxviruses, such as smallpox (variola virus), no longer exist in nature, other poxviruses can still cause disease. These include the monkeypox virus, orf virus, molluscum contagiosum, and others. B. Human parvovirus B19 has multiple different presentations, which are influenced by the infected individual's age and hematologic and immunologic status. The five well- established syndromes associated with parvovirus B19 are: Fifth disease/erythema infectiosum in children, arthropathy, transient aplastic crisis in those with chronic hemolytic disorders, fetal infection leading to non-immune hydrops fetalis, (or intrauterine fetal death, miscarriage, or cardiomyopathy), and pure red blood cell aplasia in immunocompromised individuals (see 'Chronic infection in immunosuppressed hosts' below) D. The orthomyxoviruses (influenza viruses) constitute the genus Orthomyxovirus, which consists of three types (species): A, B, and C. These viruses cause influenza, an acute respiratory disease with prominent systemic symptoms. Pneumonia may develop as a complication and may be fatal, particularly in elderly persons with underlying chronic disease. Type A viruses cause periodic worldwide epidemics (pandemics); both types A and B cause recurring regional and local epidemics. Influenza epidemics have been recorded throughout history. In temperate climates, the epidemics typically occur in the winter and cause considerable morbidity in all age groups. An epidemic with associated mortality has occurred in most of the past 100 years. The worst of these was the 1918 pandemic, which caused about 20 million deaths worldwide and about 500,000 deaths in the United States. E. The picornaviruses are small (22 to 30 nm) nonenveloped, singlestranded RNA viruses with cubic symmetry. The virus capsid is composed of 60 protein subunits, each consisting of four poly-peptides VP1–VP4. Because they contain no essential lipids, they are ether resistant. They replicate in the cytoplasm. The picornaviruses that affect humans are the enteroviruses, found primarily in the gut; the rhinoviruses, found in the upper respiratory tract and 1327 Board Vitals · Diseases hepatovirus (Hepatitis A virus) in the intestine and liver. Subclinical infections with the picornaviruses are common.

question: Which of the following is correct regarding smallpox infection?

choices.B: The rash is worse on the face and extremities than on the trunk and consists of lesions at the same stage of development.
choices.C: The smallpox virus is a parapoxvirus.
choices.D: Guarnieri bodies seen on histopathology are diagnostic of smallpox.
choices.E: The most common complications of smallpox involve the CNS.

correct_text: The rash is worse on the face and extremities than on the trunk and consists
explanation:
of lesions at the same stage of development. The characteristic rash of smallpox consists of papules, vesicles, and pustules. Many lesions will have central umbilications and will continue to crust over a 2-week period. Unlike Varicella, the lesions are all in the same stage of development. Lesions develop uniformly throughout the disease and progress from macules to papules to vesicles over the course of 4 to 5 days. Within another 1 to 2 days, the vesicles often umbilicate and evolve to pustules that are round, tense, firm to the touch, and deep-seated within the dermis

Incorrect Answers:
A. The incubation period of smallpox is approximately 12 days.
D. Guarnieri bodies are seen in all poxvirus infections and are not specific to smallpox.
E. The most common complications of smallpox involve the respiratory system.

question: A 9-year-old girl presents to the pediatrician with a recent flu-like illness. Just after the flu-like illness started, a disseminated rash of erythematous papules began. Some of the papules began to turn into vesicles as more papules arose. Eventually, some of the vesicles began to crust over, but many are currently either erythematous papules or vesicles. Which of the following is true of this illness?

choices.A: Infection of a pregnant woman from 5 days before to 2 days after delivery can cause congenital varicella syndrome.
choices.B: An individual lesion will evolve from macule to papule to vesicle to crust over 5 days.
choices.C: The highest risk of congenital varicella syndrome occurs within the first 20 weeks of pregnancy.
choices.D: The varicella vaccine consists of heat-killed virus.
choices.E: Disseminated herpes zoster is defined as 5 lesions outside an affected dermatome.

correct_text: The highest risk of congenital varicella syndrome occurs within the first 20
explanation:
weeks of pregnancy. This patient has varicella-zoster virus (VZV) infection. The highest risk of congenital varicella syndrome occurs within the first 20 weeks of pregnancy. The syndrome consists of hypoplastic limbs, ocular abnormalities, and CNS abnormalities

Incorrect Answers:
E. Twenty or more lesions outside the affected dermatome is diagnostic of disseminated herpes

question: A 43-year-old Brazilian immigrant presents with skin lesions shown in the image below. A diagnosis of Hansen disease (leprosy) is suspected. Which of the following is the preferred way to confirm the diagnosis?
explanation:
Leprosy or Hansen disease is seen infrequently in the United States, with most new cases observed among immigrants. It results from infection with Mycobacterium leprae or Mycobacterium lepromatosis, with skin and peripheral nerve involvement seen most often. Transmission is felt to occur via the respiratory route following close contact with an affected individual. Leprosy is generally classified based on skin, neurologic, and biopsy findings. The disease spectrum is shaped by an individual's immune response and the number of acid-fast bacilli found in the dermis. A full-thickness skin biopsy should be obtained to help evaluate skin lesions suggestive of leprosy. Specialized stains can help substantiate the diagnosis; culture of the biopsy tissue can also help exclude alternative mycobacterial infections

Incorrect Answers:
A. A culture of biopsy tissue can exclude alternative infectious etiologies, but a blood culture is not
C. The lepromin test is performed by injecting autoclaved M. leprae into the skin and monitoring for
D. While serologic tests for M. leprae exist, they are not sufficiently sensitive to diagnose leprosy.
E. Skin smears of collected dermal fluid are no longer used to diagnose leprosy.

question: A 36-year-old pet store owner presents with a nodule on the dorsum of her hand. She states that the lesion started as a red bump. The patient recalls cutting her hand recently while cleaning the pet store fish tanks. On physical exam, you note an erythematous, verrucous, and indurated plaque on the dorsum of her hand, with a small erythematous indurated patch on her forearm. Which is the best treatment for this condition?
explanation:
This patient's presentation is highly suggestive of Mycobacterium marinum infection. M marinum is aquatic and found in both fresh and salt water. The portal of entry in most individuals is at the site of a minor abrasion, laceration, or puncture/bite. Those putting their hand into a contaminated fish tank can develop a granuloma (fish tank granuloma). In vitro susceptibility testing should be used to guide the treatment of M. marinum. It is usually susceptible to clarithromycin, ethambutol, rifampin, and trimethoprim- sulfamethoxazole. Treatment duration is variable but usually should be for at least 4-6 weeks. Surgical debridement may be required if there is a poor response to drug therapy

Incorrect Answers:
B. While isoniazid is commonly used as part of a multidrug regimen for Mycobacterium
C. While pyrazinamide is commonly used as part of a multidrug regimen for Mycobacterium
D. While streptomycin may be used to treat multidrug-resistant Mycobacterium tuberculosis,
E. While ciprofloxacin may be used for the treatment of multidrug-resistant Mycobacterium

question: A 45-year-old male presents to the ED with a persistent rash on his palms and soles. The lesions are tan/pink colored macules. He also has a small erythematous and white plaque on his right lateral tongue. A diagnosis of syphilis is suspected. Which of the following forms of syphilis is incorrectly matched with its time course after primary infection?
explanation:
Tertiary syphilis – 18 weeks to 15 years Syphilis can progress through several stages if untreated or improperly treated. Primary syphilis typically manifests as a syphilitic chancre 10 to 90 days after primary infection. Secondary syphilis manifests 4 to 10 weeks after the appearance of a primary syphilitic chancre, with the symptoms described in the clinical vignette (which below would be calculated as 5 to 23 weeks in reference to primary infection). Tertiary syphilis will present with gumma, cardiac sequelae, and neural sequelae. Tertiary syphilis typically appears anywhere from 2 to 15 years, not as early as 18 weeks. Latent forms are defined by the 1- year post-primary infection mark: early latent is under 1 year, and late latent is over 1 year. Latent syphilis is defined by lack of symptoms but the presence of positive serology

Incorrect Answers:
D. These stages are all correctly matched to their timeline.

question: A 38-year-old male presents with complaints of a spot on his penis that has been there for a few weeks. On physical exam, there is a painless soft erosion on the dorsal penis. There is also bilateral fluctuant inguinal lymphadenopathy with the presence of a groove sign. A clinical diagnosis of lymphogranuloma venereum is made. Which of the following genital ulcerative diseases is incorrectly matched with an appropriate treatment?
explanation:
Lymphogranuloma venerum caused by C. trachomatis (serovars L1, L2, and L3) is treated with Doxycycline, not penicillin

Incorrect Answers:
A. A chancroid caused by H. ducreyi is typically treated with azithromycin, ceftriaxone,
B. Primary syphilis caused by T. pallidum is treated with penicillin.
C. Granuloma Inguinale caused by Klebsiella granulomatis is treated with bactrim, doxycycline,
E. Genital herpes simplex infection is treated with acyclovir or valacyclovir.

question: A 2-year-old patient who has just moved to the United States presents for his first checkup. He was delivered at home, and the mother noted that the newborn was "quite small" and had some "snuffles." The possibility of congenital syphilis is considered. Which of the following is unlikely to occur in this patient?
explanation: Congenital syphilis can be divided into early and late stages. The early stage occurs prior to 2 years of age and consists of low birth weight, saw-toothing of bone metaphyses, rhinitis (snuffles), epitrochlear lymphadenopathy, and neurosyphilis. The late stage after 2 years of age may present with mulberry molars, peg teeth, dactylitis, high arched palate, saber shins, saddle nose, enlargement of medial clavicle, retinitis, gummas of palate and nose, and eighth nerve deafness, and keratitis. Eighth nerve deafness, keratitis, and peg teeth make up Hutchinson’s triad. Trigeminal nerve palsy is not a typical manifestation of late congenital syphilis.

question: Numerous tests to detect syphilis have been developed. Which of the following serologic tests is paired with the proper corresponding description?

choices.A: VDRL – First detects infection about 3 months after infection and usually becomes nonreactive after treatment
choices.B: RPR – Stays reactive through late-stage syphilis and after treatment
choices.C: ELISA – Has the lowest specificity of the serologic tests
choices.E: Fluorescent treponemal antibody absorption (FTA-ABS) – Becomes positive 3 weeks after infection and is the most sensitive serologic test for primary syphilis

correct_text: Fluorescent treponemal antibody absorption (FTA-ABS) – Becomes positive
explanation:
3 weeks after infection and is the most sensitive serologic test for primary syphilis. FTA-ABS becomes positive 3 weeks after an individual is infected, and it is the most sensitive serologic test of those listed for detecting primary syphilis

Incorrect Answers:
B. RPR, like VDRL, generally (but not always) becomes non-reactive in late-stage syphilis and in
D. MHA-TP remains reactive after treatment, as does FTA-ABS.

question: A 46-year-old HIV-positive woman presents with tan-to-pink palmar macules. On further questioning, she reports a painless ulcer on her labia a few months ago. The lesion resolved, and she thought nothing more of it. The practitioner is worried that the patient may have secondary syphilis. Which of the following statements is correct concerning syphilis treatment?

choices.A: Azithromycin is the preferred alternative agent if the patient has a true allergy to the firstline agent.
choices.B: The preferred treatment for neurosyphilis is penicillin G benzathine (2.4 million units IM) once a week for three weeks.
choices.C: A twofold (50%) decline in the nontreponemal titer is considered an adequate serologic response following treatment.
choices.D: The treatment is the same for all stages of early syphilis (primary, secondary, and early latent syphilis).
choices.E: If the timing of an infection is not known, a person is presumed to have early latent syphilis.

correct_text: The treatment is the same for all stages of early syphilis (primary, secondary,
explanation: and early latent syphilis). Patients with neurologic, ocular, or otic manifestations of early syphilis require intravenous (IV) therapy. For those without such manifestations, a single dose of penicillin G benzathine (2.4 million units intramuscularly) is standard therapy. The treatment s the same for all stages of early syphilis (primary, secondary, and early latent syphilis). Primary syphilis usually consists of a painless chancre at the site of inoculation accompanied by regional adenopathy. Approximately 25% of individuals with an untreated primary infection will develop a systemic illness that represents secondary syphilis. Clinical manifestations include a disseminated rash, condylomata lata, lymphadenopathy, alopecia, and/or hepatitis. Early latent syphilis refers to the period when a patient is infected with T. pallidum, as demonstrated by serologic testing, but has no symptoms. Early latent syphilis by definition occurs within the first year of initial infection. Patients diagnosed with early latent syphilis should be considered to be potentially infectious

question: An 8-year-old male immigrant from South America presents with several growths on his right arm. His mother relates that the growths started as a painless papule that turned into a large nodule. Several smaller identical nodules have appeared around it. A diagnosis of yaws is considered most likely. Which of the following is responsible for the suspected diagnosis.
explanation:
T. pallidum pertenue causes yaws

Incorrect Answers:
A. T. pallidum denticola is responsible for periodontitis. Bejel is caused by T. pallidum endemicum.
C. T. pallidum pallidum causes syphilis.
D. T. pallidum carateum causes pinta.

question: A 42-year-old man who recently immigrated from Africa presents to the clinic with a 4 cm ulcer on his right forearm. The erythematous/yellow thin ulcer has an erythematous raised border. He remembers being bitten by "some type of fly" about 2 months ago. A biopsy is performed, and a rod- shaped kinetoplast is observed within the amastigote. Which of the following is incorrect concerning this condition?

choices.A: The vectors are Phlebotomus (old world) and Lutzomyia (new world).
choices.B: The causative organism is cultured on Novy-MacNeal-Nicolle medium.
choices.D: New-world causative organisms include L. tropica, L. major, and L. infantum.
choices.E: L. donovani is an old-world organism that causes the visceral form of the disease.

correct_text: New-world causative organisms include L. tropica, L. major, and L. infantum
explanation:
tropica, L. major, and L. infantum. L. tropica, L. major, and L. infantum (along with L. aethiopica and L. donovani) are all old- world Leishmania. Specimens used in diagnosis should be collected from lesions that are active and ulcerative that have no evidence of secondary infection. The Leishmania amastigote is an oval to round organism that is 1.5 to 4.0 microns in diameter with a cell membrane, cytoplasm, internal nucleus, and rod-shaped kinetoplast containing mitochondrial DNA. On Giemsa staining, the kinetoplast appears red or violet

Incorrect Answers:
A. The sandfly species that serve as vectors for Leishmaniasis are Phlebotomus (old world) and
C. Pentavalent antimony is the treatment of choice for Leishmaniasis.

question: Which of the following is incorrect regarding trypanosomiasis?

choices.A: T. brucei rhodensiense is the causative agent in West Africa.
choices.B: T. cruzi is the causative agent in South and Central America.
choices.C: In Africa, the disease vector is the tsetse fly.
choices.D: In the African form, Winterbottom's sign is typically present
choices.E: In the South American form, Romana's sign can be present.

correct_text: T. brucei rhodensiense is the causative agent in West Africa
explanation:
brucei rhodensiense is the causative agent in West Africa. This is the false statement. T. brucei gambiense is the West African form, and T. brucei rhodesiense is the East African form

Incorrect Answers:
D. Winterbottom's sign is a symptom of the African form. It consists of posterior cervical
E. Romana's sign may be part of the South American form. It consists of unilateral eyelid edema

question: A 36-year-old African immigrant visits the ED with unilateral swelling in his right leg. He is a recent immigrant and is from an area of Africa where filariasis is common. This is the suspected diagnosis, and the appropriate workup is initiated. In general, which of the following mosquito species is not a likely vector for lymphatic filariasis?
explanation: Lymphatic filariasis is a disease marked by lymphatic block due to parasitic organisms that causes lymphedema and elephantiasis. The typical causative organisms are Brugia malayi, Brugia timori, and Wuchereria bancrofti. Mosquitos are the primary vectors, namely Aedes, Mansonia, Culex, and Anopheles types. Psorophora does not typically transmit filariasis. Other types of filariasis include subcutaneous and serous cavity filariasis.

question: A 48-year-old African immigrant presents to the clinic with a flat-topped, thin nodular ulceration on his dorsal foot. A thin, white, thread-like object can be seen emerging from the center of the nodular ulceration. The man notes recent intense burning pain in that leg. Which of the following is correct concerning this condition?

choices.A: This condition is caused by Onchocerca volvulus, transmitted by the blackfly, and is a leading cause of blindness in endemic regions.
choices.B: This condition is caused by Loa loa, transmitted by the Chrysops fly, and is treated with diethylcarbamazine
choices.C: This condition is caused by Wuchereria bancrofti, transmitted by mosquitoes, and can lead to lymphatic filariasis and elephantiasis
choices.D: This condition is caused by Dracunculus medinensis, transmitted by ingestion of contaminated water containing copepods, and treatment involves slow extraction of the worm

correct_text: This condition is caused by Dracunculus medinensis, transmitted by
explanation: ingestion of contaminated water containing copepods, and treatment involves slow extraction of the worm. This man’s presentation is classic for Dracunculus medinensis, or Guinea worm disease. Humans become infected by drinking contaminated water that contains copepods (tiny freshwater crustaceans) harboring the larvae. After ingestion, the larvae mature into adult worms, and gravid females migrate through the subcutaneous tissue until they reach the skin, often of the lower extremities. The female worm produces a painful blister and eventually emerges from the skin, producing the striking finding of a long, white, thread-like worm protruding from an ulcer. Patients describe intense burning pain at the site. Treatment is unique compared to most parasitic infections: the worm must be removed slowly and carefully by winding it around a stick or gauze over several days to weeks. There is no widely available curative drug therapy. Public health campaigns have reduced the annual incidence to only a handful of cases, mostly in sub-Saharan Africa

question: Which of the following parasites is incorrectly matched with the disease it causes and the typical treatment offered to affected patients in the United States?
explanation:
This is incorrectly matched. Lymphatic filariasis is caused by B. malayi, B. timori, or W. bancrofti. M. perstans causes serous cavity filariasis. Diethylcarbamazine is the treatment of choice for lymphatic filariasis. There is no current consensus on treatment for M. perstans serous cavity filariasis, but combination drug therapies are being investigated

Incorrect Answers:
B. Loa Loa causes Loiasis. Diethylcarbamazine is the treatment of choice.
C. Strongyloidiasis is caused by S. stercoralis. The treatment of choice is ivermectin.
D. Onchocerciasis is caused by O. volvulus. The treatment of choice is ivermectin.

question: A 22-year-old female patient presents to the clinic for a new onset rash that appeared fairly suddenly and is very pruritic. On physical exam, there are numerous erythematous papules scattered on the buttocks and bilateral breasts. Other pertinent history includes a recent trip to the beaches of the Florida coast, where she spent a lot of time in the water. She is wondering if something from the trip caused this odd rash and asks if she got bed bugs from the hotel room where she stayed. What is the correct response to her question?

choices.A: "No, this is not bed bugs. It is something called swimmer's itch."
choices.B: "Yes, this is likely bed bugs. Be sure to wash all your clothing and bed linens."
choices.C: "No, this is not bed bugs. However, it is caused by an organism from the family Schistosomatidae."
choices.D: "Yes, this is likely bed bugs. I will prescribe you an oral anti-itch medication and a topical steroid. You should also call the hotel to alert them."
choices.E: "No, this is not bed bugs. I will prescribe you an oral anti-itch medication and a topical steroid."

correct_text: "No, this is not bed bugs. I will prescribe you an oral anti-itch medication and
explanation: a topical steroid.". I will prescribe you an oral anti-itch medication and a topical steroid." The patient in this vignette has seabather's eruption, which is caused by the larvae of Edwardsiella lineata and Linuche unguiculata. While in saltwater, swimmers will encounter the larvae, which become trapped under swimming garments. The larvae have nematocysts that are automatically discharged when dried or when they encounter fresh water. This causes a rash in the typical "bathing suit" distribution. Swimmer's itch is typically caused by the freshwater forms of Schistomomatidae flatworms (saltwater forms do exist, however). Lesions appear on uncovered skin. There is no special treatment for seabather's eruption or swimmer's itch other than symptomatic relief.

question: Which of the following Rickettsial diseases is incorrectly matched with its causative organism?

choices.A: Rocky Mountain spotted fever - Rickettsia rickettsii

explanation:
Scrub typhus is caused by Orientia tsutsugamushi. R. akari causes Rickettsialpox

Incorrect Answers:
B. Epidemic typhus is caused by R. prowazekii. Endemic typhus is caused by R. typhi.

question: Which of the following tickborne diseases is matched improperly with its corresponding tick vector?

choices.C: Southern tick-associated rash illness (STARI) - Ambylomma americanum

explanation:
Tularemia is caused by Francisella tularensis. It is spread to humans by the dog tick (Dermacentor variabilis), the wood tick (Dermacentor andersoni), and the lone star tick (Amblyomma americanum). Rhipicephalus sangunineus is known to spread Rocky Mountain spotted fever

Incorrect Answers:
A. Lyme disease is caused by Borrelia burgdorferi and transmitted by Ixodes scapularis.
B. Ehrlichiosis is caused by Ehrlichia chaffeensis transmitted by Ambylomma americanum.
E. Babesiosis caused by Babesia microti is spread by Ixodes scapularis.

question: A 28-year-old male graduate student reports with severe malaise and a cough. He is experiencing night sweats and has had minor weight loss. He recently visited caves in Mexico to collect specimens for his lab work. His illness started about 3 days after his return. A diagnosis of histoplasmosis is suspected. Of the following, which fungus is correctly matched with its histological description?

choices.A: Histoplasma capsulatum - intracellular 2-4 micrometer budding or singular cells with a capsule
choices.B: Blastomyces dermatitidis - granuloma reaction with 8-15 micrometer broad-based budding cells
choices.D: Paracoccidioides brasiliensis - 20-80 micrometer round cells with multiple buds attached to a main cell through a thin connection
choices.E: Aspergillosis sp. - hyphae branching at 90-degree angles with small round conidia

correct_text: Blastomyces dermatitidis - granuloma reaction with 8-15 micrometer broadbased budding cells
explanation:
Blastomyces dermatitidis demonstrates a granuloma reaction with 8-15 micrometer broad- based budding cells

Incorrect Answers:
C. Coccidioides immitis consists of 20-80 micrometer spherules.
E. Aspergillosis sp. demonstrates hyphae branching at 45-degree angles with small round conidia.

question: A 34-year-old female was seen in the hospital by the dermatology service for an evaluation of erythematous targetoid lesions, necrotic nodules, and abscesses. A biopsy showed 45-degree branching hyphae. A fungal culture grew Fusarium. Which of the following is incorrect concerning invasive Fusarium?

choices.A: Fusarium is seen more often in samples of air than Aspergillus.
choices.B: Fusarium is more likely to occur in patients with hematologic malignancies.
choices.C: Fusarium is the most common fungal infection in burn patients.
choices.D: Appropriate treatment for invasive infections includes amphotericin B and/or voriconazole (Vfend).
choices.E: Fatality reaches approximately 50% in patients with prolonged severe neutropenia.

correct_text: Fatality reaches approximately 50% in patients with prolonged severe
explanation:
neutropenia. Fatality of patients with severe prolonged neutropenia reaches approximately 100%. The other options all represent correct statements

Incorrect Answers:
B. Patients with heme malignancies are predisposed to Fusariosis. Other groups include those
C. This is the most common fungal infection in burn patients. Aspergillosis is the second most

question: An intubated patient in the intensive care unit has a suspected hypersensitivity reaction. The patient’s family reveals that the patient has a known penicillin allergy. While reviewing the list of the patient’s current medications, the clinician notes that no penicillins are listed, but the patient is receiving a cephalosporin. Which of the following is a fourth-generation cephalosporin?
explanation:
Cefepime is a fourth-generation cephalosporin. Fifteen percent of people with penicillin allergy have cross-reactivity to cephalosporins. Also, 2% of people with a cephalosporin allergy have cross-reactivity to penicillins. Cephalosporins and penicillins may cause similar cutaneous findings, including morbilliform eruption, urticaria, drug fever, eosinophilia, and acute generalized exanthematous pustulosis

Incorrect Answers:
B. Cephalexin is a first-generation cephalosporin, along with cefadroxil and cefazolin.
C. Cefaclor is a second-generation cephalosporin along with cefprozil, cefuroxime axetil,
D. Cefotaxime is a third-generation cephalosporin along with cefixime, cefdinir, cefpodoxime,
E. Ceftriaxone is a third-generation cephalosporin.

question: A 26-year-old male recently returned from traveling to Brazil. Once home, the patient developed skin lesions and diarrhea. Which of the following organisms likely causes diarrhea in association with the cutaneous findings pictured below?
explanation:
Strongyloides stercoralis causes a distinctive form of cutaneous larva migrans that is recognized as larva currens. Larva currens presents with serpiginous erythematous papules and subsequent hypopigmentation on the buttocks, upper thighs, and lower abdomen. Perianal involvement or hematogenous spread in an immunosuppressed patient can lead to intestinal infestation resulting in diarrhea

Incorrect Answers:
A. S. pyogenes infection can cause skin manifestations such as impetigo and ecthyma, but it is not
B. epidermidis is normal skin flora that is unlikely to cause a skin infection in an immunocompetent
C. In echinococcosis, the liver and lungs are the major sites of involvement, with the development
D. Skin manifestations of onchocerciasis include subcutaneous nodules containing adult worms,

question: A 61-year-old presents to the emergency department with a 5 to 6-day history of persistent fever, headache, and abdominal pain following a tick bite. The patient notes no recent travel. On physical examination, his temperature is 101.4°F, and a blanching rash with macules involving the wrists and ankles is noted. Basic laboratory testing is significant for a platelet count of 93K. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
This patient's presentation is highly suggestive of Rocky Mountain spotted fever (RMSF). RMSF is a tickborne illness caused by Rickettsia rickettsii. In the Eastern, Central, and Pacific coastal United States, it is most often transmitted by Dermacentor variabilis, the American dog tick. Dermacentor andersoni, the Rocky Mountain wood tick, is the most frequent vector in the Western United States. Patients with RMSF often experience fever, headache, myalgias, and arthralgias early after infection. A rash usually develops after 3-5 days, starting on a patient's wrists and ankles. The rash is usually blanching and erythematous with macules that can evolve into petechiae. While thrombocytopenia is common, disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC) is rare

Incorrect Answers:
A. Patients with meningococcal infection (meningococcemia) typically present with sudden onset
B. Measles is a highly contagious viral illness that begins with a prodrome of fever, malaise,

question: A 14-year-old girl presents with a 3-year history of yellow-crusted plaques and extensive hair loss. There is peripheral scutula formation as well. KOH examination of the hairs reveals infected hair filled with hyphae producing bubbles of gas. What is the most likely cause of this condition?
explanation: Scutula formation, chronicity, and extensive hair loss are clinically consistent with tinea favosa (favus), a severe form of tinea capitis caused by T. schoenleinii. In tinea favosa, upon exposure to KOH, there is bubbling of KOH through the air spaces between hyphae elements. This severe form of hair infection is not typical of other causes of tinea capitis such as T. tonsurans and M. canis. Rarely, favus is caused by Trichophyton violaceum, Trichophyton mentagrophytes var quinckeanum, or Microsporum gypseum. None of the other options have been reported to cause favus.

question: Which of the following diseases has been associated with polyomavirus?

question: A young patient with diffusely distributed, flat, verrucous papules is found to have a mutation in the EVER1 gene. How did the patient most likely acquire this gene mutation?
explanation:
This patient most likely has Epidermodysplasia verruciformis (EV), a rare autosomal recessive skin disease that is characterized by an abnormally high susceptibility to infection with certain types of human papillomavirus (HPV) called EV-HPV. The requires a skin biopsy and is based upon the histologic features and demonstration of HPV in a skin lesion. Patients have disseminated polymorphic lesions, including flat-topped, wart-like papules and pityriasis versicolor-like macules, predominantly on skin areas exposed to sunlight. Patients with EV have an increased risk of developing nonmelanoma skin cancers (mainly cutaneous squamous cell carcinomas and Bowentype carcinoma at an early age. There are no curative therapies for EV, and the predominant treatment is with systemic retinoids, but the benefit is often only transient. Patients should be educated to adopt strict sun protection measures to prevent the development of nonmelanoma skin cancer, and undergo lifelong regular skin examination for early diagnosis and treatment of precancerous skin lesions and skin cancer

Incorrect Answers:
A. Although susceptible to HPV, the virus does not cause EV.
B. EV is not acquired through a post-zygotic mutation, it is an inherited disorder.

question: A 9-year-old female is admitted for recurrent staphylococcal cellulitis. On exam the patient is noted to have numerous verrucous papules and plaques, including perianally. Lab workup reveals low levels of IgG. Which of these is likely to be true for this patient?

question: A 57-year-old presents with weight loss, myalgias, and tender erythematous nodules on the extremities. A biopsy reveals necrotizing vasculitis involving medium-sized arteries. Which other clinical finding may be found in this patient?
explanation:
This presentation suggests polyarteritis nodosa (PAN)—a systemic necrotizing vasculitis involving medium-sized arteries. Patients with PAN commonly present with systemic symptoms (e.g., fatigue, weight loss, weakness, fever, and arthralgias) and signs (e.g., skin lesions, hypertension, renal insufficiency, abdominal pain, and neurologic dysfunction). Skin lesions with PAN can include purpura, livedo reticularis, and ulcers. They may be focal or diffuse but usually are most pronounced over the lower extremities. While the pathogenesis of PAN is not well understood, immune complexes (especially those associated with hepatitis B) may play a role. Accordingly, testing for hepatitis B infection (by serum antibody or antigen serology) is recommended. The American College of Rheumatology has established ten criteria for PAN in a patient with vasculitis. The presence of at least three criteria in a patient with documented vasculitis has a sensitivity and specificity of 82% and 87%, respectively. These include: Unexplained weight loss greater than 4 kg Livedo reticularis Testicular pain or tenderness Myalgias, weakness of muscles, tenderness of leg muscles, or polyneuropathy Mononeuropathy or polyneuropathy New-onset diastolic blood pressure >90 mm Hg Elevated levels of serum blood urea nitrogen (BUN) (>40 mg/dL) or creatinine (>1.5 mg/dL) Evidence of hepatitis B virus infection by serum antibody or antigen serology Characteristic arteriographic abnormalities not resulting from a noninflammatory disease process Biopsy of a small- or medium-sized artery containing polymorphonuclear cells

B. This is not one of the American College of Rheumatology criteria for the classification of PAN in
D. This is not one of the American College of Rheumatology criteria for the classification of PAN in
E. This is not one of the American College of Rheumatology criteria for the classification of PAN in

question: A 23-year-old woman presents with a sudden, severe acneiform eruption involving her face that began shortly after an elective ambulatory surgical procedure. She has a history of long-term oral antibiotic therapy for acne vulgaris. Prior treatment with topical tretinoin cream and oral ciprofloxacin has been ineffective. Physical examination reveals numerous pustules and inflammatory papules in a follicular distribution. A culture of a pustule grows Klebsiella species. What is the most appropriate next step in management?
explanation:
This patient has gram-negative folliculitis, a condition classically associated with long-term oral antibiotic therapy for acne vulgaris. Prolonged antibiotic use suppresses normal gram- positive skin and nasal flora, allowing overgrowth of gram-negative organisms within hair follicles. Common causative organisms include Klebsiella species, Escherichia coli, Enterobacter, Proteus, and Pseudomonas aeruginosa. P. aeruginosa is most commonly associated with hot tub folliculitis, whereas Klebsiella and other enteric organisms are typical in antibiotic- associated cases. Oral isotretinoin is the treatment of choice for recalcitrant gram-negative folliculitis, particularly when the condition is unresponsive to antibiotic therapy (e.g., trimethoprim– sulfamethoxazole, ampicillin, ciprofloxacin). Isotretinoin reduces sebaceous gland size and sebum production, eliminating the follicular environment that supports gram-negative bacterial growth and leading to durable remission. Because isotretinoin is highly teratogenic, initiation requires pregnancy testing (β-hCG) in patients of childbearing potential, along with baseline laboratory studies (e.g., liver transaminases and triglycerides), informed consent, and enrollment in the iPLEDGE Risk Evaluation and Mitigation Strategy (REMS) program prior to treatment

Incorrect Answers:
A. Given the positive culture and a clinical presentation consistent with gram-negative folliculitis, a
B. Minocycline has a broad spectrum of activity against gram-positive and gram-negative
C. Although benzoyl peroxide does have antibacterial properties, it is not the best choice for
D. Metronidazole gel is rarely helpful in gram-negative folliculitis.

question: A 2-year-old male presents to the emergency room with 2-3 days of fevers, chills, and diarrhea. On examination, you note painful oral erosions and tender red papules on the palms and soles. The mother states that the patient has not been eating well but was able to take some acetaminophen for the fevers. Which of the following describes the cause of this condition?
explanation: This describes a case of hand-foot-and-mouth disease. Coxsackievirus A16, a picornavirus, is the most common cause of hand, foot, and mouth disease in the United States. Picornaviradae are single-stranded, positive-sense RNA viruses. Enterovirus 71, also a picornavirus, has been implicated in outbreaks as well.

question: A 2-year-old male presents to the emergency room with painful oral ulcers and difficulty swallowing. There is no evidence of other mucocutaneous lesions. The family denies recent upper respiratory illness. The patient was given a few doses of acetaminophen for pain and diphenhydramine for sleep. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:

question: A 6-year-old male presents to the emergency room with pruritic papules in the distribution limited to the area shown in the photo below. The lesions appeared a few hours after he had returned from the beach, where he had been playing in salt water. His exam is otherwise unremarkable. What is the most likely etiology?
explanation:
Seabather's eruption is characterized by eruption of intensely pruritic erythematous papular dermatitis in areas covered by a bathing suit or in skin folds that occur after exposure to thimble jellyfish in saltwater, particularly in Florida, the Caribbean, and Gulf of Mexico. Thimble jellyfish, or Linuche unguiculata scyphomedusae, are approximately 1.5 cm in size. Their larvae are entrapped against the skin when the bather leaves the water, which is filtered through the bathing suit. Contact with freshwater or a change in osmotic pressure activates toxin release by the larvae through harpoon-like organelles called nematocysts that contain the toxins that result in the characteristic rash. Other symptoms may include fever, chills, nausea, vomiting, headache, abdominal pain and diarrhea, but these systemic symptoms occur primarily in patients with severe envenomation or children

Incorrect Answers:
A. Herpes simplex virus is not a cause of pruritic papules in this distribution.

question: A 22-year-old male graduate student returns from studying abroad in Brazil with a round, dry, scaling, hypopigmented macule with an erythematous border on the posterior medial aspect of his forearm. A biopsy obtained from the edge of the lesion reveals acid-fast bacilli. Without treatment, which of the following is most likely to occur?
explanation:
This patient has leprosy, which results from infection with Mycobacterium leprae. M. leprae is endemic in India, Brazil, parts of Africa, Indonesia, Nepal, and Bangladesh. It may also be found in Louisiana, Florida, and Texas populations, and infection may result from exposure to infected armadillos. Transmission is usually respiratory, although infection via breaks in the skin is possible. Peripheral neuropathy is a common complication, resulting in numbness, pain, and motor deficits. Nerves that are commonly involved include the ulnar, median, common peroneal (foot drop), posterior tibial (claw toes and plantar insensitivity), facial (lagophthalmos), radial cutaneous, and great auricular. In up to 10% of cases, neurological abnormalities occur without associated skin lesions. Diagnosis is confirmed by skin smears or biopsy specimens demonstrating acid-fast bacilli. Leprosy has variable manifestations, and the disease is categorized according to the Ridley-Jopling classification. The six categories are tuberculoid, borderline tuberculoid, midborderline, borderline lepromatous, lepromatous, and indeterminate. Cutaneous manifestations include (1) painless, pruritic, erythematous skin patches; (2) hypopigmented macules with dry, scaling centers and erythematous borders that are characteristic of tuberculoid leprosy); (3) extensive, bilateral, symmetric macules, nodules, plaques, and papules with poorly defined borders and raised indurated centers that are also characteristic of lepromatous leprosy; and (4) thickened, indurated skin leading to "leonine facies." The lesions often occur in cooler regions of the body, including the ears and extremities. Based on the type of disease (tuberculoid vs. lepromatous), treatment includes different regimens consisting of dapsone, rifampin, clofazimine, macrolides, minocycline, or fluoroquinolones

Incorrect Answers:
B. This finding is the pupillary changes seen in neurosyphilis.
C. Retinal degeneration does not occur, although neuropathies can affect eyelid muscles and
D. Tabes dorsalis is due to demyelination of dorsal root ganglia as a late consequence of
E. Facial nerve (CN VII) palsy is sometimes seen as a complication of Lyme disease. The

question: Match the mechanism of action with the following antifungal agents.
explanation: Flucytosine interferes with DNA and RNA synthesis selectively in fungi. It is synergistic with amphotericin. It may cause systemic toxicity in the host due to DNA and RNA effects. Ketoconazole causes a blockade of fungal P450 enzymes and also interferes with ergosterol synthesis. It is poorly selective as compared to itraconazole. It also interferes with mammalian P450 function. Caspofungin blocks the β glucan synthase and prevents synthesis of the fungal cell wall. Terbinafine inhibits epoxidation of squalene in fungi; accumulation of increased levels of squalene is toxic to fungi.

question: A 38-year-old female who presents with several diffuse patches of hypopigmented skin around her face, arms, and legs. The patient notes more patches on her back and buttock areas. In the areas of hypopigmentation, her hair had "fallen out and stopped growing." On physical exam, the patches are demarcated with a raised erythematous border and scaly-looking centers. When asked when the rash appeared, the patient states that approximately 9 months ago, she began to feel painful tingling in her arms. The rash appeared in those areas months later. A lesion biopsy on her arm reveals acid-fast bacilli and granuloma formation within dermal nerves. Which of the following is the most likely causative agent?
explanation:
Mycobacterium species of bacteria are acid-fast, and this clinical presentation describes leprosy. The patchy rash in leprosy is described as "white spots" or areas of hypopigmentation that lack hair follicles or sweat glands. Granulomas within dermal nerves on a lesion biopsy are consistent with a diagnosis of Mycobacterium leprae infection. Leprosy is classified based on the RidleyJopling classification and can be tuberculoid (TT), borderline tuberculoid (BT), mid-borderline (BB), borderline lepromatous (BL), lepromatous (LL), and indeterminate (I). Typical findings include: hypopigmented or erythematouspatch(es) on the skin, diminished sensation or loss of feeling within skin patch(es), paresthesia (tingling or numbness in the hands or feet), painless wounds or burns on the hands or feet, lumps or swelling on the earlobes or face, and tender, enlarged peripheral nerves. There are also immunologic reactions that occur before, during, or years after treatment that result in constitutional symptoms and skin findings. Type 1 reaction (reversal reaction) can last months. The diagnosis is often clinical. Type 2 reaction is more acute and includes a sudden, painful eruption of numerous nodules that tend to ulcerate and discharge yellow pus. Very rarely, this type 2 reaction can result in what is known as the Lucio phenomenon, where the patient develops a sudden necrotizing vasculopathy when they have long- standing untreated lepromatous leprosy. It tends to primarily occur in patients with Mexican ancestry, particularly from the Sinaloa area. This rare complication can be life-threatening

Incorrect Answers:
A. Treponema pallidum subspecies pallidum is a spirochete bacteria and the causative agent of
C. Treponema pallidum subspecies pertenue causes yaws. This subspecies is an endemic
D. Treponema pallidum subspecies carateum causes pinta. This spirochete is endemic to Central

question: A 7-year-old patient is seen in the clinic on Monday morning after being sent home from school for the rash depicted below. This presentation is a common clinical presentation following infection of parvovirus B-19. Following the initial infection, a mild prodrome consisting of a low-grade fever, headaches, and myalgias occurs 7-10 days before the classic erythema of the cheeks. The appearance of the rash marks the antibody isotype switching of IgM to IgG and also signifies a conclusion of the infectious state. Following initial inoculation and during the prodrome, while viremia takes place, the predominant antibody is IgM (a marker of the infectious state)

question: Four days following intense erythema of the face, which was mistaken for a sunburn by his parents, an 8-year-old child is brought to your clinic with the clinical presentation illustrated below. Aside from the onset of this new rash, he is in no acute distress and denies any significant symptoms. His parents report that he was not feeling well 2 weeks prior and may have had an upper respiratory tract infection. Which of the following lab abnormalities will aid your diagnosis?
explanation:
Parvovirus B-19 can manifest with a lacy reticular eruption of the extremities several days following the typical slapped cheek appearance and sometimes occurs prior to the resolution of the facial erythema. This occasionally pruritic eruption typically begins as confluent faint pink to erythematous patches on the arms and legs, followed by central clearing and the reticular pattern pictured. At this stage of presentation, when the infectious and viremic stage has passed, several lab changes can be noted. Parvovirus has a significant tropism for erythroid progenitor cells, most importantly erythrocytes (more specifically a surface antigen on erythrocytes known as P-antigen or globoside). This affinity is the pathogenesis of erythrocyte destruction, which can be measured by a decreased reticulocyte count and decreased hemoglobin values. Healthy patients with a parvovirus B infection experience some degree of hemolysis, which has a low potential to present clinically. Transient aplastic crises can occur in patients who have parvovirus B infection and concomitant diseases that are characterized by increased red blood cell destruction or decreased red blood cell production (e.g. iron deficiency anemia, spherocytosis, sickle-cell disease, and glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase deficiency). Prior to the conclusion of the prodromal phase, which lasts 7-10 days and begins 3-4 days following inoculation, the leukocyte count increases and peaks prior to and during the onset of the cutaneous signs of erythema infectiosum

Incorrect Answers:
A. The pathogenesis of erythrocyte destruction is measured by a decreased reticulocyte count and
C. A decreased leukocyte count takes place only during the initial infectious state and does not
E. Elevated liver transaminases and anti-nuclear antibody (ANA) are not features of parvovirus-B

question: A 13-year-old male is brought to your clinic following the development of erythema and edema of the palms and soles. On physical exam, you note significant petechiae and purpura on the palms and soles. The patient is otherwise healthy and reports only a mild fever. Which of the following is correct regarding this patient’s presentation?

choices.A: These findings are consistent with infection by a gram-positive bacterium.
choices.B: These findings are consistent with infection by an RNA virus.
choices.C: These findings are consistent with infection by a retrovirus.
choices.D: These findings are consistent with infection by a double-stranded DNA virus.
choices.E: These findings are consistent with infection by a single-stranded DNA virus.

explanation: The vignette describes the clinical findings consistent with papular purpuric gloves and socks syndrome (PPGSS). This less-common manifestation of parvovirus B19 infection differs from that of erythema infectiosum in that it has a broader range of age involvement, typically affecting young adults, and can also be characterized by an enanthem consisting of erosions and petechiae of the palate, pharynx, and tongue. Parvovirus B19 is a single- stranded DNA virus.

question: You attend a morbidity and mortality conference where the discussion is focused on a patient who succumbed to a neurodegenerative disorder following notable personality changes, prolonged coma, and eventual death. Her past medical history was relatively uneventful, but her parents were not supportive of standard vaccinations. The most likely cause of death can be attributed to which of the following infections?
explanation: This patient succumbed to complications of non-vaccination to rubeola, also known as measles. This syndrome is known as subacute sclerosing panencephalitis. It is a delayed neurodegenerative disease that presents years after measles infection. Symptoms consist of personality changes, coma, and eventually death. Routine vaccination against the measles virus is recommended to avoid these complications. The other options are not associated with subacute sclerosing panencephalitis.

question: A 6-year-old male is brought to your clinic by his mother with concerns about his nails falling off. She mentions that he just started attending daycare in the late spring, and within a few weeks developed a rash involving the trunk, arms, legs, hands, and feet. There were no significant complications following the rash, and his daily activities were not interrupted. Three months later, she noted that most of his fingernails and toenails have begun to shed. Which of the following is the most likely cause of his onychomadesis? 1433
explanation:
The most frequently encountered cause of onychomadesis (proximal shedding of the nails) is hand-foot-mouth disease caused by Coxsackie viruses. Enteroviruses are a subgroup in the family of Picornaviridae that causes a wide range of illnesses and exanthems. The most commonly encountered enteroviral manifestation is hand-foot-mouth disease. It is characterized by a vesicular eruption on the hands and feet as well as erosive stomatitis. Not every enterovirus infection manifests with the specific findings that preclude a diagnosis of hand-foot-mouth disease, but symptoms including cutaneous findings are generally the same. There are cases of hand-footmouth disease that present with no oral findings and cutaneous involvement in atypical areas such as the buttocks

Incorrect Answers:
B. Parmyxoviridae include parainfluenza virus and the viral cause of measles/rubeola.
C. Herpesviridae include HSV 1 and 2, the varicella-zoster virus, cytomegalovirus and others.
D. Togaviridae include the viruses causing both eastern and western equine encephalitis.
E. Poxviridae include viruses responsible for cowpox, monkeypox, pseudocowpox, and smallpox.

question: In patients with acute measles, which of the following supplementations is recommended to prevent significant morbidity and mortality?
explanation: The World Health Organization recommends treatment with vitamin A for all children with acute measles. In children older than 12 months, 200,000 IU are given for 2 consecutive days, children 6-11 months of age should receive 100,000 IU for the same time course, and children under 6 months of age should receive 50,000 IU for 2 days. No specific antiviral treatment exists for measles, but several studies have reported low serum levels of vitamin A with increased measlesrelated morbidity and mortality, so the above recommendations should be considered.

question: Which of the following sets of clinical findings and infectious etiologies is incorrectly paired?

choices.A: Grouped vesicles on the lower labial mucosa and herpes simplex virus I
choices.B: Petechial and red macules on the soft palate and German measles
choices.C: Painful vesicles and erosions on the posterior pharynx and echovirus
choices.D: Asymptomatic gray-white papules on the buccal mucosa and cytomegalovirus

explanation:
cytomegalovirus. Asymptomatic gray-white papules on the buccal mucosa are consistent with Koplik spots associated with rubeola/measles and not cytomegalovirus. This viral enanthem appears during the prodromal phase consisting of a fever, a cough, rhinorrhea, and rhinoconjunctivitis. At the time of the typical cephalocaudally spread exanthem, the oral findings are no longer present

question: A 3-year-old male is referred to your clinic for an eczematous rash by a local pediatrician. Three weeks prior, he had a mild upper respiratory tract infection but otherwise has no significant past medical history. The rash of 2 weeks appears to involve the right axilla, right lateral flank, and right thigh, but the mother reports worsening and spread to the left side of his body. They were given a topical corticosteroid by the pediatrician for itching that has been of minimal help. The next step in managing this patient consists of which of the following?
explanation:
This patient presents with the typical findings associated with unilateral laterothoracic exanthem (ULTE). This rash typically presents as a morbilliform or eczematous eruption confined to a single side of the body, originating in the axillae, progressing downward, and occasionally extending to the arm, waist, and thigh. With time, lesions can extend to the contralateral aspect of the body, but the predominance of skin involvement remains on the initial side involved. It can be preceded by viral upper respiratory or gastrointestinal illnesses and has an approximately 3-6-week presence before resolving on its own. Occasionally, there can be associated pruritus, but topical corticosteroids are of minimal help

Incorrect Answers:
A. A biopsy of the skin lesion would not aid in the diagnosis, as the histological features are
B. Although associated with viral infections, the cutaneous lesions themselves would yield no
D. Despite ULTE's similarities to herpes zoster, which also presents in a well-defined distribution

question: A neonate is born to a mother with suspected cytomegalovirus infection during pregnancy. The neonate demonstrates microcephaly, pneumonitis, hepatosplenomegaly, and cutaneous signs of extramedullary hematopoiesis. Which of the following scenarios most likely represents the causative factor contributing to these findings?

choices.A: The neonate was born to a mother who had a viral reactivation of cytomegalovirus and was asymptomatic.
choices.B: The neonate was born to a mother who had a viral reactivation of cytomegalovirus and was symptomatic.
choices.C: The neonate was born to a mother who had a primary viral infection in her third trimester.
choices.D: The neonate was born to a mother who had a primary viral infection in her first trimester.
choices.E: The neonate acquired the infection perinatally following exposure to infectious secretions in the birth canal.

correct_text: The neonate was born to a mother who had a primary viral infection in her
explanation:
first trimester. Cytomegalovirus represents the most commonly diagnosed congenital infection, and its incidence is as high as 1-2% of all births. Cutaneous findings include those seen with additional viral infections causing “blueberry muffin” syndrome, which is due to extramedullary hematopoiesis. Systemic findings of congenital CMV infection are outlined in the above vignette but can also include chorioretinitis. Primary CMV infection during the first trimester carries the greatest risk of cutaneous and systemic involvement

Incorrect Answers:
C. While primary infection during the third trimester certainly carries a greater risk of symptomatic

question: The clinician receives an alert in her inbox informing her that a culture taken from a 2-day-old newborn with suspected cytomegalovirus (CMV) infection came back positive. The child has scattered petechiae and mildly jaundiced, but is well appearing with a stable respiratory status, and tolerating oral feeds. Laboratory evaluation reveals a mild elevation in liver transaminases, and mild thrombocytopenia. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
explanation: Antiviral treatment is indicated for infants with symptomatic congenital CMV infection (generally defined as involvement of at least one end-organ symptom). The treatment of choice for infants with non-life-threatening disease is valganciclovir oral solution. Non-life threatening disease is defined as a child who is well appearing or only mildly symptomatic, who is being managed in the newborn nursery or at home with the following: ●Tolerating oral feeds ●Stable respiratory status (in room air or low supplemental oxygen) ●Stable and gaining weight For life-threatening disease, the treatment is IV ganciclovir. Foscarnet and cidofovir are reserved for cases of refractory CMV disease.

question: Which of the following statements regarding bullous impetigo is correct?

choices.A: Impetigo is transmitted via person-to-person contact or via fomites.
choices.B: Impetigo most commonly occurs in children who are over 6 years of age.
choices.C: Peak incidence is in the winter months.
choices.D: Non-contact sports like running, swimming, and biking increase the risk of transmission.
choices.E: Streptococcus pyogenes is usually the implicated bacterial species in impetigo.

explanation:
Impetigo is a highly contagious infectious bacterial disease and is transferred either person-toperson or through contact with fomites

Incorrect Answers:

choices.A: Systemic circulation of exfoliating toxin that targets desmoglein 1

choices.C: Auto-immune attack of desmoglein 1 by circulating antibodies
choices.D: Auto-immune attack of desmoglein 3 by circulating antibodies
choices.E: Inherited mutations in intercellular adhesion proteins

explanation:
The pathogenesis of impetigo is best described as a bacterial production of an exfoliating toxin (ETA or ETB) by phage group II of Staph aureus at localized sites of infection

A. In Staphylococcus scalded skin syndrome (SSSS), the same toxins are circulated systemically
D. Autoimmune attacks on desmoglein 1 and 3 are responsible for mucous membrane and

question: Which of the following scenarios warrants eradication of Staphylococcus carriage through twice daily application of mupirocin to the nares for 5-10 days?
explanation:
Recurrent folliculitis due to Staphylococcus aureus is a sign of possible chronic carriage. Eradicating nasal carriage through the application of twice-daily mupirocin for 5-10 days for the patient and close contacts may lead to resolution of folliculitis with lowered rates of recurrence

Incorrect Answers:
B. Both bullous and non-bullous impetigo are localized, toxin-mediated skin infections, and their
D. Gram-negative folliculitis is a result of chronic and long-term antibiotic use. It is often seen in
