question: A 15-year-old girl presents with a rash around her umbilicus. She just bought a new pair of jeans and a belt. She has a history of an itchy rash developing on her ears in response to earrings. Avoiding which of the following foods is most likely to prevent recurrence of symptoms?
explanation:
This patient's presentation suggests allergic-contact dermatitis (ACD) to nickel. The umbilicus and ears are common sites for this reaction because jeans' buttons and earrings often contain nickel or nickel products. Patients with ACD typically develop well- circumscribed erythematous pruritic papules or plaques at the site of contact with the allergen. Vesicles and bullae can also appear in acute ACD, whereas scaling and fissuring can be seen in chronic ACD. Patients who develop ACD to nickel should wear clothing and jewelry that is nickel-free or made from metals such as surgicalgrade stainless steel, pure sterling silver, platinum, or titanium. They should avoid foods with high amounts of nickel, including soy, broccoli, legumes, licorice, cocoa powder, clams, and cashews. The diagnosis of nickel ACD is usually made by history and physical examination. For confirmation, patch testing can be performed. Management includes avoiding allergens and topical steroids or oral antihistamines if symptoms develop

Incorrect Answers:
B. Fresh fruits are not typically high in nickel.
C. Conventional dairy products (including milk) are not high in nickel.
D. Eggplants are not high in nickel.

question: A 4-month-old male is brought to the pediatrician’s office for a check-up. He has had red papules on the cheeks and forehead, now extending to the trunk and the extremities. There is a family history of asthma and childhood eczema. How does infantile atopic dermatitis differ from childhood atopic dermatitis?

choices.A: Infantile atopic dermatitis typically affects the extensor surfaces.
choices.B: Childhood atopic dermatitis typically affects the face and scalp.
choices.C: Infantile atopic dermatitis is usually not pruritic.
choices.D: There is no difference between the presentation of infantile and childhood atopic dermatitis.

correct_text: Infantile atopic dermatitis typically affects the extensor surfaces
explanation:
Atopic dermatitis is a chronic condition, part of the atopic triad, along with allergic rhinitis and asthma. It is the most common form of eczema, followed by nummular and papular eczema. Individuals with atopic dermatitis can retain less moisture in their skin, leading to xerosis (dry skin) and pruritus. Acute atopic dermatitis causes red plaques, vesicles, and crusted plaques, whereas chronic atopic dermatitis leads to thickened, scaly, and dry plaques. The plaques of atopic dermatitis are classically pruritic. Genetic and environmental factors may predispose individuals to atopic dermatitis, and triggers such as harsh soaps, heat, or dry weather can cause atopic dermatitis flares. Other findings in patients with atopic dermatitis include keratosis pilaris and hyper linear palms. Infants, children, and adults may be affected by this condition, although about 85% of patients present within the first year, and about 95% appear by 5 years of age. Infantile atopic dermatitis classically affects the extensor aspects of the extremities, face, and trunk. Atopic dermatitis in children and adults typically affects the flexural aspects and trunk. First-line treatment for atopic dermatitis includes topical steroids for active plaques, low-potency steroids used for the face and intertriginous areas, and mid- to high-potency topical steroids used for the trunk, hands, and feet. Alternatively, topical nonsteroidal agents can be used when there is a concern for adverse effects from topical steroids. For extensive skin diseases not controlled by topical therapies, agents such as cyclosporine, azathioprine, light therapy, and dupilumab have been used. For pruritus affecting sleep, antihistamines such as hydroxyzine may be used. If atopic dermatitis plaques are secondarily infected, treatment must target the underlying infection (e.g., bacterial infection due to Staphylococcus aureus and viral infection due to herpes simplex virus). Patients with difficult-to-control atopic dermatitis should be followed regularly by dermatology

Incorrect Answers:
B. Childhood atopic dermatitis typically affects the flexural surfaces and trunk. The face and scalp
C. Plaques of infantile atopic dermatitis (and other forms of atopic dermatitis) are quite pruritic.

question: An infant presents with bright red, "beefy" lesions in the diaper region, accompanied by numerous satellite pustules. The lesions are particularly concentrated around the anal area. Which of the following is expected on a skin scraping?

choices.B: Spores and hyphae in a spaghetti and meatball appearance
choices.E: None (this is not a fungal infection)

explanation:
This infant’s presentation is consistent with Candida diaper dermatitis. Patients have well- defined plaques of bright red erythema with satellite pustules. The pustules and subsequent erosions are key features in distinguishing this condition from other diaper rashes. The diagnosis is primarily clinical. Skin scrapings placed in 10% potassium hydroxide demonstrate hyphae and pseudohyphae. Treatment for localized Candida diaper dermatitis includes topical antifungals such as nystatin, ketoconazole, or miconazole cream. Other forms of diaper dermatitis include irritant contact dermatitis from urine and feces, allergic contact dermatitis (which can arise from sensitivity to products in the diaper or wipes), psoriasis, and others. It is important to distinguish between these conditions as treatments differ

B. Spores and hyphae in a “spaghetti and meatball” appearance occur in tinea versicolor due to
C. Broad-based budding yeast occurs in blastomycosis.
D. Bacteria may be seen on skin, but the cause of the rash, in this case, is Candida.
E. The rash in this presented case is due to Candida.

question: A 4-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department for 3 days of a sore throat, fevers, and rash. He does not have a cough. His temperature is 100.7°F (38.2°C). He has a bright red tongue, tonsillar swelling, tender cervical lymphadenopathy, and a diffuse, blanching, rough, erythematous rash that spares his palms and soles. Which of the following is the most likely complication of this condition if left untreated?
explanation:
Scarlet fever is an acute bacterial infection that most often affects children and is characterized by a diffuse erythematous rash associated with pharyngitis. The pathogenesis involves a delayed-type hypersensitivity reaction to Streptococcus pyogenes exotoxin A. Typical physical findings include a “strawberry tongue” and a diffuse, blanching, rough “sandpaper-like” rash with small papules that spreads from the groin and armpits to the trunk and extremities, sparing the palms and soles. The rash usually fades within a week and is followed by desquamation, which can last for several weeks. The diagnosis is based on clinical presentation and is supported by rapid streptococcal testing or throat culture. Penicillin is the treatment of choice for all patients and can prevent acute rheumatic fever. Cephalosporins, macrolides, and clindamycin are alternatives for patients with penicillin allergies

Incorrect Answers:
A. Coronary artery aneurysm is a potentially lethal complication of Kawasaki disease (also known
B. Epididymo-orchitis is the most common complication of mumps infection, occurring most often
D. Herpes simplex virus type 1 (HSV-1) encephalitis is the most common cause of sporadic fatal
E. Pneumonia is an uncommon complication of scarlet fever.

question: A 16-year-old girl is evaluated for joint pain and swollen fingers that have worsened over three weeks. She has had malaise and fatigue for several months and is bothered by bright sunlight. She has lost 7 pounds since a visit one year ago. There is an erythematous rash on both cheeks (image) and several shallow ulcerations of the buccal mucosa on physical examination. She has fusiform swelling of her fingers with pain on movement. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
Answer: Systemic lupus erythematosus Explanation: The patient has a malar rash typical of systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE), a multisystem autoimmune disease. Other common symptoms and signs include oral ulcers, arthritis, and photosensitivity. Features are variable and depend upon the organ system affected. SLE can result in cardiovascular, renal, gastrointestinal, pulmonary, ophthalmologic, and hematologic abnormalities. Constitutional symptoms include fevers, weight loss, and fatigue. Laboratory findings highly associated with SLE include antinuclear antibodies (ANA), anti-double-stranded DNA antibodies [anti-dsDNA], and anti-Smith antibodies [anti-Sm]. SLE occurs most commonly in females and is often diagnosed during adolescence. The associated chronic renal disease causes significant morbidity and mortality

A. Dermatomyositis is characterized by skin disease and muscle weakness (particularly proximal).
B. JRA is characterized by arthritis of at least 6 weeks duration. Skin complaints usually include a
C. Rheumatic fever is a complication of streptococcal pharyngitis and includes polyarthritis,
D. Lyme disease is caused by the tick-borne bacterium Borrelia burgdorferi. It often presents with a

question: A 4-year-old girl is brought to the pediatrician in September for three days of low-grade fever, a rash on her hands and feet, and blisters in her mouth. Other children in her nursery school have had similar symptoms. She is reluctant to eat but has been drinking fluids readily. Physical examination reveals 1 mm vesicles on her tongue and posterior pharynx. There are 1-2 mm papules and vesicles on erythematous bases on her hands (Figure 1) and feet, which are tender. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
Hand, foot, and mouth disease (HFMD) is a common, typically minor, systemic enteroviral infection affecting children and adults. The illness presents typical oral and skin eruptions, although multiple organs can be involved. It is caused by a wide range of Coxsackie A viruses, most commonly A16 and A71, and increasingly, A6. Herpangina is a related condition also caused by Coxsackie viruses, and it is generally limited to mouth lesions. The virus is shed in saliva, stool, respiratory secretions, and fluid from ruptured skin lesions. HFMD typically occurs in the summer and early fall, and the incubation period is 3-5 days. Given its contagious nature, HFMD can spread within families, schools, camps, etc. The ingested virus first replicates in the gastrointestinal tract and oropharynx, which results in primary viremia. Other organs (e.g. skin, heart, liver, CNS) are seeded, and further replication occurs, resulting in clinical illness. Skin lesions are classic and give HFMD its name. Lesions are maculopapular and vesicular and tend to occur on the hands and feet, including the palms and soles. The extremities and trunk may also be involved. In the mouth, vesicles on erythematous bases form on the tongue, buccal mucosa, and palate. All lesions may be painful; in children, this may cause a reluctance to eat. The illness is usually mild and selflimited but occasionally can be severe, causing myocarditis, aseptic meningitis, and other CNS manifestations. Coxsackie A71 is the strain most likely to cause complicated infections. Treatment of HFMD is primarily supportive

Incorrect Answers:
B. Herpes simplex virus causes herpetic gingivostomatitis, accompanied by fever, irritability, and
C. Oral aphthous ulcers, also known as canker sores, are necrotic ulcers consisting of fibrinous
D. Traumatic ulcerations occur in children who bite their cheeks.
E. Behcet syndrome is a multisystem disease that is rare in children. It involves oral and genital

question: A 2-month-old female presents with a red lesion on her chest. She was delivered without complications after an uneventful pregnancy and has been healthy thus far. Her skin was clear at birth, but she now has a rapidly enlarging growth (see image below). The parents are quite concerned about the long-term cosmetic outcome. What is the appropriate management at this time?
explanation:
Infantile hemangiomas of the skin (strawberry hemangiomas) are common vascular tumors of infancy. They occur in 4-5% of infants and affect more females than males. Prematurity, low birth weight, multiple gestations, preeclampsia, older maternal age, and a family history of hemangiomas are risk factors. They are often absent at birth but typically become visible a few days to months later. Multiple hemangiomas may be present, and they may occasionally be internal. The rapid growth of the lesions slows after the first few months and typically stops by a year of age. Spontaneous regression is typical and may take several years. Studies have shown that 90 percent of hemangioma involution is often complete by four years of age. Most hemangiomas do not improve significantly after three to four years of age. Most cases are of only cosmetic concern, but a location in certain areas may be problematic. Enlarging hemangiomas on the eyelid may interfere with visual development, and lesions on areas subjected to pressure or abrasion may ulcerate or bleed. Not all hemangiomas require treatment, but those on the face, or those that are large, pedunculated, deep, or in risky areas, should be referred for therapy. Topical beta-blockers are the medical treatment of choice in most cases, and either propranolol or timolol are most frequently prescribed. For high-risk hemangiomas, oral propranolol is used, but patients should be monitored for systemic side effects. For the patient described in this case, one could opt for continued observation. Given the parents’ concerns, a topical beta-blocker is appropriate since the risks of treatment are low

Incorrect Answers:
A. Topical steroids are not used in the treatment of strawberry hemangiomas.
B. Laser therapy is only used for complex lesions.
D. Excision of simple hemangiomas is not indicated because the lesions usually regress, and
E. Cryoablation is not commonly used for simple hemangiomas.

question: A previously healthy 5-year-old child has had flu-like symptoms with fever for the past week. She is now feeling better but presents with redness of the cheeks and a rash over the extremities and trunk. The rash is maculopapular, with some clearing of lesion centers, creating a lace-like appearance (Figure 1). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
Erythema infectiosum, or “fifth disease,” is a common and highly contagious disease of childhood, caused by Parvovirus B19. Patients may be asymptomatic or may present with flu- like symptoms initially, including headaches, low-grade fevers, and a runny nose. After the prodromal symptoms, patients develop a red macular rash on their cheeks (“slapped-cheek”) that spares the perioral area and may spread to the rest of the body. By the time the rash develops, patients are no longer infectious. Patients may have flares of the rash for months after the infection, precipitated by sunlight or emotional state. Diagnosis is mostly clinical but can be confirmed with specific antibody testing to parvovirus B19. Adults may have arthralgias or arthritis as prominent symptoms. For immunocompetent individuals, infection is usually self-limited, so no treatment is needed. In patients with sickle cell anemia or other pre-existing blood abnormalities, infection with parvovirus B19 may lead to an aplastic crisis due to inhibition of erythropoiesis. Patients may experience symptoms of severe anemia, including shortness of breath, worsening heart failure, and tachycardia. This typically resolves within a few weeks after the infection has resolved. If a woman becomes infected with Parvovirus B19 during pregnancy, the fetus can develop aplastic anemia. The result is often miscarriage due to severe hydrops fetalis

Incorrect Answers:
A. Measles is a more severe illness. The maculopapular rash begins on the head and spreads
B. Due to universal immunization, there are now almost no cases of rubella occurring in the United
C. Systemic lupus erythematosus is a multisystem autoimmune disease. It can cause a malar
E. Contact dermatitis presents with a history of exposure to an allergen, and the skin is pruritic and

question: A 4-month-old female infant is brought to the pediatrician due to a rash on her head. Her parents report the lesion has persisted for the past 2 months, and they worry an infection may have caused it. The patient's birth history is uncomplicated, and she was born vaginally at term. The patient has met her developmental milestones and is gaining weight by breastfeeding. Her temperature is 99° F (37.2° C), blood pressure is 70/45 mm Hg, pulse is 125/min, and respiratory rate is 30/min. The physical exam reveals a healthy young infant with a regular cardiopulmonary exam and a scaly, oily rash on her scalp (Figure 1). Which of the following is the best initial treatment for this patient?
explanation:
Seborrheic dermatitis (SD) is a chronic, inflammatory skin condition that is linked to an overproduction of sebaceous matter and is often exacerbated by Malassezia yeast. While mild cases in adults might respond to emollients and non-medical shampoos, in infants, a more targeted treatment is often required due to the sensitivity of their skin and the risk of complications. Topical antifungals like ketoconazole are often recommended as a first-line treatment for seborrheic dermatitis in infants. Ketoconazole helps in reducing the fungal activity on the skin, thus providing relief from symptoms

question: A 7-month-old boy is evaluated for multiple swellings on his scalp. His parents report that several swollen areas over the child’s scalp have become progressively larger over the past month. The child is also urinating more frequently than usual. His vital signs are normal. A physical examination reveals five soft tissue swellings on his head. Also noted is asymmetric exophthalmos. A cranial computed tomography (CT) scan reveals multifocal lytic calvarial lesions. Which of the following would be most likely to be seen with electron microscopy?

choices.B: Cytoplasmic azurophilic granules creating needle-like structures
choices.C: Rod-shaped granules with a latticed matrix

correct_text: Rod-shaped granules with a latticed matrix
explanation:
Langerhans cell histiocytosis (LCH) refers to a group of proliferative disorders characterized by abundant Langerhans cells, accompanied by a decrease in other hematopoietic cells such as Tlymphocytes, macrophages, and eosinophils. Langerhans cells are dendritic (antigen- presenting) cells, which are activated by bacterial products. They upregulate costimulatory molecules and migrate to draining lymph nodes. LCH has variable presentations. One of these is Hand-SchullerChristian disease, which manifests as scalp rash, exophthalmos, and diabetes insipidus. Electron microscopy of the lesions may reveal Birbeck (“tennis racket”) granules, which are cytoplasmic latticed rod-shaped granules specific to Langerhans cells

Incorrect Answers:
A. Proliferative monoclonal plasma cells characterize multiple myeloma, which can manifest with
B. Auer rods (needle-like structures consisting of aggregations of cytoplasmic azurophilic granules)
D. Hairy cell leukemia (HCL) exhibits B cells with hair-like cytoplasmic projections due to mature B
E. “Buttock cells,” or atypical lymphocytes with cerebriform nuclei, can be seen on skin biopsy in

question: A patient presents with multiple skin and uterine leiomyomas. What is the associated cancer?
explanation:
Hereditary leiomyomatosis and renal cell cancer (HLRCC). HLRCC is an autosomal dominant familial cancer syndrome that is characterized by cutaneous leiomyomas, uterine leiomyomas (fibroids), and renal cell cancer, as well as rare reports of leiomyosarcoma. Renal cell cancer in HLRCC follows an aggressive clinical course and may be metastatic at the time of initial diagnosis. Germline mutations in the fumarate hydratase (FH) gene predispose to HLRCC. FH encodes an enzyme in the Krebs cycle that converts fumarate to malate. Patients with suspected HLRCC should be screened for renal, uterine and skin tumors and offered genetic counseling and FH mutation testing. HLRCC surveillance screening is continued throughout the lifetime of individuals at risk for HLRCC

Incorrect Answers:
B. Adrenal carcinomas are not associated with HLRCC.
C. Colon adenocarcinomas are associated with Gardner’s syndrome and Muir-Torre syndrome.
D. Pancreatic cancer is associated with necrolytic migratory erythema (glucagonoma syndrome).
E. Ovarian cancer is associated with dermatomyositis.

question: What patients have the highest incidence of SCC?
explanation:
After adjustment for age and type of immunosuppressive regimen, lung and heart-lung transplant recipients have a higher risk of SCC than other solid organ transplant patients. Heart transplant patients alone have the second-highest risk of SCC

C. Renal transplant patients have the third-highest risk of SCC, after lung and heart transplant

question: What is the associated virus for the condition below?
explanation:
HHV-8 aka Kaposi’s sarcoma-associated herpesvirus is a latent virus found in the vast majority of all types of Kaposi’s sarcoma worldwide. The classic form of KS is typically found in men of Mediterranean and Ashkenazi Jewish descent in their sixth decade of life. Classic lesions initially develop as purplish-red plaques primarily on the lower legs of elderly men. The disease generally has a slow progression. In addition to the classic form of KS there are: HIV associated, African endemic, and drug-induced immunosuppression associated forms. AIDS related is more widespread and presents as small macules, patches, or plaques, but can become exophytic and ulcerative. Approximately 80% of AIDS patients with KS will develop GI involvement. The second most common process associated with HHV-8 is Castleman’s disease

A. HHV-7 is associated with exanthem subitum (Sixth Disease, Roseola Infantum).
C. Hep B is associated with Giannoti-Crosti syndrome.
D. Hep C is associated with lichen planus, porphyria cutana tarda, polyarteritis nodosa,
E. EBV is associated with nasopharyngeal carcinoma, oral hairy leukoplakia.

question: Patients with juvenile dermatomyositis are at increased risk for?
explanation:
Juvenile dermatomyositis is more highly associated with calcinosis, and tends to favor sites of trauma

question: Which of the following is false regarding relapsing polychondritis?

choices.A: May lead to saddle nose deformities

choices.D: May be treated with systemic corticosteroids

explanation:
Relapsing polychondritis is associated with Type II collagen antibodies (not Type I)

question: A patient presents with chronic urticaria, fever, bone pain, athralgias and myalgias. Which underlying systemic association must be ruled out?
explanation:
Chronic urticaria along with fever, bone pain, athralgias, and myalgias suggests Schnitzler’s syndrome, which is associated with monoclonal immunoglobulin M (IgM) gammopathy

Incorrect Answers:
A. IgG kappa gammopathy is associated with scleredema and necrobiotic xanthogranuloma.
B. IgG lambda gammopathy is associated with scleromyxedema.

question: Which of the following regarding this condition is true?

choices.A: associated with an IgG lambda paraproteinemia
choices.B: histologically characterized by mucin deposition, increased collagen deposition, and proliferation of irregularly arranged fibroblasts
choices.C: thyroid function tests should be ordered, and are expected to be normal
choices.D: examination of the proximal interphalangeal joint (PIP) may reveal induration surrounding a centrally depressed area (termed donut sign)

correct_text: all of the above are true
explanation: All the statements are true statements. A. Scleromyxedema is associated with an IgG lambda paraproteinemia. B. Histologically, scleromyxedema is associated with a triad of microscopic features: a diffuse deposit of mucin in the upper and mid-reticular dermis, an increase in collagen deposition, and a marked proliferation of irregularly arranged fibroblasts. C. Since the histologic differential diagnosis of scleromyxedema includes pretibial myxedema, thryoid function testing should be ordered. Normal thyroid function is expected in scleromyxedema. D. The ‘donut’ sign describes induration surrounding a centrally depressed area, typically along the PIP joint. This finding is classically associated with scleromyxedema.

question: Which of the following regarding this condition is false?

choices.B: may be associated with diabetes and thyroid disease
choices.C: spontaneous resolution is expected in localized disease

explanation:
Anti-topoisomerase 1 antibodies (anti-SCL70) are associated with systemic sclerosis or diffuse scleroderma. Limited sclerosis (CREST syndrome) presents with calcinosis, Raynaud’s, esophageal dysmotility, sclerodactyly, and telangiectases, and serologically demonstrates anticentromere antibody positivity. Pulmonary function tests (PFTs) should be ordered in patients with suspected SSc. Some degree of pulmonary involvement is identified in >80 percent of patients with SSc. The two principal manifestations are interstitial lung disease (also called fibrosing alveolitis or pulmonary fibrosis) and pulmonary vascular disease, leading to pulmonary arterial hypertension. At diagnosis, the following laboratory studies should be obtained: complete blood count and differential, which may reveal anemia due to malabsorption, iron deficiency, or gastrointestinal blood loss; serum creatinine level, which may indicate renal dysfunction; creatine kinase (CK), which may be elevated in patients with myopathy or myositis; and urinalysis with urine sediment, which may reveal proteinuria and/or cellular casts

question: A patient being treated with interferon for Hep C begins growing hair on his forehead. What should you check?
explanation:

question: A 43-year-old male with a history of alcohol abuse presents to the ED with three days of nausea and vomiting. His abdomen is noticeably distended, and his skin and eyes are yellow. His records indicate a history of cirrhosis related to heavy alcohol use. Which of the following dermatological findings could be present in this patient? ￼

choices.D: Raised red lines on the skin after contact ("skin writing")

explanation:
Muehrcke nails (see image) are not specific to cirrhosis and can be seen in other disorders that may lead to low albumin, such as nephrotic syndrome. Another nail finding in cirrhosis is Terry nails, which refers to white nail beds with sparing of the tips. Other skin disorders associated with cirrhosis include palmar erythema, clubbing, and telangiectasias, also known as spider angiomata. Spider angiomata are small, dilated blood vessels commonly found on the trunk, face, and upper limbs of cirrhotic individuals. Although the pathogenesis of telangiectasias is unclear, they are thought to be caused by imbalances in sex hormone metabolism

A. Blue lunulae, or blue nails, are seen in Wilson's disease. The blue color involves the crescentshaped white part of the fingernail bed and fades proximally.

question: A 68-year-old woman presents to your clinic with an erythematous rash affecting the cheeks bilaterally as well as the bridge of her nose. She states she has no significant past medical history or notable family history. Which of the following laboratory studies would be most appropriate to perform initially?
explanation:
The hallmark of autoimmune connective tissue diseases is the presence of circulating autoantibodies. The most commonly performed laboratory exam when there is suspicion of an autoimmune connective tissue disease is the measurement of anti-nuclear antibodies (ANA). The patient in the vignette presents with one of the cardinal signs of systemic lupus erythematosus, the malar rash. The findings of a bilaterally erythematous facial rash can also represent other dermatologic conditions such as rosacea or sunburn. If the suspicion for a connective tissue disease is present, the initial test of choice is the anti-nuclear antibody titer. It is also more costeffective then submitting an entire panel for the testing of various other autoantibodies

B. Anti-double stranded DNA antibodies (dsDNA) are specific for lupus erythematosus and can
C. Anti-Jo 1 antibodies are myositis specific antibodies directed at aminoacyl tRNA. It is also
D. Antibodies to Scl-70 are specific for systemic sclerosis.

question: A patient presents to your clinic for a second opinion about her diagnosis and associated lab results. She was initially seen by her primary care physician for mild joint pain associated with a facial rash that comes and goes and was described as photosensitive by the patient. She states that labs were done revealing an elevated ANA. Which of the following conditions can be associated with positive ANA titers in the absence of lupus?
explanation: All of the above choices can lead to an elevated ANA without insinuating a diagnosis of a connective tissue disease such as dermatomyositis or systemic lupus erythematosus. The estimated sensitivity of ANA is approximately 98% however, its specificity is only 90%, meaning it is more difficult to rule- in the diagnosis of a mixed connective tissue disease if a patient demonstrates a positive ANA. The reasons for this are represented in choices A-D. A/B. Autoimmune diseases such as Grave’s Disease, Hashimoto’s Thyroiditis, and Pernicious anemia can contribute to a positive ANA in patients with autoimmune dermatologic disorders such as vitiligo and alopecia areata. C. Medications such as minocycline and hydralazine can lead to druginduced lupus and also cause elevated ANA levels. Additional testing including histone antibodies should be performed if there is suspicion for drug-induced lupus, and will aide in the extrapolation of a positive ANA. D. To confound matters more, healthy patients can exhibit a positive ANA which increases in incidence as the cut-off for positivity increases. For instance, if a titer of 1:80 is interpreted as positive, 13.3% of a healthy population can demonstrate a positive ANA. Similarly, when the titer cut- off is decreased to 1:160 and 1:320, corresponding positive findings in a healthy population decrease to 5.0% and 3.3% respectively. This highlights two important factors in the interpretation of an ANA titer. The first is that different labs may have a different cut-off for what can be interpreted as positive, and secondly, a significant number of healthy individuals can demonstrate a positive ANA regardless of the cut-off level used.

question: Which of the following immunofluorescent ANA patterns demonstrates the greatest degree of specificity?
explanation:
Immunoflourescent ANA patterns do not typically lend a significant amount of specificity enabling the clinician to establish a diagnosis. The one exception to this rule is the centromeric pattern, which is a pattern that is strongly and specifically associated with the limited form of systemic sclerosis (SSc). The centromeric pattern, or discrete speckled pattern, results from antibody binding to the polypeptide components of chromosomal centromeres

B. The peripheral or rim pattern likely targets autoantibodies to DNS including double-stranded
C. The speckled pattern is associated with SLE, Sjögren syndrome, scleroderma, polymyositis,
E. The Nucleolar pattern is associated with scleroderma and polymyositis.

question: One of the patients you have been following for psoriasis presents to your clinic with the onset of a new facial rash, fevers, and laboratory evidence of thrombocytopenia and leukopenia. You suspect drug-induced lupus, but begin to consider alternative diagnoses when his lab report returned positive for ANA (with a titer of 1:160), negative for anti-histone antibodies, and positive for autoantibodies to double- stranded DNA. The most likely cause of these lab findings is which of the following?
explanation: Several of the biologics used to treat psoriasis, psoriatic arthritis, and rheumatoid arthritis, that fall into the category of tumor necrosis factor-alpha inhibitors, can cause drug-induced lupus that differs from typical drug-induced lupus. Medications like infliximab and etanercept can cause druginduced lupus that leads to an elevation in ANA as well as dsDNA while having no effect on the anti-histone autoantibodies that can occur with drug-induced lupus caused by other medications (Choices A, B, and D). Other systemic immunomodulators used to treat psoriasis such as ustekinumab (Choice E) have not been consistently reported in association with ANA/dsDNA positive drug-induced lupus and this may be due to the inhibition of different pro-inflammatory targets. Skin findings in TNF-alpha inhibitor drug-induced lupus can mimic the findings of acute SLE (malar rash and/or discoid lesions), subacute cutaneous LE, and chilblains. Photosensitivity and purpura may also be present in patients with this variant of drug-induced lupus. Additionally, systemic symptoms such as fever, pancytopenica, phlebitis, thrombocytopenia, and serositis can occur in patients with drug-induced lupus caused by TNF-alpha inhibitors. Drug-induced lupus is anti-histone antibody positive in more than 75% of patients, as opposed to approximately 20% in SLE. Drug-induced lupus caused by medications like hydralazine, procainamide, and minocycline, does not typically demonstrate the salient cutaneous features of acute SLE such as the malar rash or discoid lesions. In these cases of drug-induced lupus systemic symptoms are more prominent then cutaneous findings.

question: A 45 year-old patient whom you have been following in your clinic returns for a follow-up discussion after a biopsy demonstrating the poikilodermatous skin changes around his neck were consistent with the diagnosis of dermatomyositis. He continues to experience no muscle weakness. Which of the following positive lab findings would most likely put this patient at increased risk for adenocarcinoma?
explanation:
Cancer-associated myositis (CAM) in adults has been associated in several studies with antibodies to transcription intermediary factor (TIF)-1gamma (anti-p155, anti-p155/140) and with antibodies to nuclear matrix protein (NXP)-2 (anti-MJ or anti-p140). In one study, 80% of adult patients with amyopathic (no skeletal muscle involvement) dermatomyositis, demonstrated positive serology for anti-p155 autoantibodies. This same antibody was found in 20-30% of juvenile-onset and classic adult-onset dermatomyositis. CAMs seen are most often adenocarcinomas. Approximately 70 percent of the cancers associated with inflammatory myopathies are adenocarcinomas of the cervix, lung, ovaries, pancreas, bladder, and stomach. Some studies have shown that the risk of ovarian cancer is particularly high in women who are anti-transcription intermediary factor 1 (TIF1) antibody positive. In some patient populations, the types of malignancies associated with DM or PM reflect cancers found in particularly high prevalence within that population. For instance, Southeast Asian patients with DM are at increased risk of nasopharyngeal carcinoma, which is more common in that population than others

question: What percentage of adults with discoid lesions will develop systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) as defined by the criteria established by the American College of Rheumatology?

choices.A: There is no associated risk of developing SLE in patients with discoid lesions
choices.B: 5-15% of patients with discoid lesions will develop SLE
choices.C: 30-40% of patients with discoid lesions will develop SLE
choices.D: 60-75% of patients with discoid lesions will develop SLE
choices.E: SLE develops in more than 75% of patients with discoid lesions

correct_text: 5-15% of patients with discoid lesions will develop SLE
explanation: Approximately 5-15% of patients with the discoid lesions of discoid lupus erythematosus will go on to develop systemic lupus erythematosus as defined by the 11 criteria set forth by the American College of Rheumatology ACR. There are some published studies pointing to an increased risk for the development of SLE in patients whose discoid lesions are extensive and involve anatomic areas below the head and neck. It is important to note, that one of the 11 criteria for SLE is the presence of discoid lesions.
