question: All of the following are chromophores targeted by lasers EXCEPT?

choices.E: All of the above are chromophores

explanation:
A chromophore is a molecule that absorbs a photon transmitted by a laser. Once the photon is absorbed by the chromophore, energy is transmitted from the photon to the chromophore, the photon ceases to exist, and the chromophore enters an excited state. This is one of the basic features of laser therapy. Three chromophores exist in the skin; they are water, melanin, and hemoglobin. Lasers targeting hair for depilation are actually targeting the melanin inside of hair. This is the main reason why blonde or grey hair is not successfully removed by laser hair removal therapy

Incorrect Answers:
D. Each of the three chromophores has its own absorption spectrum in which specific lasers can

question: The time it takes for a specific heated tissue to cool halfway to its original temperature defines which of the following principles of laser therapy? 1921
explanation:
As described in the question, the thermal relaxation time for a given chromophore is defined as the time it takes for the target chromophore to dissipate its absorbed heat and reach the halfway point to its original temperature prior to photon absorption and transference of heat. The goal of laser therapy is to denature the target chromophore prior to heat-dissipating to surrounding, nontargeted tissue

Incorrect Answers:
A. Fluence is the amount of energy (Joules) delivered per unit of area (centimeters squared)
B. Power is the rate at which energy is delivered and is measured in Watts.
C. Pulse Duration or pulse width, is the amount of time a targeted chromophore is exposed to
E. Energy is the fundamental unit of work, measured in Joules.

question: Which of the following laser therapy targets has the longest thermal relaxation time?
explanation:
The thermal relaxation time (TRT) for a given chromophore is defined as the time it takes for the target chromophore to dissipate its absorbed heat and reach the halfway point to its original temperature prior to photon absorption and transference of heat. The goal of laser therapy is to denature the target chromophore prior to heat-dissipating to surrounding, non- targeted tissue. The thermal relaxation time for a given molecular target is proportional to the square of the diameter of a chromophore (d2), or simply speaking, the larger the molecule, the longer the thermal relaxation time. The diameter of a chromophore that is part of a leg vein is estimated to be 1mm, and its thermal relaxation time is measured in seconds, usually 1-2 total. Of the above options, leg veins have the longest thermal relaxation time

Incorrect Answers:
A. Melanosomes, which have molecular chromophores approximating 0.5 microns in diameter,
B. In contrast, capillaries, having chromophores that are microns in diameter, demonstrate a TRT
D. Terminal hair follicles exhibit a TRT that is measured in the tens to one hundred milliseconds.

question: The greatest effective penetration depth into skin occurs with which of the following combinations?

choices.A: Small spot size and wavelengths greater than 1300 nm
choices.B: Small spot size and wavelengths in the range of 600 to 1300 nm
choices.C: Large spot size and wavelengths less than 600 nm
choices.D: Large spot size and wavelengths greater than 1300 nm
choices.E: Large spot size and wavelengths in the range of 600 – 1300 nm

correct_text: Large spot size and wavelengths in the range of 600 – 1300 nm
explanation: The greatest effective penetration in-depth takes place when laser therapy is applied with a large spot size, and a wavelength between 600 and 1300 nm. Smaller spot sizes enable increased photon scattering and loss of photons from the beam as the laser enters into the skin. Larger spot size provides the ability for a greater number of photons to maintain their presence in the beam enabling a greater percentage of skin penetration and a decrease in potential scatter. Wavelength dependent penetration relies on the directly proportional relationship between depth reached and increasing wavelength. Beyond 1300 nm, penetration decreases because of the amount of absorption of light by water.

question: A patient presents to your clinic with a hemi-facial vascular lesion that is pink to faintly erythematous and involves the right side of the face in a V1 distribution. You discuss potential treatments and complications and he wishes to proceed with laser therapy. Which of the following side effects is most common from the laser used to treat this patient’s lesion?
explanation:
The patient in this vignette presents with a port-wine stain on the right side of the face. These capillary malformations, when involving the face, tend to occur in a dermatomal distribution following the three branches of the trigeminal nerve. The most common laser used to treat these lesions is the pulsed dye laser (585-595nm), and the most commonly associated side effect to treatment with this laser is purpura. Purpuric lesions appear almost instantaneously following the use of the laser, and some clinicians use this as a marker to determine which areas are responding to the therapy and to prevent overlapping with repetitive passes. Longer pulse widths (> 6ms) can be used in order to avoid the cosmetically unacceptable purpura that results from intravascular thrombosis, however, some degree of this thrombosis may be necessary for the efficacious treatment of port-wine stains and may be unavoidable

Incorrect Answers:
E. While other side-effects can occur, they do so in a much lower frequency, and adjustments to

question: The parent of a 6 month-old child seeks your advice as a second opinion regarding treatment of his child’s port-wine stain located in the V1 and V2 distribution on the left side of her face. You begin to discuss the risks and benefits of pulsed dye laser therapy as a beneficial modality for the treatment. All of the following are reasonable arguments for the treatment of port-wine stains with pulsed dye laser EXCEPT?

choices.A: The vessels of a port-wine stain do not involute
choices.B: The surface area of a port-wine stain will increase proportionally as a child grows
choices.C: The port-wine stain may grow thicker and develop nodules
choices.D: The port-wine stain may impact a child’s social and cognitive development impacting adult social relationships
choices.E: All of the above are correct

correct_text: All of the above are correct
explanation: There are numerous physical and psychological reasons supporting the early and aggressive treatment of port-wine stains with pulsed-dye laser therapy. In addition to the physical aspects regarding their change in size, thickness, and permanence it is important to remember the sociological impact living with a cosmetically visible lesion can have on a child. Several studies exist demonstrating the impact living with untreated lesions have had on the ability of patients to form mature adult relationships, and all support and give further support for initiating early treatment.

question: A patient presents to your Mohs clinic for the removal of a squamous cell carcinoma on the dorsum of the right hand. question: All of the following statements regarding the metabolism of local anesthetics are true EXCEPT?

choices.A: Amides are metabolized by the CYP450 system
choices.B: Esters are metabolized by plasma pseudocholinesterase
choices.C: Esters are excreted by the kidneys
choices.D: Amides are excreted by the liver into the entero-hepatic circulation
choices.E: Para-aminobenzoic acid (PABA) is a metabolic by product of ester anesthetics Explanation Correct answer: D. Amides are excreted by the liver into the entero-hepatic circulation. Choice (D) is false. Amides are metabolized by the CYP450 hepatic enzyme system (Choice A), and the kidneys excrete their metabolites. Patients with significant liver disease, such as cirrhosis, are placed at an increased risk for anesthetic toxicity with the use of amide anesthetics. Esters are metabolized by plasma pseudocholinesterase (Choice B). Patients with a deficiency of pseudocholinesterase may experience prolonged paralysis following the use of ester anesthetics. This class of anesthesia should be avoided in such patients. Metabolites of esters are excreted by the kidneys (Choice C). Para-aminobenzoic acid (PABA), is a metabolite of ester anesthetics (Choice E), and are responsible for allergic reactions associated with this class of anesthesia. Incorrect Answers:

correct_text: D. Amides are excreted by the liver into the entero-hepatic circulation. Choice (D)

Patients with significant liver disease, such as cirrhosis, are placed at an increased risk for anesthetic toxicity with the use of amide anesthetics. Esters are metabolized by plasma pseudocholinesterase (Choice B). Patients with a deficiency of pseudocholinesterase may experience prolonged paralysis following the use of ester anesthetics. This class of anesthesia should be avoided in such patients. Metabolites of esters are excreted by the kidneys (Choice C). question: A woman returns to your clinic for the examination of a suspicious mole located on the left breast she had presented with 6 months ago. On exam, you notice that it has become slightly irregular in symmetry and there is notable color variation. You elect to perform an excisional biopsy. She informs you that she is 5 months pregnant. Which of the following local anesthetics is most appropriate for use during this procedure?
explanation:
Lidocaine is the only listed local anesthetic that is Category B and safe to use in pregnant women. Studies performed on women who received Lidocaine during the first trimester of pregnancy demonstrated no increased risk of the development of anatomic abnormalities in neonates. Regardless, because organogenesis occurs during the first trimester and up to 4 months, it is recommended that lidocaine be used cautiously during this time period to prevent teratogenicity. If possible, delaying any procedures until after the fourth month is an acceptable approach in the use of local anesthetics in pregnant women

Incorrect Answers:
D. Choices A-D are all pregnancy Category C and are not deemed safe to use during pregnancy.

question: Which of the following local anesthetics has a vasodilatory effect on cutaneous blood vessels?
explanation: All local anesthetics, except for Cocaine, exert a vasodilatory effect on the smooth muscles of cutaneous blood vessels. This is problematic because when administered locally, vasodilation increases the circulation to and from the cutaneous vasculature injected, decreasing the availability of the anesthetic to local tissue and preventing the desired effect. It is for this reason, epinephrine, a vasoconstrictor, is often co-administered. Epinephrine prolongs the duration of anesthesia through its vasoconstrictive effect and also serves to reduce intraoperative bleeding. Esters and amides, like those listed in choice A and B are vasodilators as are all local anesthetics aside from Cocaine (Choice C)

C. Transfer of dermis, epidermis and adnexal structures describes a full thickness skin graft.

question: A 40 year old man complains of a 6 week history of the rash in the photo. The lesions are pruritic and appear on his trunk, legs and arms. explanation:

Terbinafine is rarely associated with lichenoid drug eruption. question: A patient has a 6mm dark brown nevus on the posterior lower leg. The nevus has grown and has an irregular shape. When discussing a skin biopsy to rule out a dysplastic nevus, the clinician asks about anesthetic allergies. The patient reports a positive patch test to para-aminobenzoic acid in the past. Which local anesthetic should be avoided in this patient?
explanation:
Esters are hydrolyzed by plasma pseudocholinesterase. PABA is a metabolite of ester anesthetics and may cause allergic sensitivity reaction in those with PABA allergy. As such, those with PABA allergies should avoid ester anesthetics and should receive amide anesthetics instead. The easiest way to distinguish between an amide and an ester anesthetic is the following: amides have two i's in their name (e.g., bupivacaine, lidocaine, prilocaine), whereas esters only have one i (e.g, benzocaine)

Incorrect Answers:
A. Bupivacaine is an amide anesthetic and is safe to use in patients with para-aminobenzoic acid
C. Lidocaine is an amide anesthetic and is safe to use in patients with para-aminobenzoic acid
D. Sodium bicarbonate use is not contraindicated in those with PABA allergy. It is frequently
E. Prilocaine is an amide anesthetic and is safe to use in patients with para-aminobenzoic acid

question: An intubated patient in the intensive care unit has a suspected hypersensitivity reaction. The patient’s family reveals that the patient has a known penicillin allergy. While reviewing the list of the patient’s current medications, the clinician notes that no penicillins are listed, but the patient is receiving a cephalosporin. Which of the following is a fourth-generation cephalosporin?
explanation:
Cefepime is a fourth-generation cephalosporin. Fifteen percent of people with penicillin allergy have cross-reactivity to cephalosporins. Also, 2% of people with a cephalosporin allergy have cross-reactivity to penicillins. Cephalosporins and penicillins may cause similar cutaneous findings, including morbilliform eruption, urticaria, drug fever, eosinophilia, and acute generalized exanthematous pustulosis

Incorrect Answers:
B. Cephalexin is a first-generation cephalosporin, along with cefadroxil and cefazolin.
C. Cefaclor is a second-generation cephalosporin along with cefprozil, cefuroxime axetil,
D. Cefotaxime is a third-generation cephalosporin along with cefixime, cefdinir, cefpodoxime,
E. Ceftriaxone is a third-generation cephalosporin.

question: A patient presents with lesion noted in the photograph. The lesion reappears when in the same location when the patient takes a specific medication. A biopsy is performed which revealed interface dermatitis, necrotic keratinocytes and eosinophils. Which of the following drugs is most likely the culprit of this reaction?
explanation:
Trimethoprim-Sulfamethoxazole is among the most common drugs that can cause a fixed drug eruption. Fixed drug eruptions happen at the same skin site with each exposure to the offending medication. Usually there is only one lesion, but multifocal eruptions may occur. Fixed drug eruptions can happen anywhere on the body, but 50% occur on the oral and genital mucosa. The photograph is a classic image of the target-like lesion that develops. Prolonged post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation often follows fixed drug eruption, but there is a non-pigmented variant as well

Incorrect Answers:
A. Any drug can cause a fixed drug eruption, but antifungals are not among the most common
B. Any drug can cause a fixed drug eruption, but Penicillin is not among the most common culprits.
C. Any drug can cause a fixed drug eruption, but Albuterol is not among the most common culprits.
E. Any drug can cause a fixed drug eruption, but Bleomycin is not among the most common

question: A patient with pruritus from the condition displayed below reports decreased nighttime awakenings from pruritus when she takes hydroxyzine. explanation:
First-generation H1 antagonists like chlorpheniramine, hydroxyzine, diphenhydramine, and doxepin cross the blood-brain barrier and are sedating. Of note, doxepin is both a selective serotonin and norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor (SNRI) and an H1 antagonist. Cetirizine, a secondgeneration H1 antagonist, is a metabolite of hydroxyzine and is much less sedating/non-sedating. The second-generation H1 antagonists like cetirizine, loratadine, desloratadine, acrivastine, mizolastine, fexofenadine, and levocetirizine cross the blood- brain barrier very poorly and thus result in less drowsiness. E. Fexofenadine, a second-generation H1 antagonist, is non-sedating.

question: A 23 year old female with erythrodermic psoriasis has failed to improve with conventional medications and is resistant to other treatments. Due to the severity of this condition you suggest acitretin as the next treatment option. What contraceptive period is advised after completing the acitretin course?
explanation: Acitretin is a retinoid and is the main metabolite of etretinate. They are equally effective, but acitretin is cleared more rapidly. Etretinate is 50 times more lipophilic than acitretin. Etretinate has a half-life of 120 days. In the presence of alcohol, acitretin may convert to etretinate. Therefore, the FDA advises a contraceptive period of 3 years to avoid teratogenicity. Etretinate is no longer available in the US.

question: During a recent dental procedure, a patient has an adverse reaction to an amide anesthetic. When performing a skin biopsy, which amide anesthetic below should be avoided?
explanation:
Systemic toxicity primarily affects the cardiovascular and central nervous system and may be fatal. Local anesthetics block voltage-gated sodium channels (VGSC) at the alpha-subunit, preventing sodium influx, subsequent depolarization, and action potential generation. This conduction block interrupts transmission of pain from sensory cells neuronal cells to the cerebral cortex. Toxicity can occur when the sodium channels in the heart or cerebral cortex are affected. Local anesthetics also have effects on sodium and potassium channels, cholinergic or N- methyl-D-aspartate (NMDA) receptors, and cellular metabolic processes, including oxidative phosphorylation, free fatty acid utilization, cyclic adenosine monophosphate production. They vary in toxicity. Cardiotoxicity is directly related to potency and the CC:CNS ratio, which is the is the ratio of the drug dose required to cause cardiac collapse to the drug dose required to produce seizures. Measures to reduce the risk of LAST include basing maximum dose on lean body weight instead of actual body weight; slow, incremental injection, reduction of dose in higher risk patients (pregnancy, extremes of age, reduced clearance due to cardiac, renal, or hepatic disease); avoidance of use in patients under heavy sedation or general anesthesia; and use of ultrasound for guidance. The classic presentation of LAST occurs shortly after injection, with central nervous system excitation, central nervous system (CNS) inhibition, cardiovascular excitation, and sometimes cardiovascular inhibition and arrest. Onset may be delayed, and only cardiovascular effects may occur in some cases. Treatment should occur immediately if LAST is suspected and includes rapid response, maintenance of oxygenation and ventilation, seizure suppression, and cardiovascular support. Lidocaine is the only amide anesthetic listed. Amides have “two i’s” in the spelling of their names. Patients who have allergies to amide anesthetics can be given ester anesthetics such as tetracaine instead. Examples of amides include lidocaine, bupivacaine, and ropivacaine. Examples of ester anesthetics include tetracaine, procaine, benzocaine

Incorrect Answers:
B. Tetracaine is an ester.
C. Benzocaine is an ester.
D. Cocaine is an ester. It has vasoconstrictive properties.
E. Procaine is an ester.

question: A 55-year-old man has pemphigus vulgaris (PV) that has become treatment- resistant. He inquires about the availability of any new treatment options. You explain that a medication, rituximab, is approved for PV. Which of the following is a black box warnings for rituximab?
explanation:

Rituximab is a monoclonal antibody that depletes CD 20+ normal and malignant B cells. It is being used off label to treat conditions such as pemphigus foliaceous, paraneoplastic pemphigus, epidermolysis bullosa acquisita, bullous pemphigoid, mucous membrane pemphigoid, dermatomyositis, cutaneous lupus erythematosus, acute graft-versus-host disease and vasculitis

question: A patient on dapsone recently noted increased fatigue and pallor. Laboratory evaluations reveal a hemolytic anemia. Which of the following vitamins may have helped prevent prevent dapsonerelated hematologic side effects?

Oculo-orogenital syndrome is characterized by angular cheilitis, glossitis, conjunctivitis, and a seborrheic dermatitis-like rash in the groin. question: A 17-year-old patient with severe acne arrives to your office 4 months into isotretinoin therapy. The patient is at a cumulative dose of 100mg/kg and tolerating the medication well. His recent lab work, however, reveals a hypertriglyceridemia of 479mg/dL. Patient is otherwise asymptomatic. What is the best next step?
explanation: Up to 25% of isotretinoin patients can develop a reversible hypertriglyceridemia while on therapy. However, lifestyle modifications (eg, diet, exercise, weight reduction) are initiated before any pharmacologic therapy in the treatment of primary and secondary dyslipidemia, particularly in patients who are asymptomatic. In this case, one important historical item must be determined prior to discussing potential interventions; namely, if the patient was fasting prior to their most recent lab work. Following failure diet and lifestyle modifications, it would still be appropriate to start a fibrate (gemfibrozil, fenofibrate) rather than discontinuing isotretinoin, as the patient is asymptomatic and near the target cumulative dose of 120-150mg/kg.

question: A patient you are treating for pustular psoriasis has been taking cyclosporine for the past three weeks. As part of his follow-up exam, you order several lab tests to monitor for side effects. You notice an increase from his baseline serum creatinine from 0.8 to 1.1 mg/dL. The next best step in his management would be which of the following?

choices.B: Reduce his dose of cyclosporine by 10%
choices.C: Reduce his dose of cyclosporine by 25%
choices.D: Reduce his dose of cyclosporine by 50%

explanation: It exerts its mechanism of action by binding to cyclophilin within the cellular cytoplasm. Despite its proven efficacy as a first-line therapy for several conditions including but not limited to pustular psoriasis, cyclosporine has a significant side effect profile and requires frequent lab testing to screen for end-organ damage. Renal disease is monitored by the frequent evaluation of BUN/ Creatinine levels, magnesium, potassium, and uric acid levels. An elevation in 25% of a patient’s BUN/Cr from baseline should alert physicians for potential renal disease. This patient's creatinine elevated from 0.8 to 1.1, which is over 25%. The first step in approaching this would be to re-check BUN/Cr levels in 2 weeks. If the levels of BUN/Cr decrease to less than 25% of baseline, cyclosporine can be continued at the current dose. Should the elevation persist after the two-week re-check it is then advised to decrease the dose of cyclosporine to 25-50% of the original dose and re-check BUN/Cr in one month. If the elevation is greater than 50% of the baseline BUN/Cr at the initial 2-week re-check, it is advised to decrease the cyclosporine levels to 50% of the original dose and re-check after one month. If after one month, regardless of the dose reduction (25-50% or 50%), no change in the BUN/Cr or improvement warrants cessation of the medication. If this patient, for example, ended up increasing from 0.8 to 1.2, that would be a 50% increase. Anything above 1.2 in this situation warrants holding cyclosporine. If however, the raise was anything >25% BUT less than 50%, the correct answer would be to re-check the level at 2 weeks.

question: A patient you have been evaluating for palmar-plantar pustular psoriasis returns to your clinic frustrated after failing several topical and oral medications. You initiate a discussion with her about initiating cyclosporine. She questions you about common side effects she should look out for. Which of the following is the most common side effect of cyclosporine?
explanation:
It is recommended that patients taking cyclosporine have baseline blood pressure measurements prior to starting the medication, and at every office visit afterward while on the medication

Incorrect Answers:
E. Side effects associated with cyclosporine.

question: Which of the following lab abnormalities would you LEAST expect in a patient taking cyclosporine?

choices.E: All of the above are side effects of cyclosporine treatment

explanation: Hypermagnesemia is not a side effect associated with the treatment of cyclosporine. Low levels of magnesium, hypomagnesemia, are a side effect of cyclosporine. Low levels of this element contribute to and exacerbate additional side effects associated with cyclosporine including paresthesias and hyperesthesia. The remaining choices, hyperkalemia, hyperuricemia, and hyperlipidemia are all potential laboratory abnormalities associated with cyclosporine and should be monitored throughout the treatment regimen.

question: Which of the following medications exerts its anti-inflammatory effect by binding to cyclophilin?
explanation:
Cyclosporine binds to cyclophilin in the cytoplasm of cells and blocks the dephosphorylation of a protein called NFAT inhibiting the production of interleukin-2. Interleukin 2 is a potent proinflammatory stimulant that leads to the recruitment and activation of components of the cellmediated immunity including CD4+ and CD8+ T-cells. Cyclosporine decreases the recruitment of T-cells in the epidermis. Topical tacrolimus and pimecrolimus belong to the family of calcineurin inhibitors

question: Which of the following enzymes is necessary for the conversion of 6- mercaptopurine to 6thioguanine?
explanation:
Hypoxanthine-guanine phosphoribosyltransferase (HGPRT) is the enzyme responsible for converting 6-mercaptopurine (6-MP) to its active metabolite 6-thioguanine (6-TG). This purine analogue is similar in structure to adenine and guanine, and because of this characteristic, is incorporated into DNA and RNA which leads to the inhibition of purine metabolism

Incorrect Answers:
A. Thymidylate synthetase is the enzyme required for the conversion of dUMP to dTMP, a function
B. Dihydrofolate reductase is an enzyme that converts dihydrofolate to tetrahydrofolate.
D. Xanthine oxidase (XO) and thiopurine methyltransferase (TPMT) are enzymes that share the

question: In a patient who has had allergic reactions to hair dyes and henna tattoos, which of the following local anesthetics would be most appropriate to use?
explanation: Most true local allergic reactions to local anesthetics have been reported in the ester anesthetic sub-group. In most cases, this is secondary to cross-reaction with contact sensitizers such as para-aminobenzoic acid (PABA), para-amino salicylic acid, and para- phenylenediamine (PPD). The patient in this question has ad allergic contact dermatitis secondary to contact sensitization to PPD which is found in hair dyes and henna tattoos and should, therefore, avoid ester anesthetics such as those listed in Choices B-E. Prilocaine is an amide anesthetic and would not increase the potential for cross-reactivity in this patient.

question: Each of the following symptoms should be concerning for early signs of lidocaine toxicity EXCEPT?
explanation: In addition to choices A-D, circumoral paresthesias as well as drowsiness are also initial indicators of lidocaine overdose. Symptoms of lidocaine overdose are directly correlated to the serum blood level of lidocaine and progressive CNS and cardiovascular signs and symptoms correspond to specific serum levels. The above choices, except for E, will occur when lidocaine levels exceed 1-6 mcg/ml. Psychosis, along with nausea, vomiting, muscle twitching, blurred vision, tinnitus, and confusion occur with lidocaine levels exceeding 6 mcg/ml. The most severe side effects of lidocaine toxicity, including coma and cardiopulmonary arrest, occur at lidocaine serum levels > 12 mcg/ml. The maximum recommended dose for lidocaine without epinephrine is 5 mg/kg and with epinephrine, it is 7 mg/kg, both are at standard 1-2% concentrations.

question: You receive a phone call from a parent who has questions about a topical anesthetic that was prescribed for their 9 month-old child’s port-wine stain prior to undergoing pulsed dye laser therapy. You review his chart and note that the vascular lesion extends from his right anterior shoulder down to his right hand. A eutectic mixture of 2.5% lidocaine and 2.5% prilocaine hydrochloride was prescribed. What is the maximum recommended area that this topical anesthetic can be applied to in this patient without risking methemoglobinemia?
explanation:
In a child 3-12 months of age, the maximum total dose of eutectic mixture of 2.5% lidocaine and 2.5% prilocaine hydrochloride should not exceed 2 grams or cover an area greater than 20 cm^2. Doses above this amount or covering an area greater than recommended, increase the risk of prilocaine hydrochloride induced methemoglobinemia. It is important to know that these dose and surface areas can be overestimated if there are breaks in the skin, or if a particular patient is susceptible to methemoglobinemia. An easy way to remember this is the maximum area in cm^2 should not be greater than ten times the maximum total dose of cream

Incorrect Answers:
A. In children less than 3 months old, the total dose should not exceed 1 gram or an area greater
D. Children 1-6 years of age should not receive doses greater than 10 grams or exceed an area
E. Children 7-12 years of age should not receive doses greater than 20 grams or exceed an area

question: All of the following are TRUE regarding the mechanism of action of antivirals for the treatment of oral herpes simplex virus except which choice?

choices.A: Conversion to monophosphates by virus-specific thymidine kinase.
choices.B: Conversion to triphosphates by cellular enzymes.
choices.D: Competitive inhibitor of viral DNA-dependent DNA polymerase.
choices.E: Inhibition of viral RNA-dependent DNA polymerase.

correct_text: Conversion to triphosphates by cellular enzymes
explanation: The ability to inhibit RNA-dependent DNA polymerase is only seen in antiretrovirals, often involved in the treatment of HIV. Acyclovir, the primary drug in the treatment of oral lesions of HSV, does not inhibit RNA-dependent DNA polymerase. In the treatment of acute extraoral HSV lesions, topical acyclovir may be used. Additionally, for primary infections, extensive oral infections, or immunosuppressed patients, IV or oral acyclovir may be given. This antiviral's mechanism of action involves conversion to monophosphates by virus-specific thymidine kinase. It is then converted to triphosphates by cellular enzymes. This triphosphate product inhibits DNA synthesis via inhibition of viral DNA-dependent DNA polymerase

question: Which of the following medications can cause white tongue in a patient undergoing hematopoietic stem cell transplantation?
explanation: Palifermin is a keratinocyte growth factor that is used to prevent mucositis caused by cytotoxic therapy in patients undergoing hematopoietic stem cell transplantation. It stimulates epidermal proliferation and differentiation. It causes white tongue which is attributed to protective mucosal thickening.

question: What chemotherapeutic agent can cause yellow-brown callus-like hyperkeratotic plaques?
explanation: Sorafenib is a tyrosine kinase inhibitor that has many side effects including palmoplantar hyperkeratosis, keratoacanthomas, SCCs, angioedema, and Stevens Johnson syndrome. Hyperkeratotic hand–foot skin reaction is a painful complication seen most frequently during the early weeks of use with sorafenib, sunitinib and pazopanib. Hyperkeratotic plaques develop predominantly over sites of pressure or friction.

question: What is an effective treatment for angiofibromas associated with tuberous sclerosis?
explanation:
Rapamycin is an mTOR inhibitor that can help with the appearance of facial angiofibromas. Improvement has been reported with either oral or topical rapamycin. It can also reduce the size of renal angiomyolipomas and improve lung lymphangioleiomyomatosis

Incorrect Answers:
A. Atralin is a brand name for topical all-trans retinoic acid, used to treat acne vulgaris and
C. Bexarotene is used to treat refractory cutaneous T-cell lymphoma.
D. Imatinib is a tyrosine kinase inhibitor used to treat chronic and acute leukemia and
E. Pimecrolimus is a topical calcineurin inhibitor used to treat mild to moderate atopic dermatitis.

question: Which of the following medications causes trichomegaly?
explanation: Trichomegaly (enlargement of the eyelashes) can be seen with many medications. Known associations include prostaglandin analogues (bimatoprost, latanoprost), HIV, EGFR inhibitors, cyclosporine, topiramate, tacrolimus, interferon.

question: A patient presents with the lesion shown below. They work with animal hides in a factory. The lesion started as a painless scrape and has worsened over the last week. Which of the following drugs should be given as post- exposure prophylaxis (PEP) for this disease?
explanation:
Most cases are cutaneous (95%); the remaining cases are inhalational and gastrointestinal. The anthrax ulcer and surrounding edema evolve into a black eschar within 7-10 days, lasting for 7-14 days before separating and leaving a permanent scar. The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) have issued updated guidelines on anthrax post-exposure prophylaxis (PEP) and treatment for nonpregnant and pregnant adults. All individuals exposed to aerosolized B. anthracis spores should receive a full 60 days of antimicrobial drugs, regardless of their vaccination status. Current first-line agents include ciprofloxacin or doxycycline for post-exposure prophylaxis due to increased penicillin resistance

A. Penicillin is no longer recommended due to increased resistance.
C. Raxibacumab, a monoclonal antibody directed at the protective antigen of
D. Rifampin can be part of a multi-drug regimen for inhalational anthrax, but it is not used for postexposure prophylaxis or as a single agent. It is also employed as part of a multi- drug regimen for

question: A 50 year old male patient presents to clinic with extensive psoriasis and joint pain. A decision is made to begin treatment with infliximab. In addition to screening for tuberculosis, prior to beginning therapy with infliximab, which test for latent infection should be performed to avoid viral reactivation?
explanation:
Infliximab (Remicade) is a chimeric IgG monoclonal antibody against TNF-α. Inhibition of TNF can lead to reactivation of hepatitis B, which can result in fulminant hepatic failure. In a retrospective study of more than 250 patients treated with infliximab 40% of hepatitis B carriers developed hepatitis B reactivation, compared to 5% of patients positive for hepatitis C antibody

Incorrect Answers:
C. Screening for hepatitis C is recommended by the AAD prior to starting biologic therapy, however
D. Screening for hepatitis A and HTLV-1 are not currently recommended by the AAD or FDA.
E. There is insufficient evidence of screening for HIV prior to initiation of TNF inhibition therapy.

question: A patient with metastatic melanoma commenced therapy with ipilimumab. Which of the following describes the mechanism of action of ipilimumab?
explanation:
Recently there have been a host of new agents available for treatment of advanced melanoma. Ipilimumab inhibits CTLA4, a negative regulator of T-cell activation that competes with CD28 for binding of B7 on antigen presenting cells. By “cutting the breaks” with an immune checkpoint inhibitor, T-cells are able to detect and destroy cancerous cells

A. Pembrolizumab and nivolumab are anti PD1 antibodies. Programmed cell death 1 is a molecule
C. Vemurafenib and dabrafenib are inhibitors of BRAF, which activates the MAP kinase pathway.
D. Trametinib works downstream of vemurafenib to inhibit MEK activation directly. Combination
E. Imantinib is a tyrosine kinase inhibitor which inhibits c-KIT. 25% of mucosal or acral melanomas

question: A 50 year old patient has severe and chronic urticaria, unresponsive to high doses of H1 antihistamines as well as leukotriene inhibitors. The decision is made to start omalizumab. Which of the following is a potential serious adverse reaction to omalizumab?
explanation:
Omalizumab, a recombinant humanized monoclonal anti-IgE antibody was first approved for asthma but is now FDA approved to treat chronic spontaneous urticaria (CSU) in adults and adolescents 12 years of age and older who remain symptomatic despite H1 antihistamine treatment. being used for chronic urticaria. Omalizumab was studied in three placebo- controlled clinical trials at 12 weeks (CSU Trial 2) and 24 weeks duration (CSU Trials 1 and 3) for chronic spontaneous urticaria. The medication is generally well tolerated but carries a risk of potentially life-threatening anaphylaxis. Anaphylaxis has occurred in less than 1% of patients treated with omalizumab, and no anaphylactic reactions were seen in the clinical trials for urticaria, but the medication contains a black box warning

Incorrect Answers:
A. Renal failure has not been reported to be associated with omalizumab.
B. Worsening congestive heart failure has been associated with TNF inhibitors.
D. Progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy was associated with the psoriasis biologic
E. Reactivation of latent tuberculosis has been seen in patients undergoing therapy with TNF

question: A 25 year old patient with atopic dermatitis reports no improvement with topical steroids. Despite strict adherence he believes his disease is getting worse since starting therapy. Patch testing reveals allergy to tixocortol-21- pivalate and budesonide. Which of the following topical steroids would be an appropriate therapy for this patient?
explanation:
Steroid allergy should be suspected if a patient’s dermatitis does not improve or worsens during therapy with topical steroids. A classification has been created to determine patterns of cross reactivity. Desoximetasone is in group C, of which allergy is very rare

Incorrect Answers:
B. Tixocortol-21-pivalate is the patch test substance for group A steroids, which includes
D. A positive patch test to budesonide characterizes allergy to group B steroids, which includes

question: A 12-year-old African American boy presents with a solitary scaly plaque with broken-off hair on his scalp. A fungal culture is pending. Which of the following is the most appropriate treatment?
explanation:
This patient’s history is typical of tinea capitis, a fungal scalp infection common in children. It is most usually caused by Trichophyton tonsurans or, less commonly, by Microsporum canis. Lesions consist of scaly plaques and pustules, and broken hairs and alopecia are also characteristic. Kerion (boggy pustular plaques) develop in more severe cases. Trichophyton is usually contracted by direct or indirect contact with other infected children, and it typically infects the hair shaft itself (endothrix infection). Infection by Microsporum is by contact with infected animals and occurs around the hair shaft (ectothrix infection). Microscopy of potassium hydroxide preparations taken from lesions may reveal fungal elements, and fungal culture may identify the causative species. Oral griseofulvin is the first-line choice for treatment. Oral terbinafine may also be used, but it has less activity against M. canis. Topical antifungals are ineffective due to the organism’s location below the skin surface and within or around the hair shaft

Incorrect Answers:
A. Terbinafine is less effective than oral griseofulvin. It may be used for tinea pedis, cruris, corporis,
C. Topical ketoconazole is not effective for tinea capitis. It may be used for tinea pedis, cruris, and
D. Oral ketoconazole is not used due to the potential for side effects.

question: A patient presents with a 3-day history of intensely pruritic, raised, erythematous wheals that appear suddenly and resolve within 24 hours without leaving residual skin changes. The lesions migrate to different areas of the trunk and extremities. She denies fever or systemic symptoms, and vital signs are normal. She is diagnosed with acute urticaria and started on first-line therapy. What is the mechanism of action of the most appropriate treatment for this condition?
explanation:
This patient's presentation is consistent with urticaria, more commonly known as hives. Urticarial lesions present as pink, edematous, well-circumscribed papules or pruritic plaques. Lesions can affect any body area but most commonly affect the trunk. Each lesion of urticaria lasts under 24 hours. This short time frame can help distinguish urticaria from other conditions with similarappearing skin lesions. Urticaria is separated into acute and chronic forms. Acute urticaria refers to new-onset or recurring episodes of urticarial lesions less than six weeks in duration. Chronic urticaria refers to recurring bouts of urticaria for over six weeks. There are many causes of urticaria, including medications, infections, pregnancy, food, autoimmune conditions, and ultraviolet radiation. In about half of the cases of acute urticaria, the cause is unknown, which is idiopathic urticaria. Diagnosis is typically clinical, and a biopsy is unnecessary without unusual findings (e.g., if the lesions last over 24 hours) suggesting a different diagnosis. Usually, no routine laboratory testing is needed for acute urticaria or mild antihistamine-responsive urticaria. Recalcitrant urticaria should prompt a further workup with a complete blood count and thyroid function testing, for example. First-line treatment for urticaria includes non-sedating antihistamines such as cetirizine or loratadine daily. A short-acting antihistamine such as hydroxyzine can be used as needed along with the daily antihistamine. A short course of oral steroids can be beneficial for severe cases of urticaria. Acute urticaria resolves within six weeks, and patients with chronic urticaria typically are clear of lesions within six months, although some cases can last for years. If patients with urticaria present with anaphylaxis, immediate medical attention is necessary, including intramuscular or subcutaneous epinephrine and stabilization in a hospital setting. The airway should be secured and vital signs monitored, as airway obstruction or shock can result from anaphylaxis

Incorrect Answers:
B. Patients with viral exanthem or other viral rashes frequently have systemic symptoms of
D. Bacterial skin infections typically produce lesions that are uniformly erythematous, nonmigratory, firm, warm, and tender to touch. Systemic signs, such as fever, may be present. The

question: A 30-year-old man presents for evaluation of axillary hyperhidrosis. He has used aluminum salts previously, which resulted in significant skin irritation. He does not like needles and asks which alternative treatment options are available. Which of the following is the best option?
explanation:
Hyperhidrosis refers to abnormally increased perspiration. It can be divided into primary and secondary forms and localized and generalized subtypes. Primary hyperhidrosis is usually more localized and can be triggered by heat and stress. In contrast, secondary hyperhidrosis is often more generalized. It may be associated with metabolic disorders such as hyperthyroidism, neurological conditions, or medication use. For secondary hyperhidrosis, it is important to identify and treat the underlying cause. Typical areas affected by primary hyperhidrosis include the hands, feet, and axilla. Patients are often embarrassed by their symptoms and desire treatment. For localized hyperhidrosis, first-line treatments include topical aluminum chloride 12%, topical glycopyrrolate 0.5-1% solution, or glycopyrronium cloth wipes. Another treatment option for localized axillary hyperhidrosis is botulinum toxin injections, which prevent acetylcholine from being released by the sympathetic nerve endings of the eccrine glands. Oral medications such as glycopyrrolate 1-2 mg or oxybutynin 5 mg daily to twice daily can relieve symptoms of hyperhidrosis. Patients may experience anticholinergic adverse effects such as urinary retention or a dry mouth, limiting their use. Since this patient is afraid of needles, botulinum toxin is not a good option. He wants to minimize adverse effects, so oral medications with anticholinergic side effects would also not be good choices. Thus, aluminum chloride should be offered to this patient

Incorrect Answers:
B. Silver nitrate is not used for hyperhidrosis. It can be used to manage cutaneous warts.
C. Botulinum toxin therapy is effective in treating hyperhidrosis. It would not be a good option for
D. Oral oxybutynin can effectively treat hyperhidrosis. Topical options are preferred first, as oral

question: A 6-year-old boy has scaly plaques on his scalp, with pustules and an area of alopecia. Which of the following is true regarding the treatment of this condition?

choices.A: Topical antifungals should be used to treat this fungal infection.
choices.B: Topical antibiotics should be used to treat this bacterial infection.
choices.C: Oral antifungals should be used to treat this fungal infection.
choices.D: Oral antibiotics should be used to treat this bacterial infection.
choices.E: Careful washing and a topical steroid are effective treatments

correct_text: Oral antifungals should be used to treat this fungal infection
explanation: This patient’s presentation suggests tinea capitis, which is a fungal infection of the scalp. It affects children more frequently than adults and is more common in patients of African descent than in those of other ethnicities. Tinea capitis is contagious and can be spread through the sharing of towels and combs. Patients typically present with scaly plaques of the scalp, pustules, as well as multiple broken hairs. The plaques may become crusted and inflamed. A more severe presentation of tinea capitis is a kerion, in which patients develop boggy plaques with pustules on the scalp. Tinea capitis and kerions can cause hair loss, so treatment is strongly recommended. On examination, patients may have postauricular lymphadenopathy secondary to the fungal infection. The diagnosis is primarily clinical. A potassium hydroxide preparation can be performed of the scaly plaques and broken hair shafts, which may demonstrate fungal elements. A fungal culture or biopsy can permit speciation of the organism and guide treatment options. The treatment of tinea capitis involves oral antifungal agents rather than topical medications because the fungal elements extend into the hair follicle, which cannot be accessed by topical therapies. First-line treatment is with either oral griseofulvin or oral terbinafine

question: A 30-year-old patient is evaluated for a skin lesion on the lower lip. They noted the onset of pain, tingling, and itching of the lower lip before the lesion appeared. Physical exam reveals a painful vesicle that crosses the vermillion border of the lip. Which of the following is the most appropriate treatment option?
explanation:
This patient has herpes labialis, typically caused by herpes simplex virus, type 1 (HSV-1) infection. Following a latent period in trigeminal nerve ganglia, reactivation of HSV-1 causes "cold sore" formation, involving painful vesicles on the lips that frequently cross the vermillion border. Prodromal symptoms, including pain, pruritis, and tingling, often precede the vesicles. Acyclovir is the treatment of choice for HSV-1 in most patients. Acyclovir is converted to its triphosphate form (acyclovir triphosphate), which mimics guanosine in infected cells. This mimicry leads to viral DNA chain termination and interruption of viral replication

Incorrect Answers:
A. Lamivudine examples of reverse transcriptase inhibitors that are used to treat human
B. Ribavirin is used to treat RSV infections in children and is combined with interferon to treat
C. Oseltamivir, a neuraminidase inhibitor, is used to treat influenza. The influenza virus has a
D. Fluoroquinolone drugs, including ciprofloxacin, inhibit DNA gyrase and topoisomerase enzymes.

question: An 82-year-old patient presents with headaches, blurry vision, dry skin and lips, and hair loss. The patient reports taking multiple vitamins at very high doses to "boost their immune system." Based on their symptoms, which of the following vitamins did they likely take in excess?
explanation:
This patient's headaches, blurry vision, dry skin and lips, and hair loss are consistent with hypervitaminosis A. Vitamin A is fat-soluble and accumulates in the liver and adipose tissue. Symptoms of hypervitaminosis A include those secondary to intracranial swelling (papilledema, headaches, seizures), hypercalcemia, dry mucous membranes, dry/peeling skin, arthralgias, hair loss, and liver damage. Vitamin A toxicity can result from dietary intake; the vitamin is heavily concentrated in animal livers and is present in cheese, milk, butter, egg yolk, green leafy vegetables, mangos, papayas, carrots, and squash. Toxicity can also be due to inappropriate supplementation or over-exposure to isotretinoin. Vitamins A, D, E, and K are fat-soluble and can accumulate to cause subacute (days to weeks) or chronic (weeks to months) toxicity. All fat-soluble vitamins are absorbed via micelles in the small intestines. These vitamins are transported in chylomicrons, which travel through the lymphatic system into the bloodstream. Lipoprotein lipase breaks down the chylomicrons, and the vitamins are released. Some are utilized, whereas others are stored in tissue. Vitamin A is stored in the liver. Because of tissue storage, fat-soluble vitamins are more likely to cause toxicity than water-soluble vitamins

Incorrect Answers:
B. Vitamin D toxicity presents subacutely with hypercalcemia, weight loss, and abdominal pain.
C. Vitamin E toxicity presents chronically with nausea, weakness, headache, fatigue, blurry vision,
D. Vitamin K toxicity can develop acutely as an anaphylactoid reaction if the vitamin is

question: A 56-year-old female presents with a 6-month history of a darkened right great toenail. The nail has become misshapen, discolored, and thickened, with splitting of the nail plate. While there is no pain, she is upset about the appearance and would like treatment. What is the mechanism of action of the first line oral treatment for the condition noted above?

choices.C: Inhibition of aminoacyl-transfer ribonucleic acid synthetase
choices.E: Disruption of synthesis of DNA, RNA and protein

explanation:
Onychomycosis refers to fungal infection of the nails and can be caused by a variety of organisms, including dermatophytes, yeast and mold. Most of the infections of the nail are caused by the dermatophyte Trichophyton rubrum, which causes the condition known as tinea unguium in 60-70% of cases. Candida species is the most common yeast associated with onychomycosis. Fungi produce proteases that degrade keratin, which is thought to facilitate invasion. Nail injury or nail disease reduces barrier protection and may increase the risk of fungal invasion. Diagnostic tests include KOH preparations, histopathologic examination of nail clippings with a PAS stain, fungal culture, and polymerase chain reaction. Treatment includes topical and systemic antifungal medications, laser, surgery, and photodynamic therapy. For mild to moderate dermatophyte related onychomycosis, terbinafine is the first line oral agent. Terbinafine inhibits squalene epoxidase, which is an enzyme required for sterol biosynthesis. The inhibition of the epoxidase causes a deficiency in ergosterol in the cell membrane of the fungus, resulting in cell death

Incorrect Answers:
A. Lanosterol 14-alpha-demethylase is involved in the ergosterol biosynthesis as well, and
B. Itraconazole works by interfering with the cytochrome P450, which reduces the synthesis of
C. Fungal protein synthesis inhibition through blocking of aminoacyl-transfer ribonucleic acid
E. This is the mechanism of action of ciclopirox, which can also be used to treat onychomycosis

question: A 4-month-old female infant is brought to the pediatrician due to a rash on her head. Her parents report the lesion has persisted for the past 2 months, and they worry an infection may have caused it. The patient's birth history is uncomplicated, and she was born vaginally at term. The patient has met her developmental milestones and is gaining weight by breastfeeding. Her temperature is 99° F (37.2° C), blood pressure is 70/45 mm Hg, pulse is 125/min, and respiratory rate is 30/min. The physical exam reveals a healthy young infant with a regular cardiopulmonary exam and a scaly, oily rash on her scalp (Figure 1). Which of the following is the best initial treatment for this patient?
explanation:
Seborrheic dermatitis (SD) is a chronic, inflammatory skin condition that is linked to an overproduction of sebaceous matter and is often exacerbated by Malassezia yeast. While mild cases in adults might respond to emollients and non-medical shampoos, in infants, a more targeted treatment is often required due to the sensitivity of their skin and the risk of complications. Topical antifungals like ketoconazole are often recommended as a first-line treatment for seborrheic dermatitis in infants. Ketoconazole helps in reducing the fungal activity on the skin, thus providing relief from symptoms

Incorrect Answers:
A. Topical triamcinolone is a steroid medication that can be used as a second-line treatment if the
C. While topical selenium sulfide has been used for managing SD, clinical data supporting its use
D. Oral itraconazole is a stronger antifungal medication that may be considered for severe cases
E. While emollients are sometimes used for mild cases, olive oil is not recommended in the

question: A 40-year-old female presents to her primary care physician with erythema and acne over her cheeks and nose. She has had the rash since she was in her early 30s. The outbreaks come and go. They worsen with emotional stresses, heat, and sun exposure. The patient recently lost her job and has low self-esteem due to her rash. She has tried many over-the-counter soaps and lotions without successfully treating her rash. The physical exam reveals telangiectasia on her bilateral eyelids and an erythematous rash with papules over the nose and cheeks, including the nasolabial folds. Which of the following is an appropriate treatment for this condition?
explanation:
Acne rosacea (AR) is a skin condition with vascular (blushing, flushing, and telangiectasia) and inflammatory (papules and pustules) rashes on the cheeks, nose, forehead, and around the eyes. Triggers of AR include hot foods and drinks, alcohol, spicy foods, sun, and heat. Conservative management consists of avoiding triggers and the use of sunscreen. Medical management includes topical metronidazole and topical sulfacetamide. For chronic or persistent flushing, isotretinoin and alpha-2 agonist brimonidine can help. For recalcitrant cases, laser resurfacing is an option. For resistant or ocular symptoms, systemic antibiotics like tetracycline are indicated

Incorrect Answers:
A. UV light can treat other skin conditions like psoriasis. It is not typically used in AR.
B. Oral tetracycline, not topical, is a treatment for AR.
D. Topical steroids should not be a treatment for skin conditions of the face, as steroids can thin
E. Oral terbinafine is the treatment for tinea fungal infections.
